summary: Albania used Ottoman stamps from about 1870 until independence, and issued its own first stamps in June 1913 — Turkish stamps handstamped with the double-headed eagle and SHQIPENIA, in six major overprint types. The next thirty years are a dense sequence of short-lived authorities: Prince Wied 1914, wartime Austro-Hungarian and Italian and French occupation zones, the Republic and then Zog's Kingdom, Italian occupation 1939-43 and German 1943-44. Postwar issuing runs continuously under the People's Republic and, from 1991, the Republic of Albania.

periods.label: Ottoman Empire
periods.authority: Ottoman Post
periods.note: No Albanian issues; Turkish stamps used. Also Austrian and Italian post offices in the Levant operated in Albanian ports.

periods.label: Provisional independent Albania
periods.authority: Provisional Government of Ismail Qemali, Vlora
periods.note: June 1913 handstamped overprints on Turkish stamps; first pictorial definitives late 1913.

periods.label: Principality of Albania
periods.authority: Prince Wilhelm zu Wied
periods.note: Very short-lived; overprints and the Essad Pasha material date from this chaotic period.

periods.label: Wartime occupations
periods.authority: Austro-Hungarian military post (Shkodra and north), Italian occupation (Vlora and south), French occupation (Korça)
periods.note: Korça issued its own 'Republika Shqiptare e Korçës' locals 1917-18. Each occupation is a separate catalogue listing.

periods.label: Republic of Albania
periods.authority: Albanian Post, Republic
periods.note: Includes the 1925 Republika Shqiptare overprints.

periods.label: Kingdom of Albania (Zog)
periods.authority: Mbretnija Shqiptare, Post of King Zog I

periods.label: Italian occupation
periods.authority: Italian administration under Victor Emmanuel III as King of Albania
periods.note: Stamps inscribed MBRETNIJA SHQIPTARE; SASENO and 12 IV 1939 overprints.

periods.label: German occupation
periods.authority: German military administration, nominally independent Kingdom
periods.note: Overprints '14 SHTATOR 1943' and issues inscribed SHQIPNIJA.

periods.label: Democratic Government
periods.authority: Qeveria Demokratike e Shqipnis

periods.label: People's Republic / People's Socialist Republic
periods.authority: Posta Shqiptare, RP e Shqipërisë then RPS e Shqipërisë (from 1976)

periods.label: Republic of Albania
periods.authority: Posta Shqiptare sh.a.

predecessors: Austrian Post Offices in the Turkish Empire
inscriptions: Korçë / Republika Shqiptare e Korçës (French-zone locals)
gaps: Effectively no issues 1914-1920 from an Albanian national authority — the country had no functioning central postal administration and mail moved under Austro-Hungarian, Italian, French and Greek military posts, each catalogued separately. Shorter interruption 1944-45 between the German withdrawal and the first Democratic Government issue. Some sources also treat 1915-16 as wholly blank for Albanian-authority issues.
forgery_risk: Among the highest in European philately. The 1913 handstamped overprints exist doubled and inverted and excellent forgeries are known for essentially every one of them; the handstamps also exist in red, blue and violet that were never officially issued. The 1914 period is a documented minefield of fakes, forgeries and outright fantasy issues, including material attributed to Essad Pasha. The Korça locals, the 1919-20 occupation overprints and the German 1943 '14 SHTATOR 1943' overprints are all heavily faked. Anything scarce from 1913-1920 should be treated as suspect without expertisation.
wallpaper_risk: Not agency-driven and not on UPU illegal-issue circulars. However Albania has a genuine CTO reputation: from the 1950s through the 1980s the state postal administration produced large thematic sets, imperforate variants and souvenir sheets sold cancelled-to-order into the Western packet trade, so much of the People's Republic output survives mainly in CTO form and mint-never-hinged postally-used material is disproportionately scarce. Worth stating plainly to collectors, but it is a CTO problem, not an IGPC/Stamperija one.
count_source: Michel Südosteuropa (Albanien) major numbers, 1913 to the mid-2020s, excluding the separately listed occupation issues. Stampworld's Albania 1913-2025 listing is broadly consistent.
uncertain: The precise Michel total was not verified against a current edition and should be confirmed; Michel, Scott and Gibbons differ notably on Albania because they disagree about which 1913-20 provisional and occupation items merit major numbers at all. The batch file records 4,239 Albania records, which exceeds any published major-number count and likely includes varieties, imperforates, souvenir sheets and occupation listings. Traps: the Ottoman era to 1912 is under Turkey; Austrian and Italian Levant post offices in Albanian ports are under those countries; the Austro-Hungarian, Italian, French/Korça and German occupation issues are separate catalogue sections; and the Italian 1939-43 issues are listed by some catalogues under Italian Colonies rather than Albania. Myth to flag: the June 1913 handstamps are often presented as a single official issue — they are six distinct overprint types applied at different offices, and the coloured handstamps circulating as 'rare varieties' were never officially issued.

summary: Algeria's own stamps begin 8 May 1924 with 32 French stamps overprinted ALGERIE; the first stamps actually inscribed for Algeria are the 1926 pictorial definitives. From 1849 to 1924 ordinary French stamps were used, and again from 1958 to 1962 when Algeria was administered as part of metropolitan France — both periods are catalogued under France, not Algeria, which silently hides ~75 years from a country-keyed search. Independent Algeria has issued continuously since 1962, initially via overprints on French stamps, then under Arabic-inscribed issues of the PTT. Output is modest and conservative by world standards.

periods.label: French stamps used, no distinct issues
periods.authority: Postes de France (French colonial administration)
periods.note: Identified only by cancellation; catalogued under France

periods.label: French Algeria overprints
periods.authority: French administration, Algeria
periods.note: French stamps overprinted ALGERIE

periods.label: French Algeria, own designs
periods.authority: French administration, Algeria
periods.note: Inscribed ALGERIE / ALGERIE POSTES

periods.label: Integration into France
periods.authority: Postes de France
periods.note: French stamps used again; no separate Algerian issues

periods.label: Independent Algeria
periods.authority: Algerie Poste / Ministere des PTT, Republique Algerienne Democratique et Populaire
periods.note: 1962 begins with EA overprints on French stamps

gaps: 1849-1924 (French stamps used, no Algerian issues); July 1958 - 1962 (Algeria administered as part of France, French stamps used). Both are administrative gaps rather than postal collapse.
forgery_risk: The 1962 'EA' (Etat Algerien) overprints on French stamps are the main danger area: many were applied locally or privately, numerous unauthorised and bogus varieties exist, and genuine locally-applied overprints are hard to separate from fakes. The 1924-26 ALGERIE overprints also attract forged and faked varieties.
wallpaper_risk: No. Algeria is not an agency-driven issuer, has no IGPC/Stamperija association, and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Its programme is restrained.
count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (estimate combining the French Algeria and independent Algeria listings, all sections)
uncertain: The total count is an estimate, not a counted figure; catalogues differ by roughly 20%. A widespread but imprecise claim is that 'Algeria's first stamps were 1924' — true for issues bearing Algeria's name, but the first stamps used in the territory were French from 1849. The trap: France 1849-1924 and 1958-62 usage is catalogued under France. NOTE: this batch file (batch_08.txt) lists Iraq, Turkey, Algeria and Poland, while the task message named Algeria, Belgium, South Africa and Colombia; both sets are covered here.

summary: The first postage stamps on Argentine soil were not national: the province of Corrientes issued a crude hand-engraved 1-real stamp in 1856, copied by Matias Pipet from the French Ceres design, followed by the State of Buenos Aires in 1858 and the province of Cordoba in 1858/59. The first stamps of Argentina as a nation are the 1858 lithographed arms of the Argentine Confederation (Scott 1-4), superseded in 1862 by the seal of the reunified Argentine Republic (Scott 5-7). These separate provincial issues reflect a genuinely fragmented polity, and general catalogues list Corrientes, Buenos Aires and Cordoba as distinct countries - a serious trap for anyone searching under 'Argentina'. Issuing has been continuous under the Republic since 1862, through every change of regime.

periods.label: Provincial issue - Corrientes
periods.authority: Province of Corrientes
periods.note: Hand-engraved individual dies, each noticeably different; 1 real, pen-revalued to 3 centavos in 1860. Catalogued separately from Argentina.

periods.label: State of Buenos Aires
periods.authority: Independent State of Buenos Aires (seceded from the Confederation)
periods.note: The famous 'Barquitos' (little ships) issue. Catalogued separately.

periods.label: Provincial issue - Cordoba
periods.authority: Province of Cordoba
periods.note: Only two values (5c, 10c). Catalogued separately.

periods.label: Argentine Confederation
periods.authority: Confederacion Argentina (Parana government)
periods.note: First national issue; lithographed arms. Superseded on reunification.

periods.label: Argentine Republic
periods.authority: Republica Argentina national postal administration; latterly Correo Argentino / Correo Oficial de la Republica Argentina
periods.note: Continuous through all subsequent constitutional, Peronist and military-government periods; no break in issuing.

inscriptions: REPUBLICA ARGENTINA (from 1862; accented REPUBLICA on some early printings)
Ministerial overprints on officials: M.I., M.R.C., M.H., M.G., M.J.I., M.M., M.O.P., M.A.
ISLAS MALVINAS (on Argentine claim issues - not a separate issuing entity)
gaps: No gaps at national level since 1862. The provincial issuers each stopped when superseded: Cordoba 1862, Buenos Aires 1862, Corrientes 1880 on absorption into the national postal system.
forgery_risk: High for the classic period. The Buenos Aires 'Barquitos' are among the most heavily forged classics of South America, with both wholesale forgeries and reprints in circulation. The Corrientes hand-engraved types are readily faked precisely because each genuine die legitimately differs, which removes the usual plating check; the 1860 pen revaluations are also faked. The 1858 Confederation lithographs exist in numerous forgeries. Separately, the ministerial official overprints (M.I., M.R.C. etc.) are simple letter overprints on cheap common stamps and are forged in quantity - a classic low-effort, high-margin target. Expertisation is advisable for anything pre-1870 and for scarce officials.
wallpaper_risk: No. Argentina is a conservative, restrained issuer with a modest annual programme, no agency involvement (no IGPC or Stamperija connection), and no appearance on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Its issues are genuine postal issues throughout.
count_source: StampWorld listing count for Argentina (major numbers, postage plus officials and back-of-book, to 2025). Scott's listing is broadly comparable. Provincial issues (Corrientes, Buenos Aires, Cordoba) are counted separately and excluded. Approximate.
uncertain: CATALOGUE TRAP - the single most important point for Argentina: the first stamps on Argentine territory (Corrientes 1856, Buenos Aires 1858, Cordoba 1858/59) are catalogued by Scott, SG and StampWorld under those names as separate countries, not under Argentina, so a country-keyed search on 'Argentina' silently hides 1856-1862 entirely and makes Argentina look like an 1858 or 1862 first-issuer. Which year counts as 'first' depends on convention: 1856 for first stamps on the territory, 1858 for the first national (Confederation) issue, 1862 for the first issue of the Republic proper. I recorded 1856 per the brief's rule and flag the alternatives here. I could not verify a precise total from a named printed catalogue edition; the figure is a StampWorld-derived approximation and general catalogues differ by 20 percent or more, chiefly over officials, ministerials and revenue-adjacent material. The exact 1858 vs 1859 start date for Cordoba is reported inconsistently across sources and I have not resolved it.

count_source: Estimate combining Scott Classic Specialized 1840-1940 for the 1919-23 'Dashnak' and Soviet-Armenian issues (roughly 350-400 major numbers) with Scott/Michel listings for the Republic from 1992; not verified against a single current edition
summary: Armenia is one of the most treacherous areas in world philately. Its first stamps were 1919 overprints on Russian imperial stamps — the so-called 'Z' or, more correctly, 'HP' monogram overprints (Armenian letters H and P for Haykakan Post, Armenian Post), authorised by the Dashnak government in November 1919. The pictorial designs by Arshak Fetvadjian, printed in Paris in 1920, were never delivered to Armenia and have no genuine postal use; they were sold to dealers in Paris around 1924. Sovietisation in 1920-21 produced further overprints, after which Armenia was absorbed into the Transcaucasian Federation (1923) and then the USSR, with no Armenian stamps from 1923 to 1992. Independent issuing resumed in 1992. Specialist literature states that the great majority of 1919-21 overprints on the market are forgeries.

periods.label: First Republic of Armenia (Dashnak)
periods.authority: Republic of Armenia postal administration (Dashnaktsutyun government)
periods.note: HP/'Z' monogram overprints on Russian imperial stamps from late 1919. The 1920 Fetvadjian Paris pictorials were never issued for postal use.

periods.label: Soviet Armenia / Armenian SSR
periods.authority: Armenian Soviet Socialist Republic
periods.note: Surcharges and pictorial issues 1921-22; unissued 1922 sets exist.

periods.label: Transcaucasian Federation
periods.authority: Transcaucasian Socialist Federative Soviet Republic (TSFSR)
periods.note: Armenian issues cease; TSFSR issues catalogued separately.

periods.label: USSR period
periods.authority: Soviet Union (Ministry of Posts and Telegraphs, USSR)
periods.note: No Armenian stamps. Soviet stamps inscribed CCCP used throughout.

periods.label: Republic of Armenia (second independence)
periods.authority: HayPost / Armenian postal administration
periods.note: Continuous since 1992.

periods.label: Nagorno-Karabakh / Artsakh
periods.authority: Self-declared Nagorno-Karabakh Republic / Republic of Artsakh
periods.note: Not UPU-recognised; catalogued separately or as local/unofficial by most catalogues. Ceased with the entity's dissolution.

inscriptions: HP monogram (Armenian letters Հ and Փ, Haykakan Post) — the so-called 'Z' overprint
ARMENIA (Roman, on some 1920 Paris pictorials and modern issues)
ARTSAKH / ԱՐՑԱԽ and NAGORNO KARABAKH (Karabakh issues, separate entity)
CCCP (Soviet stamps used 1923-91 — not Armenian issues)
gaps: A single very large gap: 1923 to 1991, when Armenia had no stamps of its own and Soviet issues inscribed CCCP were used. There are also effectively no genuine postal issues in 1920 proper (the Paris pictorials never arrived), so the useful issuing record is 1919-1923 and 1992-present.
forgery_risk: Extreme — among the worst in world philately. Specialist sources state that a large majority (figures around 80% are cited in the literature) of 1919-21 Armenian overprints on Russian stamps in the market and in collections are forgeries. The HP/'Z' monogram overprints, the 1921-22 Soviet Armenian surcharges, and the framed and unframed handstamps are all forged in quantity, including contemporary forgeries made for the dealer trade. The 1920 Fetvadjian Paris pictorials exist genuinely but are widely forged and, critically, no genuine postal use exists — any 'used' example or cover should be treated as fabricated. Serebrakian is a name associated with large-scale supply of Armenian material to the trade. Forged and fantasy cancellations are pervasive. Certificates are essential for anything pre-1923.
wallpaper_risk: Modern Armenia (1992-) has a modest and broadly legitimate programme and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars; it is NOT an agency wallpaper country. However Nagorno-Karabakh/Artsakh issues are not UPU-recognised and are treated as local or unofficial by major catalogues, and a substantial body of outright bogus and fantasy 'Armenia' labels circulates from the 1919-23 era.
uncertain: BATCH LIST CONFLICT: this country appears in the batch file but not in the task message's list. See the note on Switzerland. MYTH TO FLAG: the 1920 Fetvadjian pictorials are routinely catalogued and sold as 'the first stamps of independent Armenia' and are frequently offered used or on cover — they were never delivered to Armenia and have no genuine postal use; used examples and covers are philatelic fabrications. A second common error is calling the monogram overprint a 'Z' — it is the Armenian HP (Հ-Փ) monogram, and the 'Z' name is a misreading that persists in older catalogues and dealer listings. CATALOGUE TRAP: the 1923-91 era is catalogued under Russia/USSR and the 1923 issues under Transcaucasian Federation, so a country-keyed search on 'Armenia' hides 68 years; earlier Transcaucasian material may also sit under Russian Post Offices in the Turkish Empire or under Russia. My total-stamp figure is a weak estimate and I could not verify it against a current catalogue edition; treat it as indicative only. Records held (2358) is far above my estimate, which likely means the held records include forgery types, varieties, Karabakh, or Soviet-era material mis-keyed to Armenia — worth auditing.

count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (Australia, Commonwealth issues 1913 onward); Australian Commonwealth Specialists' Catalogue (ACSC) for detailed classic listings; Stanley Gibbons Australia gives a broadly similar major-number total
summary: The territory's first stamps are the 1850 issues of New South Wales and Victoria, not the 1913 Commonwealth issue — a distinction that matters because all colonial-era material is catalogued under the six individual colonies/states, not under Australia. The Commonwealth's own first stamps are the Kangaroo and Map series of 2 January 1913, though the states continued issuing for about a decade after Federation in 1901. Australia has issued continuously since 1913 with no gaps, and its output has risen sharply since the 1990s.

periods.label: Colonial period
periods.authority: Six separate self-governing colonial postal administrations: New South Wales (1850), Victoria (1850), Tasmania (1853, initially as Van Diemen's Land), Western Australia (1854), South Australia (1855), Queensland (1860)
periods.note: Each catalogued as its own country. States continued issuing after Federation in 1901; the last state issues run to about 1912-13.

periods.label: Commonwealth of Australia
periods.authority: Postmaster-General's Department
periods.note: Begins with the Kangaroo and Map definitives, 2 January 1913. Postage Due and 1902 Commonwealth-wide postage labels precede the definitives.

periods.label: Australia Post era
periods.authority: Australian Postal Commission / Australia Post
periods.note: PMG's Department split into Australia Post and Telecom in 1975.

inscriptions: COMMONWEALTH OF AUSTRALIA (on some early issues and postage dues)
POSTAGE (on Kangaroo and Map series, with AUSTRALIA in the design)
VAN DIEMEN'S LAND, later TASMANIA (predecessor colony)
gaps: None. Continuous issuing from 1913 to the present, uninterrupted by either world war. The only 'gap' is definitional: 1901-1913, when a federated Australia existed but the constituent states were still issuing their own stamps rather than a national issue.
forgery_risk: Modest by world standards but real in specific areas. The Kangaroo and Map high values (5s, 10s, £1, £2) are faked, and more commonly repaired, reperforated or regummed. The classic colonial issues carry their own risks — the New South Wales 'Sydney Views' (1850) are widely forged, as are the Western Australia 4d inverted-swan error and the Victoria 'Half-Length' issues. Forged and altered cancellations are a persistent problem on colonial material. Modern Australia has a separate and growing problem with counterfeit current definitives produced for postal fraud rather than for collectors.
wallpaper_risk: Australia does NOT use a philatelic agency such as IGPC or Stamperija and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars — all issues are legitimate and postally valid. That said, Australia Post has a well-earned reputation among collectors for high-volume modern output: large numbers of annual issues, plus extensive gutter strips, booklets, self-adhesive coils, personalised stamps and repeated design variants. Many collectors regard the modern era as excessive even though it is not agency-driven.
uncertain: The total is an estimate and is highly sensitive to counting rules — whether self-adhesive and sheet versions of the same design, personalised stamps, and booklet variants are counted as separate major numbers varies enormously between Scott, SG and ACSC, easily by more than the stated 20% margin. Exact Van Diemen's Land / Tasmania first-issue dating varies slightly between sources.

count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (Austria); Michel Österreich-Spezial is the specialist standard and is more granular
summary: Austria's first stamps appeared on 1 June 1850 — a coat-of-arms design inscribed K.K. POST-STEMPEL, issued by the Austrian Empire for a territory far larger than modern Austria. The single most important cataloguing trap is that Austria's early issues cover the whole Habsburg realm, and parallel Lombardy-Venetia issues (identical designs, centesimi values) are catalogued as a separate country. Austria's only true gap is 1938-1945, when the Anschluss abolished Austrian stamps in favour of German ones. Issuing resumed in 1945 under both Soviet and Allied auspices before the Second Republic consolidated it.

periods.label: Austrian Empire
periods.authority: K.K. Post (Imperial-Royal Post of the Austrian Empire)
periods.note: First issue 1 June 1850. Covers the whole empire. Parallel Lombardy-Venetia issues in centesimi are catalogued separately.

periods.label: Austria-Hungary (Cisleithania)
periods.authority: K.K. Österreichische Post — the Austrian half of the Dual Monarchy
periods.note: Hungary issued its own stamps from 1871 and is catalogued separately. Austrian offices in the Levant/Turkey and in Crete are separate catalogue headings, as are Bosnia and Herzegovina and the WWI Austrian occupation issues for Italy, Serbia, Montenegro and Romania.

periods.label: First Republic (Deutschösterreich then Österreich)
periods.authority: Republic of German-Austria, then Republic of Austria
periods.note: Early republic issues overprinted or inscribed DEUTSCHÖSTERREICH.

periods.label: Federal State (Austrofascist)
periods.authority: Bundesstaat Österreich under Dollfuss and Schuschnigg
periods.note: Continues to be catalogued under Austria.

periods.label: Anschluss — German annexation
periods.authority: Deutsche Reichspost
periods.note: Austrian stamps abolished; German stamps used. Transition took several months in 1938, with a period where both were required. This material is catalogued under Germany.

periods.label: Post-war occupation
periods.authority: Allied Military Government (posthorn series, 28 June 1945, printed by the Bureau of Engraving and Printing, Washington DC) for the western zones; Soviet-zone Vienna issues concurrently
periods.note: AMG posthorn stamps valid into 1947 in Upper Austria, Salzburg, Tyrol, Vorarlberg, Styria and Carinthia.

periods.label: Second Republic
periods.authority: Österreichische Post (Post und Telegraphenverwaltung, later Österreichische Post AG)
periods.note: Fully sovereign issuing from the 1955 State Treaty onward.

predecessors: Austrian Empire (as its own predecessor entity)
inscriptions: DEUTSCHES REICH / GROSSDEUTSCHES REICH (1938-1945, catalogued under Germany)
gaps: 1938 to 1945 — no Austrian stamps at all. The Anschluss of March 1938 ended Austrian issues; German Reich stamps served the territory (with a transitional period during 1938 in which both were required). Issuing resumed in 1945. Note that this era is not absent from philately, it is simply catalogued under Germany, which silently hides seven years from an Austria-keyed search.
forgery_risk: The 1850 first issue exists in official reprints (Neudrucke) from several dates, which are common and frequently sold as originals — distinguishing original printings from reprints by paper, gum and plating is the central skill for this issue. The 1850-54 issues are also forged outright, and forged or transferred cancellations are widespread. The 1945 local and provisional issues, and the Soviet-zone overprints, attract fakes. The various WWI occupation overprints for Italy, Serbia, Montenegro and Romania are heavily faked. The 1920 Kärnten (Carinthia) plebiscite overprints are another known problem area. The 1946 Renner and 1933 WIPA issues are among the most-faked modern Austrian items.
wallpaper_risk: Austria does NOT have an agency-driven or excessive-issue reputation. It has never used IGPC, Stamperija or a similar agency, and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Modern Austrian output is moderate, conservative and genuinely postal, with a strong reputation for printing quality. Austria has issued some novelty formats (embroidered, ceramic, meteorite stamps) which some collectors dislike, but these are legitimate national issues, not agency wallpaper.
uncertain: The total is an estimate and has not been checked against a physical catalogue; Michel Österreich-Spezial counts substantially more than Scott because of its finer treatment of types, papers and perforations. The precise end-date of Austrian stamp validity in 1938 varies by region and source — the transition was staggered over several months, so any single date should be treated with caution. Whether the 1945 Soviet-zone Vienna issues and the AMG posthorn series should be listed under Austria or as separate occupation entities differs between catalogues.

summary: The Azerbaijan Democratic Republic issued its first stamps on 20 October 1919 - ten imperforate lithographed values in roubles showing a soldier with the national flag, and a farmer with Baku. Soviet issues follow from 1921 as the Azerbaijan SSR, then from 1923 the territory is absorbed into the Transcaucasian Federation and thereafter the USSR, giving a 68-year gap. Independent Azerbaijan resumed on 26 March 1992 without overprinting Soviet stamps. The area carries an unusually heavy bogus-issue burden at both ends of its history.

periods.label: Russian Empire postal use
periods.authority: Imperial Russian Post
periods.note: Russian stamps used; catalogued under Russia.

periods.label: Azerbaijan Democratic Republic
periods.authority: ADR government, Baku
periods.note: First issue 20 Oct 1919; two printings (1919 white paper/whitish gum, 1920 buff paper). Surcharged in 1921-22.

periods.label: Azerbaijan SSR
periods.authority: Soviet Socialist Republic of Azerbaijan
periods.note: 1921 set of 15 (oil derrick, mosque and other local subjects); 1922-23 surcharges and 'Soviet star' overprints.

periods.label: Transcaucasian Federation / USSR
periods.authority: Transcaucasian SFSR (1923); thereafter USSR Ministry of Communications
periods.note: No Azerbaijani stamps; TSFSR issues are catalogued under Transcaucasia.

periods.label: Republic of Azerbaijan
periods.authority: Azermarka / Azerpost
periods.note: First issue 26 Mar 1992; no Soviet overprints were used.

inscriptions: АZӘRBAYCAN / AZERBAIDJAN (1919, Latin and Arabic script alongside Cyrillic on some values)
Arabic-script Azerbaijani country name on the 1919-20 issues
AZƏRBAYCAN (modern Latin Azerbaijani, from 1992 - note the schwa Ə)
Script note: three-way script history - Arabic to 1929, Latin 1929-39, Cyrillic 1939-91, Latin again from 1991. Modern issues use the Latin orthography with Ə, Ğ, Ş.
gaps: 1923-1991: no Azerbaijani issues, superseded by Transcaucasian and then USSR stamps. Short gap 1920-21 during the Soviet takeover.
forgery_risk: High relative to the size of the area. The scarcer 1919 first printing is forged, as are the 1921-23 Soviet-period surcharges and star overprints. The 1923 'famine relief' stamps of Azerbaijan are bogus in the first place - and have themselves subsequently been forged, an unusual double trap. Assorted fantasy overprints on ADR stamps circulate.
wallpaper_risk: Yes, though not through the state administration. In 1998 some 19 sets of illegal stamps were put out in the name of Nakhchivan - thematic subjects including Diana, Princess of Wales and the Spice Girls - and the Azerbaijani government notified the UPU that these were counterfeit. Azerpost's own official programme is modest and conventional; the risk lies in Nakhchivan-attributed material and post-1992 unauthorised thematics.
count_source: Approximate: Scott major numbers for 1919-23 (roughly 80) plus 1992 to date. Not verified against a current catalogue edition; Michel and Stanley Gibbons differ.
uncertain: The total is a rough estimate only and should be verified before publication. Also unverified: whether any Nakhchivan-inscribed material has ever been legitimately postally valid - the 1998 sets are clearly illegal, but the status of a small number of earlier locally-produced items is unclear. Cataloguing trap: 1923 Transcaucasian Federation issues covering Azerbaijan are listed under 'Transcaucasian Federated Republics', and everything pre-1919 under Russia.

count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (approximate major numbers through the early 2020s)
summary: Bangladesh's first stamps are the set of eight designed by Biman Mullick and printed by Format International Security Printers in England, released 29 July 1971 by the Mujibnagar government-in-exile during the Liberation War — issued largely as a claim to international recognition and given postal validity when post offices reopened on 20 December 1971. Earlier material from the territory is catalogued under India (East Bengal) and then Pakistan. A large and messy provisional period of locally produced BANGLADESH overprints on Pakistan stamps ran from 20 December 1971 to 30 April 1973.

periods.label: British Indian usage (East Bengal)
periods.authority: Government of India Post Office
periods.note: Also Bengal-region usage of the 1854 India issues; catalogued under India.

periods.label: East Pakistan
periods.authority: Pakistan Post Office Department
periods.note: Pakistan stamps only; no separate East Pakistan issues.

periods.label: Liberation War / government-in-exile
periods.authority: Provisional Government of Bangladesh (Mujibnagar)
periods.note: Eight stamps, 29 July 1971, printed in England.

periods.label: Provisional overprints
periods.authority: Bangladesh post offices and local authorities
periods.note: BANGLADESH handstamps on Pakistan stamps, each office devising its own; style and ink colour unspecified. Ended 30 April 1973 with the first Bangladesh definitives.

periods.label: People's Republic of Bangladesh
periods.authority: Bangladesh Post Office
periods.note: Continuous national issuing.

inscriptions: Provisionals: BANGLADESH overprinted on stamps inscribed PAKISTAN
gaps: None since 1971. The territory has no issues of its own before 1971 — that material is under India and Pakistan.
forgery_risk: Very high for the 1971-73 provisional overprints, which are among the most heavily faked modern material. Because overprints were locally improvised there is no single genuine standard; fakes include impossible dates (before 20 Dec 1971 or after 30 Apr 1973), unrecorded ink colours (red was rare; green, mid-blue and brownish-black were never used), and handstamps recut from illustrations in Tornow's handbook. Identical strikes on covers from widely separated towns are a standard warning sign. The 1971 Mullick set also exists in forged and bogus 'overprinted' variants.
wallpaper_risk: No. Bangladesh Post runs a modest, self-administered programme, with no IGPC or Stamperija agency involvement and no listing on UPU illegal-issue circulars.
uncertain: Total count is approximate and highly sensitive to whether the 1971-73 provisionals are catalogued (Scott and Gibbons largely decline to give them major numbers, while specialist handbooks list thousands of varieties). The common statement that the 29 July 1971 stamps were 'issued in Bangladesh' is misleading — they were printed and first sold abroad, mainly in London, and only became postally valid in country on 20 December 1971.

summary: Belarus as a catalogued stamp issuer begins in 1992; Michel and Scott both open the country at that point. Before then the territory used Russian Empire stamps (from 1858), then Soviet issues inscribed CCCP for seventy years. The 1918 material attributed to the Belarusian People's Republic and the 1920 'Special Belarusian Detachment' items are not accepted as regular postal issues, and the German and Polish 1918 issues used on Belarusian soil are catalogued under Germany and Poland respectively. Modern output is modest and genuinely postal.

periods.label: Russian Empire
periods.authority: Imperial Russian Post
periods.note: No Belarus-specific issues; Russian Empire stamps used throughout.

periods.label: German military administration (Ober Ost / 10th Army)
periods.authority: German 10th Army civil post
periods.note: Control-stamp issues in German currency, listed by Michel under Germany, not Belarus.

periods.label: Polish I Corps
periods.authority: Polish I Corps in Russia
periods.note: Two sets of overprinted Russian stamps; listed by Michel, Gibbons and Yvert under Poland.

periods.label: Belarusian People's Republic (BNR)
periods.authority: BNR, claimed
periods.note: Existence as postally used stamps is disputed; widely treated as labels or later productions.

periods.label: Byelorussian SSR / Soviet Union
periods.authority: Narkompochtel, then Ministry of Communications of the USSR
periods.note: All stamps inscribed CCCP; no republic-level issues. Includes German occupation 1941-44, whose Ostland overprints are catalogued under German occupation issues.

periods.label: Republic of Belarus
periods.authority: Belpochta / Ministry of Communications and Informatisation
periods.note: First issue 20 March 1992 (Cross of St Euphrosyne of Polatsk).

inscriptions: Беларусь / Belarus (bilingual Cyrillic-Latin, common on modern issues)
gaps: No issues of Belarus's own from 1919 to 1991 — seventy-two years covered entirely by Soviet issues inscribed CCCP. This is the single largest hidden era for a country-keyed search. There is no gap in the modern series since 1992.
forgery_risk: Concentrated entirely in the 1918-1920 period. The 1920 issues attributed to the Special Belarusian Detachment / Bulak-Balachowicz are now generally regarded as bogus. The 1918 BNR items are best treated as unverified until certificated. Genuine 1992-94 provisional overprints on Soviet stamps and the early inflation-period surcharges are also commonly faked, as are the scarce local provisional handstamps of 1992. Modern Belarusian issues carry essentially no forgery risk.
wallpaper_risk: No. Belarus is not agency-driven, has no IGPC or Stamperija involvement, and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Annual output is modest — well under 100 stamps a year — and issues are genuinely available at post office counters.
count_source: Stampworld, Belarus 1992-2025, roughly 1,642 major numbers. The Belarusian Ministry's own 2020 statement gave about 1,380 postage stamps to that date, which is consistent. Michel Osteuropa (Weißrussland) gives a broadly similar figure.
uncertain: The batch file records 4,261 Belarus records, which is roughly 2.6 times any published catalogue count of Belarusian major numbers — that figure almost certainly includes minor varieties, souvenir sheets, booklets, postal stationery, or Soviet-era material misattributed to Belarus, and should be audited before it is treated as a stamp count. Whether the 1918 BNR items were ever postally used could not be established from available sources and is genuinely contested in the literature; I have not assigned them a first_issue_year. Traps: everything 1919-1991 is under Soviet Union, the 1918 German civil post is under Germany, the Polish I Corps issues are under Poland, and the 1941-44 occupation is under German occupation issues (Ostland) — four separate catalogue countries hide the whole pre-1992 record. Myth to flag: Belarus is sometimes said to have begun issuing in 1918 as an independent state; the honest catalogue answer is 1992.

summary: Belgium issued its first stamps, the 'Epaulettes' 10c and 20c showing Leopold I, valid from 1 July 1849. Like Great Britain's first stamps they carry no country name, only POSTES and the value — a standard identification trap. Belgium has issued continuously since, with only the two World Wars interrupting national issues, and Dutch-language inscriptions appearing alongside French from 1893. It is a large, long-running, entirely legitimate issuer.

periods.label: Kingdom of Belgium, French-only inscriptions
periods.authority: Administration des Postes, Kingdom of Belgium
periods.note: BELGIQUE only

periods.label: Kingdom of Belgium, bilingual
periods.authority: Regie des Postes / Regie der Posterijen
periods.note: BELGIQUE-BELGIE

periods.label: German occupation, WWI
periods.authority: German Imperial Post, Government-General of Belgium
periods.note: German stamps overprinted BELGIEN with currency surcharge; catalogued in Belgium occupation section

periods.label: Kingdom of Belgium, interwar
periods.authority: Regie des Postes / Regie der Posterijen

periods.label: German occupation, WWII
periods.authority: German military administration
periods.note: Belgian stamps largely remained in use; limited occupation-specific material

periods.label: Kingdom of Belgium, postwar to present
periods.authority: Regie des Postes, later De Post/La Poste, now bpost

inscriptions: BELGIEN (German occupation overprint; also on trilingual issues)
gaps: No true gap. National issues were suspended or curtailed during the 1914-18 German occupation, when overprinted German stamps served the occupied zone while the Belgian government at Le Havre continued limited issues. WWII saw continuity of Belgian issues under occupation.
forgery_risk: The 1849 Epaulettes and 1850-54 Medallions attract forgeries and, more commonly, repairs, reperforating and faked cancellations. The WWI BELGIEN occupation overprints are forged. Overprints generally (Eupen, Malmedy, and the 1919-20 Allied occupation of German territory) are the main danger area.
wallpaper_risk: No. Belgium is a conventional national issuer with no agency arrangement, no IGPC/Stamperija connection, and no UPU illegal-issue listing. Its 20th-century semi-postal output was high but legitimate.
count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (estimate across regular, semi-postal, airmail, parcel post and railway sections)
uncertain: The total is an estimate. Belgian railway/parcel stamps (CHEMINS DE FER / SPOORWEGEN) are frequently mistaken for ordinary postage and are catalogued in a separate section. Belgian Congo, Ruanda-Urundi and the Belgian post offices in Turkey/China are catalogued as separate entities and will not appear under a Belgium country key.

summary: Bolivia's first stamps are the 1867 imperforate engraved 'Condor' set of eight (Scott 1-8), designed by Ramon Estruch and engraved by Casimiro Mallea, inscribed CORREOS BOLIVIA CONTRATOS, each stamp on the plate engraved individually. The 1868 Coat of Arms 'Nine Stars' issue followed. Bolivia has issued continuously as a republic since, through the War of the Pacific, the Chaco War and repeated changes of government, with no change of issuing authority in the catalogue sense. Its classics are among the most heavily forged in South America.

periods.label: Pre-adhesive / stampless
periods.authority: Spanish colonial post, then Bolivian republican post (republic from 1825)
periods.note: Handstruck markings only; no adhesives. Some mail routed via Peru and Chile and franked with their stamps.

periods.label: Republic of Bolivia
periods.authority: Correos de Bolivia / Direccion General de Correos; later ECOBOL, now Agencia Boliviana de Correos (AGBC)
periods.note: Continuous republican issuing. Since 2009 the official state name is the Plurinational State of Bolivia.

predecessors: Peru (some pre-1867 Bolivian mail franked with Peruvian stamps)
inscriptions: ESTADO PLURINACIONAL DE BOLIVIA (modern, from c.2009)
Script: Latin/Spanish throughout, no script change. Some modern issues add Aymara or Quechua wording.
gaps: No documented complete gap since 1867, though output was thin and irregular in several stretches - the 1870s, the War of the Pacific years (1879-1884), and periods of political instability when new issues were postponed rather than cancelled. Pre-1867 is a stampless era, not a gap.
forgery_risk: Very high for the classics. The 1867 Condor and the 1868 Coat of Arms 'Nine Stars' issues are among the most forged stamps of South America. The key test is process: genuine early issues are line-engraved, most forgeries lithographed. For the 1868 issue, genuine stamps are engraved on yellowish-white wove paper perf 12, while forgeries are lithographed on white or yellowish-white wove with perf 12.5 or 13.5. Reprints exist alongside outright forgeries and are routinely sold as originals; thick/thin paper varieties complicate identification. Later risk areas include forged airmail and 1930 Zeppelin-related overprints and other surcharges, where a forged overprint sits on a genuine base stamp. Expertisation strongly advised for anything before 1900.
wallpaper_risk: Not a wallpaper country. Bolivia has no IGPC or Stamperija agency programme and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Output is modest, domestically produced and postally motivated. The caveat is the opposite problem: postal administration has been unreliable at times, and unauthorised or irregularly released material plus reprints circulate - an authenticity problem, not an over-issuing one.
count_source: Approximation of Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue major numbers for Bolivia (regular postage; airmails and back-of-the-book add several hundred more). +/- 20%.
uncertain: Total not verified against a specific catalogue edition and is sensitive to whether the large Bolivian airmail listing is included. The exact date within 1867 of the Condor issue's release was not established. Also unverified: how regularly Peruvian or Chilean stamps were used on Bolivian mail before 1867 - this is often asserted but catalogue treatment of 'Peru used in Bolivia' is not standardised, so early Bolivian postal history can hide under Peru. Myth to flag: the 1867 design is habitually called a condor and the inscription supports that, but the bird as engraved is frequently described in the literature as vulture-like, and species identification from the design alone is not reliable.

summary: Bosnia and Herzegovina has one of the oldest distinct listings in the region: Austria-Hungary began issuing stamps for the occupied territory in 1879, initially a textless design showing the Austrian double-eagle arms. After 1918 the territory used SHS overprints and then Yugoslav stamps. Since 1993 the country is unusual in having THREE concurrent stamp-issuing postal administrations, each with its own catalogue listing.

periods.label: Ottoman period (pre-stamp for BiH)
periods.authority: Ottoman post and foreign post offices
periods.note: No BiH-specific stamps; Ottoman and Austrian foreign-office material only.

periods.label: Austro-Hungarian occupation and annexation
periods.authority: k.u.k. Militarpost / Austro-Hungarian administration
periods.note: First issue 1879, textless double-eagle arms design. 1906 pictorials inscribed BOSNIEN HERZEGOWINA. Military post, newspaper stamps, postage dues 1904 and 1916.

periods.label: State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs - Bosnia
periods.authority: SHS postal administration
periods.note: Austrian/Bosnian 1910 pictorials overprinted for the State of SHS in both Latin and Cyrillic. Catalogued under Yugoslavia.

periods.label: Kingdom of Yugoslavia / NDH / SFR Yugoslavia
periods.authority: Kingdom of Yugoslavia; NDH 1941-45; Yugoslav PTT 1945-92
periods.note: No BiH-specific issues. During WWII the territory fell within the Independent State of Croatia and used NDH stamps.

periods.label: Republic of Bosnia and Herzegovina (Sarajevo)
periods.authority: BH Posta / JP BH Posta, Sarajevo
periods.note: First issue 27 Oct 1993, seven imperforate definitives designed by Jasminko Mulaomerovic and Sava Vasiljevic, showing the blue arms with six gold fleurs-de-lis; produced in Sarajevo under siege.

periods.label: Croat administration
periods.authority: Hrvatska Posta Mostar (HP Mostar)
periods.note: Separate catalogue listing. Originally issued for Herceg-Bosna; labelling harmonised from 2011.

periods.label: Serb administration
periods.authority: Srpske Poste / Posta Srpske, Banja Luka
periods.note: Separate catalogue listing for Republika Srpska.

predecessors: State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs
inscriptions: No inscription at all on the 1879 first issue - arms design only (a key identification point)
HRVATSKA POSTA MOSTAR / HP MOSTAR (Croat administration)
REPUBLIKA SRPSKA / РЕПУБЛИКА СРПСКА and POSTE SRPSKE (Serb administration)
Script varies deliberately by administration: Sarajevo uses Latin and Cyrillic, Mostar Latin only, Banja Luka predominantly Cyrillic
gaps: No BiH-specific issues 1921-1992 — roughly seventy years covered by Yugoslav and, in 1941-45, NDH stamps. Genuine postal disruption 1992-93 during the siege of Sarajevo before the first republic issue in October 1993; mail in that window carries provisional handstamps and adapted Yugoslav stock rather than BiH stamps.
forgery_risk: High for the 1992-93 transitional window. Several widely traded 1992-93 overprint sets are spurious: Yugoslav definitives overprinted 'Souverena Bosna i Herzegovina' and 'Souverana Herzeg-Bosna' with a Mostar postmark dated 06.07.92, and a set of eight Yugoslav definitives with 'Jugoslavija' blacked out and replaced by the Croatian checkerboard arms, sold as 'Bosnia-Herzegovina Croatian overprints'. Both are rejected by the philatelic community. Austro-Hungarian period material is comparatively sound, though the 1879 first issue is forged and perforation varieties are faked.
wallpaper_risk: Mixed, and this needs stating plainly. BiH does not use IGPC or Stamperija and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. However, the three-administration structure produces a triple annual programme for a country of under four million, and the Croat (HP Mostar) and Serb (Banja Luka) administrations in particular have drawn criticism for issue volume and for thematic material aimed at collectors rather than postal need. The 1992-93 bogus overprints are a separate and more serious problem than agency wallpaper.
count_source: Not reliably established. Colnect reports roughly 705 for the Croat administration and 991 for the Serb administration, but no verified figure for BH Posta Sarajevo, the Austro-Hungarian era, or a combined total. Michel Europa is the correct authority and lists the three modern administrations separately.
uncertain: BATCH FILE MISMATCH: batch_15.txt does not contain Bosnia and Herzegovina; it appears in batch_21.txt with 108 records held. Please confirm which list is authoritative. Also unverified: exact combined catalogue count; the precise start dates of the HP Mostar and Srpske Poste listings (both commonly given as 1993 but I could not confirm the day/month for either); the exact nature of the 2011 labelling harmonisation. Trap flag: a country-keyed search on 'Bosnia and Herzegovina' will typically return only the Sarajevo administration, missing both the Mostar and Banja Luka listings entirely, plus the whole 1879-1918 Austro-Hungarian era which some catalogues file under Austria.

summary: Brazil issued the 'Olho de Boi' (Bull's Eye) numerals on 1 August 1843 — 30, 60 and 90 réis — making it the first stamp-issuing country in the Americas and the first after Great Britain to issue stamps valid throughout an entire nation. Issuing authority has run continuously from the Empire (Correio Geral) through the Republic to today's Empresa Brasileira de Correios e Telégrafos, with no meaningful interruption. Early classics carry no country name at all, so identification rests on the numeral designs, the 'CORREIO GERAL DO RIO DE JANEIRO' inscription and the réis denominations. Brazil is a large, well-catalogued, conservative issuer with no agency-driven reputation.

periods.label: Pre-adhesive
periods.authority: Portuguese colonial / Imperial Brazilian postal service
periods.note: Handstruck marks only; no adhesives.

periods.label: Empire of Brazil
periods.authority: Correio Geral, Empire of Brazil (Pedro I / Pedro II)
periods.note: Bull's Eyes 1843, Inclinados 1844, Olhos-de-Gato 1850, Colon rouletted 1854, Dom Pedro portrait series from 1866 — the first to carry the word BRAZIL.

periods.label: Old Republic
periods.authority: Republica dos Estados Unidos do Brazil
periods.note: Inscriptions shift to E.U. DO BRAZIL / ESTADOS UNIDOS DO BRAZIL.

periods.label: Vargas era and Republic
periods.authority: Departamento dos Correios e Telégrafos (DCT)
periods.note: Orthographic reform moves BRAZIL to BRASIL in this period.

periods.label: ECT era
periods.authority: Empresa Brasileira de Correios e Telégrafos (Correios)
periods.note: Still issuing.

inscriptions: (no country name on 1843–1861 numeral issues — identify by design and réis values)
CORREIO GERAL DO RIO DE JANEIRO
gaps: No genuine gaps. Brazil has issued in essentially every year since 1843. The only thing that reads like a gap in some albums is 1846–1849, a lull between the Inclinados and the Olhos-de-Gato.
forgery_risk: The 1843 Bull's Eyes are heavily forged, and equally often 'improved' — reperforated margins, added or cleaned cancels, and fake 30r+60r se-tenant pairs. Plate reconstruction matters: genuine Bull's Eyes must match one of the known plate positions, and this is the standard authentication test. Early Inclinados and Olhos-de-Gato are also faked. Certificates are expected on anything of value in the 1843–1854 range.
wallpaper_risk: No. Brazil does NOT have an agency or wallpaper reputation. Issues are produced by Correios itself, the annual programme is moderate, and Brazil does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars.
count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue — approximate count of major numbers across all Scott categories (regular postage, airmail, semi-postal, newspaper, postage due, official) through the mid-2020s. Michel and RHM (Catálogo de Selos do Brasil, the Brazilian standard) number differently and RHM is the reference collectors there actually use.
uncertain: BATCH LIST CONFLICT: the file batch_03.txt lists Moldova, Romania, Brazil, India — the task message lists Brazil, India, Finland, Armenia. Only Brazil and India appear in both. I profiled the four named in the task message and did NOT profile Moldova or Romania; those two need a separate pass. MYTH TO FLAG: Brazil is routinely called 'the second country in the world to issue postage stamps'. Precisely stated, it was the second to issue stamps valid nationwide, but the Zurich cantonal stamps of Switzerland appeared on 1 March 1843, five months before the Bull's Eyes, so Brazil is third among issuing authorities. Both claims circulate as if identical. The exact total-stamp count is an estimate; catalogue totals for Brazil vary by well over 20 percent depending on whether RHM or Scott numbering is used, and RHM splits many issues Scott lumps.

count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (approximate major numbers across regular postage, semi-postal, airmail and postage due, to mid-2020s); Michel Sudosteuropa runs roughly 10-15% higher
summary: Bulgaria's own stamps begin 1 May 1879 with the 'Lion' definitives of the autonomous Principality, denominated in centimes/francs and inscribed wholly in Cyrillic. Before that the territory was served by Ottoman post and by foreign post offices, so pre-1879 covers are catalogued under Turkey and under the Russian/Austrian Levant offices. Bulgaria has issued continuously ever since, through Principality, Kingdom, People's Republic and Republic, with no true gap year. The parallel Eastern Rumelia / South Bulgaria issues of 1881-85 are catalogued separately and are the most forgery-prone corner of the area.

periods.label: Ottoman period (no Bulgarian issues)
periods.authority: Ottoman Empire postal administration; also Russian and Austrian Levant post offices
periods.note: Material is catalogued under Turkey / Turkish Empire and under the Levant offices, not under Bulgaria - a common country-keyed search trap.

periods.label: Principality of Bulgaria
periods.authority: Principality of Bulgaria (Ottoman suzerainty, de facto independent)
periods.note: First 'Lion' definitives 1 May 1879. Cyrillic-only inscriptions from the outset.

periods.label: Eastern Rumelia / South Bulgaria (separate catalogue country)
periods.authority: Autonomous province of Eastern Rumelia, then Bulgarian provisional administration after the 1885 union
periods.note: Own issues from January 1881; 1880 Turkish stamps overprinted ROUMELIE ORIENTALE were prepared but refused by the provincial government. September 1885 Bulgarian lion overprints = South Bulgaria.

periods.label: Kingdom (Tsardom) of Bulgaria
periods.authority: Kingdom of Bulgaria
periods.note: Includes Bulgarian occupation issues of Romania (1916-18), Thrace, and WWII administration in Greek Macedonia/Thrace and parts of Yugoslav Macedonia.

periods.label: People's Republic of Bulgaria
periods.authority: People's Republic of Bulgaria (Narodna Republika Bulgaria)
periods.note: Very high output of thematic/topical sets, much of it CTO and aimed at hard-currency export.

periods.label: Republic of Bulgaria
periods.authority: Republic of Bulgaria / Bulgarian Posts
periods.note: Output sharply reduced after 1990; euro-era conventional programme.

predecessors: Russian Post Offices in the Turkish Empire
Austrian Post Offices in the Turkish Empire
inscriptions: БЪЛГАРСКА ПОЩА (Bulgarian Post) - standard on early issues
BULGARIA / BULGARIE - Latin script added on some 20th-century and most post-1990 issues, sometimes alongside Cyrillic
Ottoman Turkish and French inscriptions on the 1881-84 Eastern Rumelia definitives
gaps: No genuine gap years since 1879. Output thinned severely in 1944-45 during the change of regime but did not stop. Eastern Rumelia/South Bulgaria as a separate issuer stops in 1885 at the union with Bulgaria.
forgery_risk: High in specific areas rather than across the board. The 1880 ROUMELIE ORIENTALE overprints on Turkish stamps are extensively counterfeited - genuine examples are scarce and were never officially issued. The 1885 South Bulgaria lion overprints are widely forged (Fournier forgeries are covered in the Serrane Guide; Schlesinger forged overprints are documented in Woodward's 'South Bulgaria: The Lion Overprints', Collectors Club Philatelist). Early Principality Lion definitives exist with faked cancels and repaired perforations. Bulgarian WWI and WWII occupation overprints (Romania, Thrace, Macedonia) also attract forged overprints.
wallpaper_risk: Bulgaria is NOT an agency-driven issuer and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. It has no IGPC or Stamperija involvement. It does, however, carry a real reputation for excessive communist-era output: 1950s-1980s topicals (space, sport, art, fauna) were produced in large quantities, much of it cancelled-to-order and sold in packet mixtures, so common-period Bulgaria has very low market value despite high catalogue counts. That is a CTO/oversupply issue, not an illegal-issue one.
uncertain: DISCREPANCY: the supplied file batch_14.txt lists United Kingdom, Iran, Serbia and Syria, not the four countries named in my instructions. I profiled the countries named in the instructions; the file's records-held figures do not correspond to this batch and I could not obtain a records-held count for Bulgaria. Total-stamp count is an order-of-magnitude estimate; I could not verify an exact Scott high number and catalogues disagree by well over 20% here depending on whether occupation issues are included. Exact day-of-issue for the very first 1879 values is given as 1 May 1879 by most sources but some cite late April - not independently confirmed. Myth to flag: Bulgaria is often said to have 'started issuing in 1878' with independence; the Principality was created in 1878 but the first stamps are 1879.

summary: Canada's first stamp is the 23 April 1851 three-pence Beaver of the Province of Canada, designed by Sandford Fleming - the world's first pictorial postage stamp and a break with the Empire convention of using the monarch's head. Issuing has been continuous since. The major catalogue trap is that the pre-Confederation British North American colonies - Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, Prince Edward Island, Newfoundland, British Columbia and Vancouver Island - are separate catalogue countries, and Newfoundland remains separate right up to 1949.

periods.label: Province of Canada
periods.authority: Post Office of the Province of Canada (postal control transferred from the British GPO in 1851)
periods.note: Pence issues 1851-1858, decimal cents issues from 1859.

periods.label: Dominion of Canada
periods.authority: Canada Post Office Department
periods.note: Begins with the 1868 Large Queens. Newfoundland only joins in 1949.

periods.label: Canada Post Corporation
periods.authority: Canada Post Corporation (Crown corporation from 1981)
periods.note: Permanent (P) non-denominated definitives from 2006.

inscriptions: CANADA POSTES POSTAGE (bilingual form, standard from the 1960s onward)
EXHIBITION MONTREAL / registration and special delivery wording on back-of-book
P with maple leaf (Permanent rate non-value indicator, from 2006)
gaps: None. Canada has issued without interruption since 1851.
forgery_risk: Moderate and well-documented. The 12d black of 1851 is one of the most famous rarities and reproductions and forgeries abound - genuine examples are counted in the dozens. Early pence issues are targets for re-gumming, repair and faked cancels rather than wholesale forgery. Re-perforated and fraudulently altered Small Queens and Jubilees appear. Faked overprints exist on the 1899 provisional surcharges and the 'O.H.M.S.' and 'G' official overprints, which are frequently forged. Modern counterfeit definitives made to defraud Canada Post have been reported. The 1959 St Lawrence Seaway inverted centre is genuine but has been imitated.
wallpaper_risk: No. Canada runs a conservative state programme with no agency involvement and has never appeared on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Modern annual output is moderate by world standards, though the picture-postage and personalised-stamp products are outside the collectable catalogue for most collectors.
count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue, Canada regular postage major numbers (approximate). Canadian specialists use Unitrade, which follows Scott numbering but lists far more varieties, papers and tagging types.
uncertain: TASK DISCREPANCY: the batch file lists Philippines, Belgium, Kyrgyzstan, South Africa rather than the countries in my instructions - please confirm which list is authoritative. The stamp total is approximate and edition-dependent. Myth flag: the 1851 Beaver is sometimes described as 'the first stamp of Canada the nation'; it is an issue of the Province of Canada, sixteen years before Confederation, and its territory did not include the Maritimes, the West or Newfoundland. Catalogue trap: all six British North American colonies listed as predecessors are separate country headings, and Newfoundland issues run 1857-1949 in parallel with Canada - a Canada-keyed search hides nearly a century of Newfoundland material.

summary: Chile issued its first stamps on 1 July 1853: a 5c and 10c bearing a left-facing bust of Christopher Columbus on an engine-turned ground, engraved and printed by Perkins Bacon & Co of London - the first stamps in the world to portray Columbus. Plates were transferred to Santiago in 1854 and local printings ran alongside London printings to about 1865, so the 'Colon' issues divide into London and Santiago printings that specialists separate by paper, shade and impression quality. Chile has issued continuously since 1853 under a single authority, with no colonial, occupation or successor complications on its own soil. Its issuing history is unusually clean; the complications are external, in the material it produced for occupied Peruvian and Bolivian territory during the War of the Pacific.

periods.label: Pre-adhesive
periods.authority: Republic of Chile postal service (Spanish colonial administration ended 1818)
periods.note: Handstruck marks only.

periods.label: Colon (Columbus) issues
periods.authority: Republic of Chile - Correos de Chile
periods.note: Perkins Bacon London printings from 1853, Santiago local printings from 1854/55 alongside London printings to c.1865.

periods.label: War of the Pacific occupation usage
periods.authority: Chilean occupation authorities in Peru and Bolivia
periods.note: Chilean stamps and overprinted/handstamped Peruvian material used in occupied territory. Catalogued under Peru, not Chile.

periods.label: Republic - modern issuing
periods.authority: Correos de Chile (state postal administration)
periods.note: Continuous through the 1891 civil war, the 1973-90 military government and the return to democracy, without interruption of issuing.

predecessors: Spain (pre-adhesive colonial mails only; no Spanish colonial stamps for Chile - independence 1818 predates adhesives)
inscriptions: COLON (arched above the Columbus bust, 1853-67 issues)
TERRITORIO CHILENO ANTARTICO / ANTARTICA CHILENA (Antarctic claim issues within Chile's own listing)
gaps: None. Chile has issued continuously since 1853, including through the 1891 civil war and the 1879-84 War of the Pacific.
forgery_risk: Concentrated in the 1853-67 Colon issues. Forgeries of the Columbus design exist, but the larger practical risk is misattribution and manipulation rather than outright fakery: London Perkins Bacon printings are far more desirable than the Santiago locals and are routinely misdescribed, and faked cancellations, repaired margins and altered shades are common on higher-value Colons. Reprints are a live problem - in 1910 the Chilean specialist Dr Hugo Hahn acquired the original engraved plate of the 20c Columbus and persuaded the government to authorise centenary reprints with new-value overprints; public objection led to the permission being withdrawn, but reprint and proof material from the original plates does circulate and is offered as issued stamps. Expertisation is standard for classic Colons.
wallpaper_risk: No. Chile is a restrained issuer with no agency involvement (no IGPC or Stamperija connection) and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. All issues are genuine postal issues of Correos de Chile.
count_source: StampWorld listing count for Chile (major numbers, postage plus back-of-book, 1853 to 2025). Approximate; Scott's count is broadly similar.
uncertain: CATALOGUE TRAP: Chile's War of the Pacific material (1879-84) - Chilean stamps used in occupied Peru, and Peruvian stamps handstamped or overprinted by Chilean occupation authorities in Arequipa, Ancachs, Chala, Paita, Pisco, Tacna and elsewhere - is catalogued under PERU (in part under Peru's 'Chilean Occupation' section), not under Chile. A Chile-keyed search shows nothing for one of the most philatelically active episodes in Chilean postal administration. The total is approximate and I could not verify it against a named printed catalogue edition; catalogues differ notably over Chile's officials and Antarctic issues. I could not establish how many distinct forgery types of the Colon issues are recorded in the literature, and I found no substantiating source on faked 'Correos' overprints specifically, so that claim should not be treated as established.

summary: China's first government stamps are the imperforate-perforated 'Large Dragons' issued by the Imperial Maritime Customs Post at Shanghai in 1878, inscribed CHINA in Latin letters and Chinese characters, denominated in candareens. Local Treaty Port posts (Shanghai Local Post from 1865) precede this but are catalogued separately. The country then runs Empire (to 1912), Republic (1912-49), a dense field of wartime puppet/occupation and Communist Liberated Area issues, and the People's Republic from 1949. The single most useful identification cue is the change of inscription from 中国人民邮政 to 中国邮政 + CHINA at the start of 1992.

periods.label: Treaty Port / local posts
periods.authority: Shanghai Municipal Council and other foreign-run municipal local posts
periods.note: Shanghai Local Post 1865 onward; closed when the Imperial Post took over. Catalogued under 'China - Treaty Ports' or 'Shanghai', NOT under China proper.

periods.label: Imperial Customs Post
periods.authority: Imperial Maritime Customs, Shanghai
periods.note: Large Dragons, Small Dragons, Empress Dowager issue.

periods.label: Chinese Imperial Post
periods.authority: Imperial Post (edict of 20 March 1896, effective 1 Jan 1897)
periods.note: Includes the 1897 Red Revenue surcharges.

periods.label: Republic of China (mainland)
periods.authority: Chunghwa Post / Directorate General of Posts, ROC
periods.note: Includes 1912 Provisional Neutrality and Founding of Republic overprints, Junk/Reaper/Hall of Classics definitives, hyperinflation surcharges 1948-49.

periods.label: Foreign post offices in China
periods.authority: British, French, German, Japanese, Russian, US and Italian postal administrations
periods.note: Catalogued under the parent country (e.g. 'Great Britain Offices in China', 'French Offices in China', 'Kiautschou' under German Colonies) - a major country-keyed search trap.

periods.label: Japanese-sponsored and occupation regimes
periods.authority: Manchukuo; North China (Hua Pei); Central China (Nanking/Shanghai); Mengkiang; Reformed Government
periods.note: Manchukuo is usually a separate catalogue country; the overprinted occupation districts sit at the end of the China listing.

periods.label: Communist Liberated Areas
periods.authority: Regional CCP postal administrations (North East China, East China, Central China, North China, Port Arthur & Dairen etc.)
periods.note: Sprawling, poorly standardised, heavily forged; separately sectioned in Scott and in Chinese catalogues.

periods.label: People's Republic of China
periods.authority: China People's Post / China Post (中国邮政)
periods.note: Inscription 中国人民邮政 to Nov 1991; 中国邮政 + CHINA from 1992.

periods.label: Republic of China on Taiwan
periods.authority: Chunghwa Post, Taipei
periods.note: Catalogued separately as 'China, Republic of (Taiwan)'; earlier Japanese-period Taiwan material is under Japan.

inscriptions: 大清國郵政 (Imperial Chinese Post, Empire)
中華民國郵政 (Republic of China Post)
中國人民郵政 / 中国人民邮政 (People's Post of China, 1949-1991)
Script note: traditional characters before 1949 and on Taiwan; simplified characters on PRC issues from the later 1950s. Early values in candareens/cents, later in yuan/fen.
gaps: No true nationwide gap. Postal service fragmented rather than stopped: 1937-45 mainland issues split among Nationalist, occupation and Liberated Area authorities; 1966-1969 the Cultural Revolution sharply curtailed the PRC programme (few sets, and the numbering series was suspended, resuming as the 'N' series in 1967-70).
forgery_risk: Very high across the whole area. Large Dragons and Small Dragons are extensively forged and reperforated/regummed; the 1897 Red Revenue surcharges (especially the Small One Dollar) are among the most faked items in world philately. 1912 Provisional Neutrality overprints, Liberated Area issues, and Japanese-occupation district overprints are forged wholesale. PRC rarities - the 1968 'Whole Country is Red', the 1967 unissued items, the 1980 Monkey - have large forgery and reprint populations.
wallpaper_risk: No. Neither the PRC nor the ROC has an agency-driven or excessive-issue reputation; both run disciplined, state-run programmes and do not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. The wallpaper problem in the China area comes from post-1972 bogus 'Chinese' labels sold to tourists and from fantasy Tibet/Manchuria items, not from the postal administrations.
count_source: Rough order-of-magnitude for Empire + Republic (mainland) + PRC major numbers combined in Scott Standard/Scott Classic Specialized; Chinese-language catalogues (Ma's Illustrated Catalogue for the early period, the ACPF/China Post catalogue for the PRC) number differently and Michel differs again. Not a verified figure.
uncertain: Exact count not verified against a current catalogue edition. The traditional '1 May 1878' first-day date for the Large Dragons is not securely established - sources differ, with dates through July-September 1878 also cited; treat the first day as unresolved and the year 1878 as the safe fact. Note also the myth that the Shanghai 1865 'Large Dragon' local stamps are China No. 1 - they are a municipal local post and catalogued apart. Big cataloguing trap: foreign post offices in China, Manchukuo, Kiautschou, Taiwan under Japan, and the Treaty Ports are all filed under other countries, so a country-keyed search on 'China' silently loses those eras.

summary: Colombia's first national stamps went on sale 1 September 1859, inscribed CONFED. GRANADINA CORREOS NACIONALES. The country name on the stamps changes repeatedly with the constitution — Estados Unidos de Nueva Granada (1861), Estados Unidos de Colombia (1862-1886), then Republica de Colombia — which makes the inscription the single best dating tool. A law of 3 June 1859 let the sovereign states run their own posts, producing the separately-catalogued state issues (Antioquia, Bolivar, Cundinamarca, Tolima, Santander, Boyaca, Cauca) that are among the most heavily forged material in the Americas.

periods.label: Granadine Confederation
periods.authority: Correos Nacionales, Confederacion Granadina

periods.label: United States of New Granada
periods.authority: Estados Unidos de Nueva Granada
periods.note: Very short-lived inscription

periods.label: United States of Colombia
periods.authority: Estados Unidos de Colombia
periods.note: Concurrent sovereign-state issues

periods.label: Republic of Colombia
periods.authority: Republica de Colombia
periods.note: Panama a Colombian department until 1903

periods.label: SCADTA / private airmail concession
periods.authority: Sociedad Colombo-Alemana de Transportes Aereos
periods.note: Semi-official airmails with country-initial overprints; absorbed into Avianca

gaps: No sustained gap in national issues since 1859, though supply was disrupted during the War of a Thousand Days (1899-1902), when provisional and departmental emergency issues proliferated.
forgery_risk: Very high, and among the worst in world philately. The sovereign-state issues — especially Antioquia and the tiny Bolivar values, printed lithographically in quantities as low as a few hundred — exist in large numbers of forgeries and reprints (genuine sheets show dividing lines between stamps; reprints lack them). SCADTA consular overprints with country initials are extensively forged. War of a Thousand Days provisionals and Cauca/Tolima issues are also dangerous.
wallpaper_risk: No. Colombia is a conventional national issuer with no agency-driven programme, no IGPC/Stamperija involvement, and no UPU illegal-issue listing. Modern output is moderate.
count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (Colombia regular, airmail and SCADTA sections; estimate, excludes the separately-listed states)
uncertain: The total is an estimate and deliberately excludes the state issues, which add well over a thousand further numbers under separate catalogue headings. Two traps: (a) Antioquia, Bolivar, Boyaca, Cauca, Cundinamarca, Santander and Tolima are catalogued as separate countries, so a Colombia-keyed search misses 1868-1904 entirely; (b) Panama's pre-1903 stamps are Colombian State of Panama issues and are catalogued under Panama.

summary: Croatian territory used Austrian and Hungarian stamps to 1918. The first Croatian-inscribed stamps were the 1918 'Hrvatska SHS' overprints on Hungarian stamps, followed by distinct SHS Croatia issues in 1919 — all catalogued under Yugoslavia. A fully separate Croatian country-listing exists for the Independent State of Croatia (NDH) 1941-45 and again for the Republic from 1991.

periods.label: Austro-Hungarian period
periods.authority: Hungarian post (Croatia-Slavonia) and Austrian post (Dalmatia, Istria)
periods.note: Generic Hungarian/Austrian stamps.

periods.label: State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs - Croatia
periods.authority: SHS postal administration, Zagreb
periods.note: 'Hrvatska SHS' overprints on Hungarian stamps, then dedicated 1919 issues. Catalogued under Yugoslavia.

periods.label: Kingdom of SHS / Yugoslavia
periods.authority: Kingdom of SHS, then Kingdom of Yugoslavia
periods.note: No Croatia-specific issues.

periods.label: Independent State of Croatia (NDH)
periods.authority: Nezavisna Drzava Hrvatska, Zagreb (Axis puppet state)
periods.note: Began with overprints on Yugoslav stock reading NEZAVISNA DRZAVA HRVATSKA, then full inscriptions. 'Landscape' definitives, 23 values, Nov 1941-Sep 1942, in use to 1944. Own catalogue listing as Croatia.

periods.label: Italian-held enclaves (Zara, Fiume/Rijeka, Dalmatia)
periods.authority: Italian administration, later German military administration
periods.note: Catalogued separately under Fiume, Zara, Italian Occupation - not under Croatia.

periods.label: SFR Yugoslavia
periods.authority: Yugoslav PTT
periods.note: 1945 transitional overprints applied a star plus 'Jugoslavija' or 'Demokratska Federativna' to NDH stock.

periods.label: Republic of Croatia
periods.authority: Hrvatska Posta (HP)
periods.note: First stamp (airmail, coat of arms) 9 Sep 1991; first regular definitive 21 Nov 1991.

periods.label: Republic of Serbian Krajina (breakaway)
periods.authority: RSK postal administration, Knin
periods.note: Self-proclaimed entity; issued its own stamps, reintegrated by 1998. Catalogued separately; philatelic status contested.

predecessors: State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs
inscriptions: Latin script throughout; no Cyrillic on Croatian issues
gaps: No Croatia-specific issues 1921-1941 and 1945-1991 (absorbed into Yugoslav issues). No true postal-collapse gap.
forgery_risk: Significant. The 1918 'Hrvatska SHS' overprints on Hungarian stamps are extensively forged — overprint forgeries on genuine Hungarian stock are cheap to make and common. NDH 1941-45 material has forged overprints, forged perforations and faked imperforate/proof varieties, plus a large body of privately produced NDH-era labels and unissued designs sold as genuine. The 1945 star/'Jugoslavija' overprints on NDH stock are also faked. Republic of Serbian Krajina issues include both genuine postal use and outright fantasy production.
wallpaper_risk: No. Croatia does NOT have an agency-driven or excessive-issue reputation. Hrvatska Posta runs a conventional, self-administered programme and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Note that the separate Croat administration in Bosnia (HP Mostar) has been criticised for issue volume, but that is a distinct catalogue entity.
count_source: Colnect country total for 'Croatia' (which appears to bundle NDH and Republic issues). Colnect is a community catalogue, not a standard reference; Michel Europa or Scott would give a materially different figure and the two disagree by well over the usual 20 percent because of differing treatment of NDH, se-tenant and definitive reprints. Treat as indicative only.
uncertain: BATCH FILE MISMATCH: batch_15.txt does not contain Croatia; Croatia appears in batch_17.txt with 187 records held. Please confirm which list is authoritative. Count not verified against a standard catalogue. Trap flag: the entire 1918-1921 'Hrvatska SHS' era and all 1921-1941 and 1945-1991 material sit under Yugoslavia, so a country-keyed search on 'Croatia' returns only NDH 1941-45 and Republic 1991-present, silently hiding roughly 70 years. Likewise Fiume, Zara and Dalmatia are separate catalogue countries despite being Croatian territory today. I could not verify the exact date the first Serbian Krajina stamps appeared.

summary: Cuba's first stamps appeared in April 1855, but they were not Cuban: they were general issues for the Spanish Antilles, valid in both Cuba and Puerto Rico, inscribed only ULTRAMAR (overseas) and carrying the portrait of Isabella II. Cuba received separate stamps from 1873, and the word CUBA first appears on stamps in 1877. The island passed to United States military administration after the 1898 Treaty of Paris, using US stamps overprinted and surcharged for Cuba, and became an independent Republic issuer in 1902. Issuing has been continuous under the socialist Republic since 1959, with very high output of collector-oriented topicals from that date. The 1855-1873 Antilles period is the classic identification trap: those stamps belong to Cuba's history but are shared with Puerto Rico and are inscribed with neither name.

periods.label: Spanish Antilles general issues
periods.authority: Kingdom of Spain - colonial administration; issues valid in Cuba and Puerto Rico jointly
periods.note: Inscribed ULTRAMAR only; no country name. Shared with Puerto Rico.

periods.label: Spanish colonial - Cuba separate issues
periods.authority: Kingdom of Spain - Captaincy General of Cuba
periods.note: Word CUBA first appears 1877; later issues under Alfonso XII and the Alfonso XIII 'baby' types.

periods.label: United States military administration
periods.authority: US Military Government of Cuba / US Post Office Department
periods.note: Puerto Principe provisional surcharges on remaining Spanish stock from December 1898; US stamps overprinted CUBA and surcharged in centavos from 1899.

periods.label: Republic of Cuba
periods.authority: Republica de Cuba national postal administration
periods.note: Includes the Machado and Batista periods; no break in issuing.

periods.label: Socialist Republic of Cuba
periods.authority: Republica de Cuba - Correos de Cuba (state postal administration)
periods.note: Very large topical output aimed at the collector market from the early 1960s; much of it available cancelled-to-order.

predecessors: Spanish West Indies / Spanish Antilles (1855-73 ULTRAMAR general issues, shared with Puerto Rico)
United States (administration 1898-1902; US stamps overprinted for Cuba)
