inscriptions: ULTRAMAR (1855-73 Antilles general issues - no country name at all)
Y1/4, Y1/2 etc. (Antilles value expressions in reales/escudos)
HABILITADO PARA (Puerto Principe and other 1898 provisional surcharges)
CUBA overprinted on United States stamps (1899-1902)
PATRIA Y LIBERTAD and similar slogans on post-1959 issues
gaps: No true gap. The nearest thing is late 1898 to early 1899, when Spanish issuing had ceased and US-overprinted stamps had not yet been supplied - covered by the improvised Puerto Principe provisional surcharges of December 1898 on remaining Spanish stock, so postal service and adhesives continued without an issue-free year.
forgery_risk: High in two well-defined areas. First, the December 1898 Puerto Principe provisional surcharges: scarce, produced locally on existing Spanish Cuban stock, and forgeries are prevalent - among the most consistently faked items in Caribbean philately. Second, the 1855-56 Antilles surcharges, of which forgeries are long established. Forged and re-applied CUBA overprints on US stamps from the 1899-1902 administration are also known. A third distinct category is the bogus/fantasy material of the independence-struggle period: various 'Cuba Libre' and insurgent-government labels of 1868-98 were never valid postal issues and are fantasies or private propaganda labels, though regularly offered as stamps. Expertisation is essential for any Puerto Principe surcharge.
wallpaper_risk: Yes, in the volume sense - Cuba has a genuine over-issue reputation. From the early 1960s the post-revolutionary administration greatly expanded its programme with heavy topical output (space, sport, fauna, art, Olympics and other collector themes), and sets from the 1960s onward are widely available cancelled-to-order and sold in bulk at deep discounts to face value. IMPORTANT DISTINCTION: this is not illegitimacy. Cuba is a UPU member, its stamps are genuine issues of the state postal administration, it does NOT appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars, and it has no IGPC or Stamperija agency relationship. The complaint is excessive commercial issuing, not fabricated issues.
count_source: StampWorld listing count for Cuba (major numbers, 1855 to 2022/2025, postage plus airmail and back-of-book). Scott is broadly comparable. Approximate - post-1959 topical volume makes the total unusually sensitive to how miniature sheets and se-tenant blocks are numbered.
uncertain: CATALOGUE TRAP, and a significant one: the 1855-1873 issues were general issues for the Spanish Antilles valid in BOTH Cuba and Puerto Rico, inscribed only ULTRAMAR with no country name. Catalogues handle them differently - some list them at the head of Cuba, some duplicate them under both Cuba and Puerto Rico, some place them under a separate 'Spanish West Indies' heading - so a Cuba-keyed search may return them, miss them, or double-count them against Puerto Rico. A loose ULTRAMAR stamp cannot be assigned to Cuba or Puerto Rico from its face at all; only the cancellation identifies the island of use. MYTH FLAG: it is often stated flatly that 'Cuba's first stamps are 1855' as though they were Cuban stamps - they were not, and Cuba has no stamp bearing its own name before 1877. A second common confusion treats the 1868-98 'Cuba Libre' and insurgent labels as postally valid revolutionary issues; they are not, and no standard catalogue lists them as such. I could not verify a precise total from a named printed catalogue edition, nor establish whether the exact date of the Puerto Principe surcharge issue is settled in the literature beyond 'December 1898'.

count_source: Pofis (Czech specialised catalogue), major numbers 1993 to mid-2020s; Pofis restarted numbering at 1 in 1993, which makes it the cleanest anchor. Scott and Michel numbering for Czech Republic also restart in 1993 and run broadly parallel.
summary: The Czech Republic as a stamp-issuing entity begins 20 January 1993, after the peaceful dissolution of Czechoslovakia on 1 January 1993. For any collector the critical point is that everything before 1993 is catalogued elsewhere: Austria before 1918, Czechoslovakia 1918-1939 and 1945-1992, and the Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia 1939-1945. A country-keyed search on 'Czech Republic' silently hides three-quarters of a century of Czech-territory philately. Output is conservative and traditionally engraved, with a strong reputation for design quality.

periods.label: Austrian rule (catalogued under Austria)
periods.authority: Imperial Austrian post (k.k. Post)
periods.note: First stamps used on Czech territory are the Austrian issues of 1 June 1850. Nothing is catalogued under Czechia for this era.

periods.label: First Czechoslovak Republic
periods.authority: Czechoslovakia (Ceskoslovenska posta)
periods.note: First issue the Hradcany (Prague Castle) design by Alfons Mucha, December 1918. Catalogued under Czechoslovakia.

periods.label: Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia
periods.authority: Nazi German protectorate administration
periods.note: Czechoslovak stamps overprinted from July 1939, then own issues. Separate catalogue country. Sudetenland (1938) is a further separate and largely unofficial area.

periods.label: Restored / Communist Czechoslovakia
periods.authority: Czechoslovak Republic, then Czechoslovak Socialist Republic
periods.note: Catalogued under Czechoslovakia throughout.

periods.label: Czech Republic
periods.authority: Czech Republic / Ceska posta
periods.note: First issues 20 January 1993. Slovakia becomes a parallel successor issuer the same year.

inscriptions: CESKA REPUBLICA / Ceska republika - the definitive marker of a post-1993 Czech stamp
CESKOSLOVENSKO - Czechoslovakia, 1918-1939 and 1945-1992 (also POSTA CESKOSLOVENSKA on the 1918-19 Hradcany and legionary issues)
BOHMEN UND MAHREN / CECHY A MORAVA - bilingual German/Czech, Protectorate 1939-1945
GROSSDEUTSCHES REICH BOHMEN UND MAHREN on later Protectorate issues
KREUZER / KR and Austrian German-language inscriptions pre-1918
Latin script throughout; no script change, but diacritics (hacek, carka) are a useful authenticity cue on Czech-language issues
gaps: No gaps for the Czech Republic itself since 1993. On the territory, there is no year without stamps in use since 1850, but the ISSUING AUTHORITY changes abruptly in 1918, 1939, 1945 and 1993. Note the 1918-19 transition when Austrian stamps remained in use alongside the first Czechoslovak issues, and 1945 when Protectorate stamps were locally overprinted during the liberation.
forgery_risk: Modern Czech Republic issues are essentially not forged - there is no commercial incentive. The forgery risk sits entirely in the predecessor areas a collector will encounter alongside them: the 1939 Bohemia and Moravia overprints (genuine overprints are composed of tiny dots, counterfeits typically show solid lines - a reliable and widely cited test), the 1938 Sudetenland 'liberation' overprints (Asch, Karlsbad, Rumburg and others), which are overwhelmingly private, unofficial or outright bogus, and the 1919 Czechoslovak overprints and 1945 local revolutionary overprints, which exist in large numbers of fabricated varieties. J.S. Gomez-Jeria has published a forgery reference for Bohemia and Moravia.
wallpaper_risk: None. The Czech Republic is NOT an agency issuer, has no IGPC or Stamperija involvement, and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Annual output is modest (roughly 40-50 major numbers a year), postally justified, and the country is regarded as one of Europe's best classical engravers.
uncertain: DISCREPANCY: the supplied file batch_14.txt lists United Kingdom, Iran, Serbia and Syria, not the countries named in my instructions; I profiled the countries in the instructions and could not obtain a records-held figure for Czechia. The ~1,250 figure is a projection from a known issuing rate rather than a directly verified catalogue high number, so treat it as approximate. Myth to flag: it is often stated that the Czech Republic 'continued Czechoslovak numbering' - it did not; Pofis, Scott and Michel all restart at 1 in 1993, so a low catalogue number can mean either 1918 Czechoslovakia or 1993 Czechia and the country name must be checked. Also frequently muddled: Sudetenland 1938 overprints are commonly sold as official German issues; the great majority were locally produced and are not recognised.

count_source: Estimate from Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (Denmark regular postage, semi-postals, airmails, officials, newspaper stamps) through the 2020s; the Danish AFA catalogue is the domestic standard and counts differently
summary: Denmark issued its first stamps on 1 April 1851 (4 rigsbankskilling brown for national postage), followed on 1 May by the 2 rbs blue for Copenhagen local use. The early inscriptions are the key identification point and change form repeatedly: KONGELIGT POST FRIMAERKE and FRIMAERKE / KGL. POST in 1851, abbreviated to KGL. POST FRM. from 1852-54 so the text could be read as either Danish or German (a deliberate accommodation to the German-speaking duchies), and DANMARK from the later 19th century. Denmark has issued continuously since 1851 including through the German occupation of 1940-45. Its former colonies and the duchies are all separate catalogue countries.

periods.label: Kingdom of Denmark (classic)
periods.authority: Kongelige Danske Postvaesen (Royal Danish Post)
periods.note: Rigsbankskilling then skilling currency; the 'Royal Emblems' square designs. Bilingual-readable abbreviated inscriptions from 1852.

periods.label: Kingdom of Denmark (modern)
periods.authority: Post Danmark / PostNord Danmark (from 2009)
periods.note: Ore/krone currency from 1875. Continuous, including through the 1940-45 German occupation, when Danish stamps continued in use under the Danish administration.

periods.label: Danish colonies and dependencies
periods.authority: separate colonial and dependency administrations
periods.note: Danish West Indies 1855-1917 (sold to the USA, becoming the US Virgin Islands); Greenland from 1938 (parcel post from 1905); Faroe Islands from 1975 (provisional 1919 and 1940-41 issues earlier).

periods.label: Schleswig / Slesvig plebiscite
periods.authority: International Plebiscite Commission (CIS)
periods.note: Issues for the 1920 plebiscite zones; northern Slesvig returned to Denmark. Catalogued as Slesvig, a separate entity.

successors: Danish West Indies (to 1917, then United States / US Virgin Islands)
inscriptions: KGL. POST FRM. (1852-54, deliberately readable as Danish or German)
gaps: None. Denmark has issued in every year since 1851, including throughout the German occupation of 1940-45, during which the Danish postal administration continued to function and Danish stamps remained in use — this is a common point of confusion, as no German occupation issues were made for Denmark proper.
forgery_risk: Moderate and well-defined. The 1851 first issue, especially the 2 rbs, is forged and — more commonly — repaired, reperforated or improved at the margins; Ferslew vs Thiele printings and plate identification drive value sharply, so misattribution is as much a risk as outright forgery. The classic skilling issues attract forged cancellations, particularly the scarce numeral cancels, and cancellation forgery is the dominant Danish problem rather than stamp forgery. Danish West Indies overprints and surcharges are extensively forged and include bogus items. Faroe Islands 1919 and 1940-41 provisionals are heavily forged. Fournier produced Danish and Danish West Indies facsimiles.
wallpaper_risk: None. Denmark has no agency involvement (no IGPC, no Stamperija), does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars, and runs a famously restrained and design-led programme — it is frequently cited as a model of conservative issuing policy. Greenland and the Faroes are likewise clean issuers.
uncertain: BATCH LIST CONFLICT: this country appears in the task message's list but not in the batch file, which names Finland, Armenia, Switzerland and Malaysia. See the note on Switzerland. CATALOGUE TRAP: Danish West Indies (1855-1917), Greenland (1938-, parcel post 1905-), Faroe Islands (1975-, with 1919 and 1940-41 provisionals) and Slesvig (1920) are all separate catalogue countries — a country-keyed search on 'Denmark' misses them, and the Danish West Indies record continues under United States/US Virgin Islands after 1917. Schleswig-Holstein's own 1850s issues are catalogued under Schleswig-Holstein and belong to the German area, not Denmark, despite Danish sovereignty at the time — that is a genuine trap. MYTH: it is sometimes said that Denmark issued no stamps or used German stamps during 1940-45; Danish stamps were used throughout and there are no German occupation issues for Denmark proper. Also, the 1851 4 rbs is often described as inscribed 'KGL. POST FRM.' — that abbreviated form belongs to the 1852 and later printings, not the 1851 first issue. My total-stamp count is an unverified estimate and Scott and AFA differ materially.

count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (Egypt); the Balian catalogue is the specialist standard for Egypt
summary: Egypt's first stamps were issued on 1 January 1866, printed in Genoa, with the country name and value supplied in Arabic. Egypt has issued continuously since, through the Khedivate, the British protectorate and occupation, the independent Kingdom, the 1953 Republic, the United Arab Republic period and the modern Arab Republic. A significant cataloguing trap: British Forces in Egypt issues, the British occupation-era material, and Egypt's own 1948-1967 Palestine/Gaza overprints are catalogued under separate headings and will not appear in an Egypt-keyed search.

periods.label: Khedivate under Ottoman suzerainty
periods.authority: Egyptian Post (Poste Khedevie Egiziane), from the nationalised Posta Europea
periods.note: First issue 1 January 1866, printed in Genoa. Egypt remained nominally an autonomous Ottoman province.

periods.label: British occupation / veiled protectorate
periods.authority: Egyptian postal administration under British control
periods.note: Stamps continue to be inscribed as Egyptian. POSTES EGYPTIENNES designs 1879-1902. British occupation did not produce a separate stamp-issuing authority for civil mail.

periods.label: British Protectorate / Sultanate
periods.authority: Egyptian Government Posts under formal British protectorate

periods.label: Kingdom of Egypt
periods.authority: Egyptian Government / Royaume d'Égypte
periods.note: Nominal independence 1922; full independence 1936. Fuad I, then Farouk.

periods.label: Republic
periods.authority: Republic of Egypt
periods.note: Monarchy dissolved. Transitional issues show King Farouk's portrait obliterated with bars — a distinctive and well-known Egyptian series.

periods.label: United Arab Republic
periods.authority: United Arab Republic (union of Egypt and Syria, 1958-1961; Egypt retained the UAR name to 1971)
periods.note: Inscribed U.A.R. Syria issued separately under the same name during 1958-61 and is catalogued under Syria.

periods.label: Arab Republic of Egypt
periods.authority: Egypt Post (National Postal Organization)

predecessors: Ottoman Empire (foreign post offices operated in Egypt before 1866)
inscriptions: Arabic script country name (on the 1866 first issue, applied by overprint)
POSTE KHEDEVIE EGIZIANE (Italian form, early issues)
gaps: None — Egypt has issued continuously since 1866. Note that pre-1866 mail from Egypt was handled by foreign post offices (British, French, Italian, Austrian, Greek, Russian) operating in Alexandria and elsewhere, and by the private Posta Europea; that material is catalogued under those foreign administrations, not under Egypt.
forgery_risk: Substantial in specific areas. The 1866 first issue and the 1867 second issue are forged, and the Genoa printings attract altered and cleaned examples. Egypt's overprinted issues are the main risk zone: the 1948-1967 Palestine/Gaza overprints (Egyptian stamps overprinted PALESTINE in Arabic and English) are widely faked, as are the various official and military overprints. Interpostal seals — a distinctively Egyptian category — were extensively reprinted and forged, and this is a notorious minefield. The Farouk obliteration bars of 1953 have been privately applied to unbarred stamps.
wallpaper_risk: Egypt does NOT have an agency-driven or excessive-issue reputation and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Output has been consistently moderate and genuinely postal throughout its history. Egypt is one of the more conservative issuers in the Middle East and North Africa, which is notable given how many of its regional neighbours are not.
uncertain: The total count is an estimate and has not been verified against a physical catalogue; Balian and Scott number Egypt differently, particularly for officials and interpostal seals. The exact status and validity dates of some early British occupation-period material are contested. Early Sudan material (from 1897) consists of overprints on Egyptian stamps but is catalogued under Sudan. The 1958-1971 UAR period is a specific trap: Syrian UAR issues are inscribed identically but catalogued under Syria.

count_source: Combined estimate: 163 stamps plus 4 miniature sheets for 1918-1940 (Estonian Philatelic sources / Eesti Postmargid), plus roughly 850-900 major numbers for 1991 to the mid-2020s per Michel Nordeuropa. No single catalogue covers both eras under one continuous numbering.
summary: Estonia's first stamps were issued 22-30 November 1918 - a flower-ornament design of 5 and 15 kopecks inscribed 'Eesti Post', printed by the Bolau press at Nomme. Issuing ran to 1940, stopped for fifty-one years under Soviet and German rule, and resumed in October 1991 at the restoration of independence. That 1940-1991 blank is the defining feature of the country: material from those years is catalogued under the USSR and under German occupation headings, not under Estonia, which makes a country-keyed search badly misleading.

periods.label: Imperial Russian post (catalogued under Russia)
periods.authority: Imperial Russian postal administration
periods.note: Russian stamps used throughout Estland and northern Livonia. Nothing catalogued under Estonia.

periods.label: German occupation of WWI
periods.authority: German military administration (Postgebiet Ob. Ost / Ober Ost)
periods.note: Catalogued separately as German occupation issues, not under Estonia.

periods.label: Republic of Estonia (first independence)
periods.authority: Republic of Estonia / Eesti Post
periods.note: First stamps 22-30 November 1918. Roughly 163 stamps and 4 blocks to 1940.

periods.label: Soviet annexation
periods.authority: USSR (Estonian SSR); stamps of the USSR used
periods.note: A short series of Soviet overprints/local issues exists at the transition; the bulk is plain USSR material.

periods.label: German occupation of WWII
periods.authority: German military and civil administration (Ostland); plus local town issues
periods.note: Eesti/Estland overprints and local issues at Parnu (Pernau), Elva (Elwa), Otepaa (Odenpah), Tartu. Catalogued under German occupation, and this is the most dangerous corner of the area for forgeries.

periods.label: Soviet Estonia
periods.authority: USSR; stamps of the USSR used throughout
periods.note: No Estonian issues whatsoever. Everything is catalogued under Russia/USSR.

periods.label: Restored Republic of Estonia
periods.authority: Republic of Estonia / Eesti Post (Omniva)
periods.note: Issuing resumed October 1991.

inscriptions: EESTI POST - the 1918 first issues and much early material
EESTI - the standard and most common form, first and second independence alike
EESTI VABARIIK (Republic of Estonia) on some issues
ESTLAND - German-language form appearing on WWII German occupation overprints, not on Estonian republican issues
Russian Cyrillic on pre-1918 material and CCCP / СССР on 1940-41 and 1944-91 Soviet-era stamps used in Estonia
Script note: Estonian issues are always Latin script. A Cyrillic inscription on a stamp used in Estonia means Russian or Soviet issue, not Estonian.
gaps: One very large gap: 1941-1991 with no Estonian republican issues at all (fifty years), caused by Soviet annexation, then German occupation, then re-annexation by the USSR. Within that, 1941-44 German occupation and local town issues exist but are catalogued as German occupation, not Estonia. A smaller effective gap in 1940 as the republic's programme terminated on annexation.
forgery_risk: Substantial and concentrated. The 1941-44 German-occupation local town issues - Parnu/Pernau, Elva/Elwa, Otepaa/Odenpah, Tartu - are heavily forged, and a significant fraction of what circulates was privately produced or made to order at the time; unused examples with clean gum should be treated sceptically without a certificate. The 1918-19 first issues and the imperforate/perforation varieties attract faked perforations and faked cancels, and genuine period cancels are scarce. Fantasy overprints purporting to be 1941 Estonian liberation issues also circulate.
wallpaper_risk: None. Estonia is NOT an agency issuer, has no IGPC or Stamperija involvement, and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Post-1991 output is small - typically around 20-30 stamps a year - and postally grounded. Reputation is clean.
uncertain: DISCREPANCY: the supplied file batch_14.txt lists United Kingdom, Iran, Serbia and Syria, not the countries named in my instructions; I profiled the instructed countries and have no records-held figure for Estonia. The 1918-40 count of 163 stamps plus 4 blocks comes from a single source and I did not corroborate it independently. The post-1991 portion of the total is an estimate from issuing rate, not a verified catalogue high number, so the overall figure is soft. Sources give the first issue variously as 22 November, 24 November and 30 November 1918 for the 5 and 15 kopeck values; I could not resolve which value appeared on which date. I could not establish how completely the 1940-41 Soviet transitional overprints are recognised across catalogues. Myth to flag: Estonia is often described as having 'issued stamps under Soviet rule as the Estonian SSR' - it did not; no stamps were issued under the Estonian SSR name and all 1944-91 material is plain USSR.

summary: Ethiopia's stamps originate in a concession Menelik II granted to Swiss engineer Alfred Ilg on 9 March 1894; seven values in two designs (Menelik in profile, and the Lion of Judah) were engraved by Louis-Eugène Mouchon and printed in Paris, reaching Ethiopia in January 1895. Ethiopia is one of only two African states never colonised in the classical sense, so its stamps are a continuous indigenous series rather than a colonial-to-independence sequence — broken only by the Italian occupation of 1936-1941. Its early overprinted issues are among the most heavily forged material in world philately.

periods.label: Empire of Ethiopia (Menelik II onward)
periods.authority: Ethiopian Imperial posts under Menelik II, Zauditu, Haile Selassie
periods.note: Concession granted 9 March 1894; first stamps in country January 1895. 1909 issue adds Latin POSTES ETHIOPIENNES alongside Amharic, values in guerches.

periods.label: Italian occupation / Italian East Africa
periods.authority: Kingdom of Italy — Africa Orientale Italiana
periods.note: May 1936: seven Italian colonial stamps inscribed ETIOPIA showing Victor Emmanuel III, in Italian, Arabic and Amharic, dated 9 May 1936. Ethiopia then absorbed into Italian East Africa with Eritrea and Italian Somaliland.

periods.label: Restored Empire
periods.authority: Ethiopian Empire postal administration under Haile Selassie
periods.note: Issuing resumed after liberation in 1941.

periods.label: Derg / Provisional Military Government (Socialist Ethiopia)
periods.authority: Provisional Military Administrative Council / People's Democratic Republic of Ethiopia
periods.note: Imperial iconography replaced by socialist themes; Haile Selassie removed from designs.

periods.label: Federal Democratic Republic of Ethiopia
periods.authority: Ethiopian Postal Service Enterprise
periods.note: Eritrea's separation in 1993 ends Ethiopian franking there.

predecessors: Great Britain, France and India (foreign post offices and consular mail used pre-1894)
French Post Offices in Ethiopia / Obock–Djibouti routes (catalogued separately)
inscriptions: Amharic script (Ge'ez alphabet) — the primary and continuous inscription from 1894
Provincial names in Amharic on the 1g issues: Shoa, Harrar, Kaffa, Tigre
ABYSSINIE / ABYSSINIA (older catalogue and some early usage)
ETIOPIA (Italian occupation, 1936, with Arabic and Amharic)
Values in guerches, later in cents/dollars (Birr)
gaps: 1936-1941: no Ethiopian national issues during the Italian occupation — the territory was served by Italian ETIOPIA issues and then by Italian East Africa (Africa Orientale Italiana) stamps. That five-year block is catalogued under Italy's colonial listings, not under Ethiopia. Some catalogues also show thin or irregular issuing in the earliest years (1894-1900) where much apparent output is overprint provisionals rather than new designs.
forgery_risk: SEVERE, and among the highest of any world issuer. The named forgers of Ethiopian material are François Fournier, Jean de Sperati and Louis-Henri Mercier. The structural problem is that genuine un-overprinted basic stamps remained cheap and plentiful, so faking the overprint was easy and profitable; consequently a large majority of early overprinted Ethiopian stamps offered for sale today are fakes, and many are hard to distinguish. Doig's Ethiopian Stamp Catalogue is the specialist reference used to sort them. Treat any early Ethiopian overprint as suspect without expertisation.
wallpaper_risk: Ethiopia does NOT have an agency-driven wallpaper reputation. It is not an IGPC or Stamperija volume client and I found no UPU illegal-issue circular naming Ethiopia. Its output is conservative and postally grounded, and modern issuing is sparse rather than excessive. The Ethiopian hazard is forgery, not overproduction — a genuinely different failure mode from the West African agency countries.
count_source: Not established. Doig's Ethiopian Stamp Catalogue is the specialist standard; Scott and Michel both list Ethiopia (Michel under Äthiopien). No verified major-number total obtained.
uncertain: No catalogue total verified, so left at 0 rather than guessed. TRAP: the 1936-41 occupation era is invisible to a country-keyed search on 'Ethiopia' — it sits under Italian East Africa / Africa Orientale Italiana, and some of the 1936 ETIOPIA issues under Italian Colonies. Pre-1894 mail from Ethiopia is catalogued under French Post Offices in Ethiopia / Obock and Djibouti. Also note older catalogues and albums file the country under ABYSSINIA, which defeats an 'Ethiopia' search outright. Not resolved: whether the 1894 or the January 1895 date should be given as the issue date — the stamps are dated and printed 1894 but were not available in Ethiopia until January 1895, and catalogues differ; I have used 1894 to match standard catalogue practice. Exact resumption date of Ethiopian issuing after the 1941 liberation not independently confirmed.

count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (regular postage major numbers) through the 2020s; the Finnish LAPE catalogue counts differently and higher
summary: Finland issued its first stamps on 3 March 1856 as an autonomous Grand Duchy within the Russian Empire — 5 and 10 kopek oval types showing the Finnish lion arms with values in both Roman and Cyrillic. This is the key point for cataloguers: Finland was a stamp-issuing entity for 61 years before independence, and its early issues are catalogued under Finland, not Russia, even though Russia was the sovereign power. Bilingual SUOMI / FINLAND inscriptions begin in 1875. Independence came in December 1917 and issuing has been continuous since. Finland has no wallpaper reputation and is regarded as a conservative, high-quality issuer.

periods.label: Grand Duchy of Finland (autonomous, under Russian Empire)
periods.authority: Finnish Post Office administration of the Grand Duchy, under the Russian crown
periods.note: 1856-60 kopek/ruble denominations; markka/pennia from 1866. Includes the 1891-92 'Russification' ring-type issues where Russian designs were imposed.

periods.label: Independent Finland
periods.authority: Posti- ja lennatinlaitos, later Posti- ja telelaitos, Finland Post, now Posti Group
periods.note: Continuous since independence, including through the Winter War and Continuation War.

periods.label: Karelian and occupation-related issues
periods.authority: Finnish military/occupation administrations and short-lived local authorities
periods.note: Aunus (Olonets) 1919 overprints on Finnish stamps; North Ingermanland 1920; Karelia 1922; East Karelia (Ita-Karjala) military administration overprints 1941-43. All are catalogued as separate entities, not under Finland.

periods.label: Aland Islands
periods.authority: Alands Post (autonomous Aland postal administration)
periods.note: Separate catalogue country from 1984; before that Aland used Finnish stamps.

predecessors: Finland stampless/envelope issues 1845 (postal stationery envelopes, not adhesives)
inscriptions: values in Cyrillic КОП. alongside Roman KOP. (1856-1860s)
gaps: No true gaps in the Finnish issuing record from 1856 to the present. Finland continued issuing throughout the Winter War (1939-40) and Continuation War (1941-44), though programmes were reduced. The related occupation entities (Aunus, North Ingermanland, Karelia, East Karelia) are short-lived by nature rather than gaps in Finland itself.
forgery_risk: Concentrated in three areas: (1) the 1856 first issue, where the 'serpentine roulette' tooth counts and repaired/added teeth drive value enormously and reperforated or faked-tooth examples are common; (2) the 1866-74 serpentine-roulette issues generally; (3) the overprinted occupation entities — Aunus, North Ingermanland, Karelia and East Karelia overprints are extensively forged, and forged cancellations on genuine stamps are the more common problem. Fournier produced Finnish facsimiles. Certificates recommended for anything scarce before 1875 and for any occupation overprint.
wallpaper_risk: None. Finland does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars and has no agency (IGPC/Stamperija) involvement. Its annual programme is small and domestically designed; if anything Finland is cited as an example of restrained, design-led issuing.
uncertain: BATCH LIST CONFLICT: this country appears in the batch file but not in the task message's list. See the note on Switzerland. CATALOGUE TRAP — significant here: Finland 1856-1917 sits under Finland despite Russian sovereignty, but Aunus, North Ingermanland, Karelia and East Karelia are catalogued as separate entities, and Aland from 1984 is a separate country; a country-keyed search on 'Finland' will miss all of them. Some Russian stamps used in Finland with Finnish cancels are catalogued under Russia. MYTH: the 1845 Finnish postal stationery envelopes are sometimes described as 'Finland's first stamps' — they are stationery, not adhesives, and do not displace 1856. I could not establish an exact Scott major-number total from a live catalogue in this session; 1750 is an estimate from published counts and should be verified against a current Scott or LAPE edition. Records held (2532) exceeds my estimate, which suggests the held records include varieties, booklet/coil types, Aland, or the occupation entities.

summary: France issued its first stamps on 1 January 1849, the imperforate Ceres series inscribed REPUB. FRANC., typographed at the Paris mint. The issuing authority tracks French constitutional history closely - Second Republic, Second Empire (Napoleon III head), Third Republic, Vichy State, Provisional Government, Fourth and Fifth Republics - and the designs and inscriptions change with each. France has issued continuously since 1849 with no gap, and its classic period is stable and well documented, but the 1870-71 siege and Bordeaux emergency printings, the two World Wars and the Liberation produced a dense band of provisional and locally-produced material.

periods.label: Second Republic
periods.authority: Administration des Postes, Second French Republic
periods.note: Ceres head, inscribed REPUB. FRANC.

periods.label: Presidency / Second Empire
periods.authority: Imperial French Post under Napoleon III
periods.note: Napoleon III head; inscriptions move from PRESIDE. or REPUB. FRANC. to EMPIRE FRANC. and later EMPIRE FRANCAIS. Perforation introduced 1862.

periods.label: Government of National Defence / Bordeaux emergency
periods.authority: Provisional government at Tours and Bordeaux
periods.note: Ceres design revived; Bordeaux printings authorised 5 November 1870 to 4 March 1871, lithographed by Augee-Delile, to supply unoccupied France while Paris was besieged.

periods.label: Third Republic
periods.authority: Administration des Postes et Telegraphes, later PTT
periods.note: Ceres, Sage 'Peace and Commerce', Merson, Semeuse, Blanc, Mouchon types. Inscription settles on REPUBLIQUE FRANCAISE / RF.

periods.label: Vichy State and German occupation
periods.authority: Etat Francais (Vichy) for the unoccupied and occupied zones; German military authorities for specific areas
periods.note: Petain definitives; inscription POSTES FRANCAISE / ETAT FRANCAIS on some issues. German field-post and local occupation issues exist for Alsace-Lorraine, Dunkirk, and the Atlantic pockets.

periods.label: Liberation and Provisional Government
periods.authority: Gouvernement Provisoire de la Republique Francaise; also Allied Military Government
periods.note: Marianne d'Alger, Coq d'Alger, Arc de Triomphe (Allied-printed) series. Large numbers of unofficial local Liberation overprints.

periods.label: Fourth and Fifth Republics
periods.authority: PTT, then La Poste / Phil@poste
periods.note: REPUBLIQUE FRANCAISE throughout; euro currency from 2002.

predecessors: Pre-stamp French royal and republican post (no adhesives before 1849)
inscriptions: Script note: Latin throughout, no script change. Currency: centimes/francs to 2001, euro from 2002 - a reliable dating cue.
gaps: None. France has issued in every year since 1849. The nearest thing to a gap is 1870-71, when normal Paris production was interrupted by the siege and supply was maintained by the Bordeaux lithographed printings and by re-use of earlier stock; and 1940-44, when issues continued but under Vichy and, in some territories, German authority.
forgery_risk: Classic France is a major forgery field. The 1849-50 Ceres values (especially the 1f vermilion and the 15c green), the 1849 tete-beche pairs, and the 1863 5f Napoleon are all extensively faked, as are the reprints and the Bordeaux issue where lithographic transfer varieties are imitated. Regummed and reperforated classics are endemic. Fake cancellations - especially Paris star and dated cancels applied to fill the gap between mint and used values, and pigeon-post / ballon monte covers - are a persistent problem. Most dangerous of all are the 1944-45 LIBERATION overprints: a large proportion of these were privately made, and many so-called Liberation towns are outright fantasies with no postal reality. The German occupation local issues (Dunkirk, Ile de Re, Lorient, Saint-Nazaire) are also heavily forged. Colonial 'France Libre' and 'FRANCE LIBRE' overprints attract fakes.
wallpaper_risk: No. France is not an agency-driven issuer and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Output has grown considerably in the modern era, with numerous booklets, self-adhesives, personalised stamps (MonTimbraMoi) and collector-oriented souvenir sheets, and some collectors regard the post-2000 programme as heavy - but it is genuine La Poste product with real postal validity, not agency wallpaper.
count_source: Approximate major numbers, Yvert et Tellier Tome 1 (France), ordinary postage plus back-of-book (airmail, postage due, precancels, military)
uncertain: The total is an estimate; Yvert, Scott, Stanley Gibbons and Michel differ by well over 10 percent because of divergent treatment of precancels (preoblitere), self-adhesives, personalised issues and booklet panes, and because Yvert's ordinary-postage series runs separately from its several back-of-book series so a single headline figure has to be assembled. CATALOGUE TRAP: a country-keyed search on 'France' will miss very large adjacent bodies of material. French Colonies general issues (1859-1906, inscribed only COLONIES POSTES with no colony name) are catalogued as their own entity; the Free French and Colonial Liberation issues, the French Offices Abroad (China, Crete, Egypt, Morocco, Turkish Empire, Zanzibar), the Occupation of Germany and Austria issues, the Sarre/Saar and Memel administrations, and Andorra (French Post) and Monaco all sit outside 'France'. Alsace-Lorraine 1870-71 is catalogued under German Occupation, not France.

summary: There is no single 'Germany' issuer before 1872. The first stamps on German soil were Bavaria's 1 Kreuzer black of 1 November 1849; roughly seventeen German States issued independently until unification, and Bavaria and Württemberg kept their own postal sovereignty until 31 March 1920. The Deutsche Reichspost issued the first genuinely national stamps on 1 January 1872, after the transitional Norddeutscher Postbezirk (1868-71). From 1945 the area fragments again into Allied zonal, Berlin, DDR and Bundesrepublik issuers before reunifying under Deutsche Post / Deutsche Post AG in 1990-91.

periods.label: German States (Altdeutschland)
periods.authority: Bavaria, Prussia, Saxony, Hanover, Baden, Württemberg, Bremen, Hamburg, Lübeck, Oldenburg, Brunswick, Mecklenburg-Schwerin and -Strelitz, Schleswig-Holstein, Bergedorf, Heligoland, Thurn und Taxis
periods.note: Catalogued as separate countries, NOT under Germany. Bavaria first (1.11.1849), Prussia/Saxony/Hanover/Schleswig-Holstein 1850.

periods.label: North German Postal District
periods.authority: Norddeutscher Postbezirk
periods.note: Absorbed most northern states' postal services; own catalogue section.

periods.label: German Empire
periods.authority: Deutsche Reichspost
periods.note: First national issue 1.1.1872 (eagle with small shield). Bavaria and Württemberg continued separately to 1920.

periods.label: Weimar Republic
periods.authority: Deutsche Reichspost
periods.note: Includes the 1922-23 hyperinflation issues to 50 Milliarden Mark.

periods.label: Third Reich
periods.authority: Deutsche Reichspost
periods.note: Plus a large family of occupied-territory and Feldpost issues catalogued separately.

periods.label: Allied occupation
periods.authority: AMG / Allied Control Council; American-British Zone (Bizone), French Zone (Baden, Rheinland-Pfalz, Württemberg-Hohenzollern), Soviet Zone (Berlin-Brandenburg, Mecklenburg, Ost-Sachsen, Sachsen, Thüringen, West-Sachsen)
periods.note: Also hundreds of 1945-46 local 'Lokalausgaben' (Gross-Raeschen, Loebau, Fredersdorf etc.), many speculative.

periods.label: Federal Republic
periods.authority: Deutsche Bundespost, then Deutsche Post AG from 1995
periods.note: Sole issuer for all Germany from 1991.

periods.label: West Berlin
periods.authority: Deutsche Post Berlin / Deutsche Bundespost Berlin
periods.note: Separate catalogue country; last issues 1990, validity ceased end 1991.

periods.label: East Germany
periods.authority: Deutsche Post der DDR
periods.note: Ceased 2 October 1990; DDR stamps valid until 31.12.1991.

periods.label: Saar
periods.authority: Saargebiet, Saarpost, Saarland
periods.note: Separate catalogue country in two spells (1920-35, 1947-59).

gaps: No true nationwide gap. The only near-blank interval is spring-summer 1945, when Reich issues ceased and Allied/local emergency issues had not yet appeared; postal service in some areas ran a few weeks on unfranked or hand-stamped mail. Bavaria/Württemberg have no issues after 31.3.1920.
forgery_risk: Very high in several areas: classic German States (Baden 9 Kreuzer error, Saxony 3 Pfennig red, Bergedorf, Heligoland reprints vs originals); Third Reich rarities and the 1945 Hitler-head and Feldpost overprints; almost the entire 1945-46 German local issues field, where speculative and outright bogus overprints outnumber genuine postal use; the 'Deutsches Reich' occupation overprints (Kurland, Lubliana, Zante, Alderney/Guernsey fantasies); postwar Soviet Zone overprints; and forged Bundespost/DDR perforation and watermark varieties. Also widespread propaganda forgeries (British and German wartime), which are collectable but often sold as genuine.
wallpaper_risk: No. Germany in all its incarnations is a genuine postal issuer with no agency involvement and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. DDR output was high and heavily CTO-marketed through Philatelie der DDR, and some Bundespost-era 'Berlin' material was collector-driven, but neither is wallpaper in the IGPC/Stamperija sense.
count_source: Michel Deutschland-Spezial, summed across Reich, Bundesrepublik, Berlin, DDR and zonal issues (excludes German States, which Michel catalogues separately in Altdeutschland). Deutsches Reich alone runs to roughly 910 major numbers, Bundesrepublik to roughly 3,600, DDR roughly 3,400, Berlin roughly 890.
uncertain: Whether to date Germany from 1849 (Bavaria, first stamps on German territory) or 1872 (first Reich issue) is a cataloguing choice, not a fact — Scott and Michel both begin 'Germany' at 1872 and put 1849-71 under German States. A country-keyed search on 'Germany' therefore silently hides the entire 1849-1871 era, plus Saar, Danzig, Memel, Berlin and DDR, all of which are separate catalogue countries. Two persistent myths worth flagging: (1) the 1923 inflation stamps are often called 'worthless paper' — the high-value Milliarden stamps on genuine commercial cover are scarce and valuable, only the off-cover mint singles are common; (2) the 1945 German local issues are frequently sold as an official transitional postal system — most were locally authorised at best and a large share were produced for collectors. The 8,000 figure is a summed estimate and catalogues disagree by well over 20 percent depending on which sub-areas are counted.

summary: Ghana's philatelic record starts as Gold Coast, whose first stamps — Queen Victoria issues — appeared 1 July 1875, with UPU membership following in January 1879. Gold Coast stamps served until independence, when Ghana's first stamps were issued 6 March 1957, the day Nkrumah proclaimed independence: four values (2d, 2½d, 4d, 1s3d) showing an egret over a map of Africa with Nkrumah's portrait. Ghana is philatelically significant as the Inter-Governmental Philatelic Corporation's very first country client, which shaped its later high-volume output.

periods.label: Gold Coast (British colony)
periods.authority: British Gold Coast colonial post office
periods.note: First stamps 1 July 1875; joined UPU January 1879.

periods.label: Ghana (Dominion)
periods.authority: Ghana Post / Posts and Telecommunications
periods.note: First issue 6 March 1957.

periods.label: Republic of Ghana
periods.authority: Ghana Post Company Ltd and predecessors
periods.note: Republic declared 1 July 1960. Nkrumah-era issues give way to varied post-1966 regimes; agency-produced output grows heavily from the 1980s-90s.

gaps: No documented gap in stamp availability from 1875 to the present. Gold Coast issues covered 1875-1957 continuously and Ghana has issued since. Modern issuing is uneven in genuine postal use rather than absent.
forgery_risk: Classic Gold Coast Queen Victoria issues, particularly the 1875-1889 high values and the 1885 provisional surcharges, are the traditional risk area for faked surcharges and repairs. The larger modern hazard is not forgery of Ghana stamps but the volume of agency-produced and unauthorised-looking material carrying Ghana's name — souvenir sheets and 'issues' of doubtful postal validity circulate widely, and buyers should treat unlisted modern sheetlets with caution.
wallpaper_risk: YES — Ghana has a genuine agency/wallpaper reputation and this should be stated plainly. Ghana, newly independent in 1957, was IGPC's first country client, appointing the agency to handle distribution and postal administration. From the 1980s onward Ghana's output became heavily agency-driven, dominated by thematically targeted sets and souvenir sheets (Disney, film stars, dinosaurs, sports) aimed at collectors rather than postal need. Ghana was not found on the UPU illegal-issue circular list of denounced African issuers in the sources checked — its material is generally agency-authorised rather than illegal — but the excessive-issue criticism is well established.
count_source: Not established. Stanley Gibbons Commonwealth: West Africa is the standard reference for Gold Coast and Ghana; Scott lists Gold Coast and Ghana as separate countries. No verified major-number total obtained.
uncertain: No catalogue total verified, so left at 0 rather than guessed — and Ghana is a case where catalogues diverge much more than the usual ±20%, because some decline to list portions of the agency-produced modern output at all. TRAP: a country-keyed search on 'Ghana' silently loses 1875-1957, which is catalogued entirely under Gold Coast. Not verified: whether IGPC's Ghana relationship has been continuous since 1957 or lapsed and resumed; whether any Ghana material has ever appeared on a UPU circular (absence in my sources is not proof of absence).

summary: Austrian K.u.K. stamps served Hungary until 31 July 1871; Hungary's own first stamps, lithographed Franz Joseph heads from the State Press of Buda, appeared 1 May 1871, followed within weeks by engraved copperplate versions - the lithographed 'Spotty Joes' are the classic Hungarian rarity. The 1871-72 issues carry no country name at all, the single most common identification trap; the first Hungarian-language inscription, MAGYAR KIR POSTA, appears on the 1874 series. The post-1918 collapse produced a dense cluster of short-lived regimes plus ten or more occupation issuers, and Hungary's inscriptions track its regime changes closely.

periods.label: Austrian stamps used in Hungary
periods.authority: K.u.K. Austrian post
periods.note: Catalogued under Austria; Hungarian usage identified only by cancellation.

periods.label: Kingdom of Hungary under the Dual Monarchy
periods.authority: Magyar Kiralyi Posta (Hungarian Royal Post), from the 1867 Compromise
periods.note: First issue 1 May 1871 anonymous; MAGYAR KIR POSTA from 1874.

periods.label: Hungarian People's Republic (Karolyi republic)
periods.authority: Provisional republican post
periods.note: KOZTARSASAG (Republic) overprints on royal issues.

periods.label: Hungarian Soviet Republic
periods.authority: Magyar Tanacskoztarsasag postal administration
periods.note: Under five months. 21 July 1919 the Harvesters and Parliament values overprinted MAGYAR TANACSKOZTARSASAG.

periods.label: Occupation issues
periods.authority: France (Arad), Romania (Debrecen I and II, Transylvania I and II), Serbia (Baranya I and II, Temesvar, Banat-Bacska)
periods.note: Ten-plus distinct series, nearly all overprints on Hungarian 1913-14 stamps; the second Debrecen issue is the only one with purpose-designed stamps. Listed under Hungary as N-numbers in Scott but also cross-referenced under France/Romania/Serbia occupation headings - a real search trap.

periods.label: Kingdom of Hungary (Regency)
periods.authority: Magyar Kiralyi Posta under Regent Horthy
periods.note: Trianon era; MAGYAR KIR POSTA, later MAGYAR POSTA. Includes 1938-41 territorial re-annexation issues.

periods.label: Post-war republic and People's Republic
periods.authority: Magyar Posta
periods.note: Second Republic 1946-49, then Hungarian People's Republic. Hyperinflation issues 1945-46 with pengo, milpengo and adopengo denominations.

periods.label: Republic of Hungary / Hungary
periods.authority: Magyar Posta Zrt.
periods.note: MAGYARORSZAG appears on modern issues alongside MAGYAR POSTA.

successors: Czechoslovakia (Upper Hungary/Slovakia, 1918-19 overprints on Hungarian stamps)
Yugoslavia / Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes (Banat, Bacska, Baranya)
Western Ukraine (overprints on Hungarian stamps in Transcarpathia)
inscriptions: (NO inscription at all on the 1871-72 first issues - Franz Joseph head only)
HUNGARY (occasionally added on modern international issues)
Script note: Latin throughout, but Hungarian diacritics (long o and u with double acute) are routinely dropped in database transcription and defeat exact-match searching.
gaps: No complete year without issues in the modern era. The nearest thing to a gap is 1919-20, when central authority collapsed and Hungarian output is fragmented across the Soviet Republic, the republic, and eight-plus occupation issuers rather than absent. 1944-45 (German occupation, Arrow Cross, Soviet advance) saw postal chaos and very restricted issuing, and 1945-46 hyperinflation produced a flood of surcharges rather than a gap. Pre-1871 is not a gap but an Austria-catalogued era.
forgery_risk: High and well documented. The 1919 occupation overprints are the worst area: forged Debrecen, Temesvar, Arad, Baranya and Banat-Bacska overprints are abundant, and expertising marks are themselves compromised - 'PAPE' is the most notorious forger, who applied his own handstamps to his products. Also forged: the 1871 lithographed first issue and its plate varieties, 1919 Soviet Republic overprints, WWII territorial overprints, and inverted/double surcharges across the inflation period. Expert certificates are essential for anything scarce from 1919-20.
wallpaper_risk: Not an agency country and not on UPU illegal-issue circulars - no IGPC or Stamperija involvement. However Hungary has a genuine, long-standing reputation for collector-directed excess of its own making: very large imperforate and miniature-sheet output, extensive souvenir sheets in the 1950s-1970s aimed at hard-currency export, and heavy CTO (cancelled-to-order) stock, which is why Hungarian used material is abnormally cheap. Stated plainly: the CTO/excess reputation is real; the agency-wallpaper reputation is not.
count_source: Approximation of Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue major numbers for Hungary (regular postage, excluding occupation N-numbers, airmails, semi-postals and postage dues, which add substantially). Michel Europa runs higher. +/- 20%.
uncertain: Total not verified against a specific catalogue edition and is unusually sensitive to whether imperforates, miniature sheets, occupation N-numbers and back-of-the-book are counted - catalogues here differ by well over 20%. Myths to flag: (1) the 1871 first issue is often said to be inscribed 'Magyar Kir Posta' - it carries no inscription at all, which is why it is habitually misfiled as Austrian; (2) the lithographed and engraved 1871 printings are commonly confused, and 'Spotty Joe' is applied loosely to both when it properly describes the lithographed printing; (3) the Temesvar surcharges are attributed to the Serbian occupation by most catalogues, but Hungarian sources place them between the Serbian and Romanian occupations - catalogue attribution and historical attribution disagree.

summary: The first adhesive used in India — and in Asia — was the circular Scinde District Dawk of 1 July 1852, a half-anna embossed local issue authorised by Bartle Frere for the East India Company's Sind province. Nationwide issues followed in October 1854 under the East India Company, inscribed EAST INDIA POSTAGE because India was not yet a Crown colony. British India ran continuously to 1947, then the Dominion and Republic of India, still issuing. The great cataloguing trap is that huge tracts of Indian material — the six Convention States and the several dozen Feudatory States — are listed separately and will not surface under a country-keyed search for 'India'.

periods.label: Scinde District Dawk
periods.authority: East India Company, Sind province (Bartle Frere)
periods.note: Circular embossed half-anna in blue, white and red wax. Suppressed October 1854.

periods.label: East India Company
periods.authority: Honourable East India Company
periods.note: Lithographed and later De La Rue Queen Victoria heads inscribed EAST INDIA POSTAGE.

periods.label: British India (Crown)
periods.authority: Government of India, British Crown administration
periods.note: Victoria, Edward VII, George V, George VI. Also the source of stamps used across Aden, the Persian Gulf, Burma before 1937, and Indian Expeditionary Force material.

periods.label: Convention States
periods.authority: Chamba, Faridkot, Gwalior, Jind, Nabha, Patiala — British India stamps overprinted with the state name
periods.note: Catalogued separately from India proper.

periods.label: Feudatory / Native States
periods.authority: Individual princely states running their own posts (Bhopal, Bundi, Cochin, Hyderabad, Jaipur, Jammu & Kashmir, Travancore and many more)
periods.note: Jammu & Kashmir issues begin 1866; the earliest state issues predate 1870. All catalogued as Indian States, not India.

periods.label: Dominion of India
periods.authority: Dominion of India
periods.note: First independent issue 21 November 1947.

periods.label: Republic of India
periods.authority: India Post, Department of Posts
periods.note: Still issuing.

inscriptions: भारत (Bharat, Devanagari — from 1947 onward, usually paired with INDIA)
CHAMBA STATE / FARIDKOT STATE / GWALIOR / JIND STATE / NABHA STATE / PATIALA STATE (Convention overprints)
gaps: No gaps in India proper since 1854; issuing has been continuous through both World Wars and 1947. The Scinde Dawk period does end abruptly — the circular stamps were officially suppressed in October 1854 as the all-India issue took over.
forgery_risk: The Scinde Dawk is extensively forged, particularly the red wax variety, which is fragile and rarely survives intact — most 'red' examples offered are fakes or reconstructions. The 1854 lithographed half-anna and four-anna heads are forged, and the four-anna is also faked as inverted-head errors. The Indian Feudatory States are one of the classic forgery minefields in world philately: Jammu & Kashmir, Bhopal, Bundi and Hyderabad have all been reprinted, forged and faked in quantity, and Kashmir in particular has forgeries dating from the 19th century that are now themselves collected. Certificates are standard for anything significant in these areas.
wallpaper_risk: No. India does NOT have an agency or wallpaper reputation. India Post produces its own stamps, the programme is domestically driven, and India does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Annual output has grown but remains within normal bounds.
count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue — approximate major numbers for British India plus Republic of India across all categories through the mid-2020s. This count EXCLUDES the Convention and Feudatory States, which run to several thousand further major numbers and are catalogued in Stanley Gibbons' separate 'India including Convention and Feudatory States' volume.
uncertain: CATALOGUING TRAP: the Scinde Dawk is listed by Stanley Gibbons and Scott under India, but Sind is in present-day Pakistan and Pakistani philately also claims it as 'the first stamp of Asia' and its own antecedent. A country-keyed search on either name can therefore return or miss it. Second trap: the Convention and Feudatory States are catalogued as 'Indian States', so an entire century of Indian material is invisible under the key 'India'. Third: British India stamps were the everyday postage of Aden, the Persian Gulf states, Burma and Zanzibar before those had issues of their own, and that material is catalogued under those names, not India. I could not establish a firm total count — Scott, Gibbons and the Indian national catalogues diverge substantially, and the state issues make any single figure arbitrary.

summary: The territory's first stamps were Netherlands Indies issues, the first appearing 1 April 1864 - a 10-cent imperforate showing King William III, printed in Utrecht. Indonesia as an issuing authority in its own name begins with the revolutionary period from 1945: Dutch Indies and Japanese-occupation stocks overprinted REPOEBLIK INDONESIA in Java and Sumatra, followed by the 1948 'Vienna' printings and the 1949-50 Republik Indonesia Serikat issues, with the unitary Republic issuing continuously from 1950. Indonesia is unusually fragmented across catalogues, and its early independence era was ignored by Scott for roughly sixty years, so a country-keyed search will badly under-represent 1945-49.

periods.label: Netherlands Indies
periods.authority: Dutch colonial postal administration (Nederlandsch-Indie)
periods.note: Catalogued as Netherlands Indies / Dutch East Indies, NOT as Indonesia.

periods.label: Japanese occupation
periods.authority: Imperial Japanese military administration (Java, Sumatra, Navy area including Borneo and the eastern islands)
periods.note: Mostly overprints and surcharges on Netherlands Indies stock, plus locally designed issues with Indonesian cultural motifs. Sumatra, Java and the Navy-administered areas ran separate postal regimes. The Riau Islands were administered with Malaya for the duration.

periods.label: Revolutionary Republic (interim issues)
periods.authority: Repoeblik Indonesia postal service, Java and Sumatra separately
periods.note: Overprints reading REPOEBLIK INDONESIA on Netherlands Indies and Japanese-occupation stamps; Java and Sumatra issued separately because Dutch-held territory divided them. The 1948 Vienna (Staatsdruckerei) and E. W. Wright, Philadelphia printings inscribed REPOEBLIK INDONESIA largely failed to reach Indonesia through the Dutch blockade.

periods.label: Netherlands Indies / Indonesia under Dutch authority
periods.authority: Dutch colonial administration, parallel to the Republic
periods.note: Dutch-controlled areas continued Netherlands Indies issues, later inscribed INDONESIA under the federal arrangement.

periods.label: Republik Indonesia Serikat (United States of Indonesia)
periods.authority: Federal Indonesian government
periods.note: RIS, RIS Merdeka, RIS Djakarta and similar commemorative overprints.

periods.label: Republic of Indonesia (unitary)
periods.authority: Pos Indonesia (formerly PTT / Perum Pos dan Giro)
periods.note: Inscription REPUBLIK INDONESIA / INDONESIA.

periods.label: West New Guinea transfer
periods.authority: UNTEA (UN Temporary Executive Authority), then Indonesia
periods.note: Netherlands New Guinea stamps overprinted UNTEA, then IRIAN BARAT / Irian Barat issues before full integration.

predecessors: Japanese Occupation of the Netherlands Indies
successors: East Timor / Timor-Leste (independent issuer from 2002; Indonesian issues used there 1976-1999)
inscriptions: NEDERLANDSCH INDIE / NED. INDIE / NEDERL. INDIE (colonial era)
REPOEBLIK INDONESIA (1945-48 overprints and Vienna printings - old Dutch-influenced spelling with OE)
REPUBLIK INDONESIA (from the second Vienna issue and standard thereafter)
Japanese occupation: Japanese characters, DAI NIPPON, and 'Dai Nippon 2602 Sumatra' style overprints

Script note: Latin throughout for Indonesian issues, with Japanese kanji/kana on occupation issues. The OE-to-U spelling reform (Repoeblik to Republik, and later the 1972 EYD reform) is a reliable dating cue.
gaps: No true gap in stamps available for use, but there is no single continuous issuing authority. 1942-45 the Republic did not exist and the Japanese administrations issued in three separate zones. 1945-49 the Republic and the Dutch administration issued in parallel over different parts of the archipelago, so 'Indonesia' in that window is not one series but several. Some individual Japanese-occupation zones and some Republic regions have years with no new issues, relying on overprinted stock.
forgery_risk: High for the transitional periods. The Japanese-occupation overprints on Netherlands Indies stamps are extensively forged - genuine ones require expertisation, and whole categories offered as occupation issues are private products. The 1945-49 REPOEBLIK INDONESIA overprints are among the most heavily faked material in Asian philately, partly because they were locally and crudely applied to begin with, which makes discrimination hard; many interim 'regional' issues circulate in forged or entirely invented form. The 1948 Vienna printings sit ambiguously between issued stamps and cinderellas: they were genuinely printed by the Austrian State Printing Office but were largely blockaded and did not see normal postal use, and reprints and unauthorised remainders exist. Used copies and covers of the revolutionary issues are frequently faked with bogus cancels. RIS and Merdeka overprints are also forged.
wallpaper_risk: Indonesia is not agency-driven and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars as an issuer. Pos Indonesia's programme is a normal national output. Two caveats: the country produces a substantial volume of souvenir sheets and philatelic-market products, and there is a separate body of bogus and fantasy labels made in the name of Indonesian separatist entities (Free Papua / OPM, Republik Maluku Selatan / South Moluccas, Aceh) - the South Moluccas labels in particular are a long-standing and prolific fantasy series with no postal validity whatsoever.
count_source: Approximate major numbers for the Republic from 1945 onward, Michel Sudostasien and the Dutch Zonnebloem Speciale Catalogus (which covers Netherlands Indies and Indonesia); Netherlands Indies 1864-1948 adds roughly a further 350 majors under its own heading
uncertain: The count is an estimate with a wide margin, and it depends entirely on which catalogue is used because they disagree about what to list at all, not merely how to number it. Scott ignored the 1945-49 revolutionary interim issues and the Vienna printings for roughly sixty years before adding them, so older Scott-derived data sets simply lack that era; Zonnebloem and Michel have listed them far longer and in far greater depth, and the specialised Dutch and Indonesian handbooks recognise hundreds of regional interim varieties that no general catalogue numbers. I could not establish a reliable single figure for the interim period. CATALOGUE TRAP - severe for this country: Netherlands Indies (1864-1948) is catalogued under its own name, the Japanese occupation issues are usually split across Netherlands Indies, separate Japanese Occupation headings and in some catalogues under Malaya for the Riau Islands, Netherlands New Guinea (1950-62) and the UNTEA overprints (1962-63) are their own entities, and Irian Barat may be listed separately from Indonesia. A country-keyed search on 'Indonesia' will silently omit 1864-1949 almost entirely. Also flag a common misconception: the 1948 Vienna printings are widely sold as ordinary issued stamps, but their postal status is genuinely disputed and many specialists treat them as at best semi-official.

summary: Iraq's catalogue run begins with British occupation overprints — provisional surcharges on Ottoman stamps reading BAGHDAD / IN BRITISH / OCCUPATION following the capture of Baghdad in March 1917, then IRAQ / IN BRITISH / OCCUPATION from September 1918. Indian stamps overprinted I.E.F. served the military mail. The occupation issues were invalidated 1 June 1923, and Iraq's first stamps of its own — a 12-value pictorial definitive series — appeared in 1923 under the Kingdom. Issuing has continued through monarchy, republic, Ba'athist rule and the post-2003 state.

periods.label: Ottoman postal administration
periods.authority: Ottoman Empire
periods.note: Turkish stamps used; catalogued under Turkey

periods.label: British occupation / Mesopotamia
periods.authority: British occupying authority; Indian Expeditionary Force
periods.note: Overprints on Ottoman stamps; I.E.F. overprints on India for military mail

periods.label: Kingdom of Iraq under British Mandate
periods.authority: Iraq Post, Kingdom of Iraq
periods.note: First own designs 1923

periods.label: Independent Kingdom of Iraq
periods.authority: Iraq Post, Kingdom of Iraq

periods.label: Republic of Iraq
periods.authority: Republic of Iraq post administration
periods.note: Includes Ba'athist period

periods.label: Post-2003 Republic of Iraq
periods.authority: Iraqi Post / Ministry of Communications
periods.note: Output sparse and irregular

gaps: No formal multi-year gap, but issuing was thin and erratic during the 1991 sanctions period and again immediately after 2003; some years have very few or no new issues. Exact zero-issue years are not established here.
forgery_risk: High for the occupation period. The 1917-18 BAGHDAD IN BRITISH OCCUPATION surcharges were crudely applied and are among the most commonly forged items in Middle Eastern philately; IRAQ IN BRITISH OCCUPATION overprints and Mosul-related provisionals are likewise faked. Later Iraqi material is comparatively safe, though Kurdish-region and post-2003 local labels circulate as unofficial.
wallpaper_risk: No. Iraq has never run an agency-driven programme, has no IGPC/Stamperija connection, and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. If anything it under-issues.
count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (Iraq regular, airmail and official sections; estimate)
uncertain: The total is an estimate. Trap: the 1917-1923 material is catalogued in some references under 'Mesopotamia' or 'Iraq (British Occupation)' rather than Iraq proper, and pre-1917 usage falls under Turkey — a country-keyed search on 'Iraq' therefore starts too late. The precise years with zero issues in the 1990s and mid-2000s were not verified.

count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue, approximate major numbers to mid-2020s. The Hibernian Specialised Catalogue of the Postage Stamps of Ireland is the standard specialist reference and numbers the overprint varieties far more finely.
summary: Ireland's first stamps appeared on 17 February 1922: British King George V definitives overprinted 'Rialtas Sealadach na hEireann 1922' (Provisional Government of Ireland) by Dollard, with further printings by Thom, Harrison and Waterlow. The first stamps actually used in Ireland are the British issues from 1840, all catalogued under Great Britain, which hides eighty years of Irish postal history from a country-keyed search. Ireland has issued continuously since 1922 with no gap. Northern Ireland is not a separate issuer - it uses British stamps, with regional definitives from 1958.

periods.label: British administration (catalogued under Great Britain)
periods.authority: British Post Office
periods.note: Penny Black onward used throughout Ireland. Irish postal history of this era is catalogued under Great Britain, identifiable only by cancellation.

periods.label: Provisional Government overprints
periods.authority: Provisional Government of Ireland (Rialtas Sealadach na hEireann)
periods.note: From 17 February 1922, British KGV definitives overprinted. Four printers - Dollard, Thom, Harrison, Waterlow - each with measurable overprint differences.

periods.label: Irish Free State
periods.authority: Irish Free State / Saorstat Eireann
periods.note: First wholly Irish-designed definitives ('Sword of Light', map, Celtic cross) from December 1922; overprints continue in parallel as SAORSTAT EIREANN 1922.

periods.label: Eire / Republic of Ireland
periods.authority: Eire, then Republic of Ireland; An Post from 1984
periods.note: 1937 constitution renames the state Eire. An Post separated from Telecom Eireann in 1984 and is the issuing authority since.

inscriptions: RIALTAS SEALADACH NA hEIREANN 1922 - the five-line Provisional Government overprint on British KGV stamps
SAORSTAT EIREANN 1922 - Irish Free State overprint
EIRE - the standard inscription from 1922 definitives onward and the single most reliable Irish identifier
EIRE with the value in Irish (pingin, scilling) on classic issues
Post-decimal and euro issues usually inscribed EIRE / IRELAND or just EIRE

Script note: from 1922 to roughly the early 1970s inscriptions were normally set in Gaelic (insular) type with the dotted lenition; later issues use ordinary Latin roman type. The Gaelic typeface is itself a rough dating cue.
gaps: No gap years since 1922. There is a hard authority break in 1922 with nothing catalogued under Ireland before it. Note that British stamps remained valid in the Free State for a transitional period in 1922, so 1922 covers can carry either.
forgery_risk: Concentrated almost entirely in the 1922 overprints, which are among the more systematically forged British-Commonwealth-adjacent material. Forged overprints on genuine GB stamps are common and the standard defences are measurement and printer characteristics: the Dollard overprint measures about 15 x 17 mm, Thom about 14.5 x 16 mm, and in the Harrison overprints the 'i' of 'Rialtas' drops below the baseline of the surrounding letters. Line spacing, ink impression and the width of the '1922' date are the accepted discriminators. Inverted and doubled overprints are the most faked varieties and expertisation is effectively mandatory on high values. Later Irish issues carry very little forgery risk beyond faked cancels on high-value definitives.
wallpaper_risk: None. Ireland is NOT an agency issuer, has no IGPC or Stamperija connection, and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. An Post's programme is modest and postally grounded. The one thing to be aware of is heavy modern personalised/'stamp designer' and commemorative sheetlet output, which is legitimate but of limited philatelic standing.
uncertain: DISCREPANCY: the supplied file batch_14.txt lists United Kingdom, Iran, Serbia and Syria, not the countries named in my instructions; I profiled the countries named in the instructions and have no records-held figure for Ireland. The ~2,400 total is approximate; I did not verify a current Scott high number and Hibernian's specialised numbering is not comparable. Sources differ on the exact composition of the first 17 February 1922 release (eight low and three high values overprinted by Dollard plus four by Thom is one commonly given breakdown, but I could not corroborate it against a second independent source). Myth to flag: Ireland is frequently said to have issued its first stamps 'on independence in 1921' - the Anglo-Irish Treaty is December 1921 but the first stamps are February 1922. A second common error: treating Northern Ireland as a separate stamp-issuing country. It is not; its regional definitives from 1958 are catalogued under Great Britain.

summary: The territory's postal history runs through Ottoman post offices and foreign post offices (Austrian, French, German, Italian, Russian) in the 19th century, then British Egyptian Expeditionary Force and Palestine Mandate issues 1918-1948 - all catalogued under Turkey, the respective foreign powers, or Palestine, not under Israel. Israel's own stamps begin with the Doar Ivri definitives distributed 16 May 1948, the day after independence; because they were designed before the state was named, they carry only 'Doar Ivri' (Hebrew Post) and not the word Israel. A two-week Interim Period (1-15 May 1948) preceded them, using overprinted JNF/KKL labels and local ad hoc issues at Safed, Nahariya and elsewhere.

periods.label: Ottoman period
periods.authority: Ottoman Post, plus foreign post offices in the Levant (Austria, France, Germany, Italy, Russia)
periods.note: Catalogued under Turkey and under each foreign power's 'offices in the Turkish Empire'. Invisible to a country search on Israel.

periods.label: British military occupation
periods.authority: Egyptian Expeditionary Force (EEF)
periods.note: EEF pictorial issues, later overprinted. Catalogued under Palestine.

periods.label: British Mandate for Palestine
periods.authority: Palestine Post (British Mandate administration)
periods.note: Trilingual English/Arabic/Hebrew with the Hebrew abbreviation Aleph-Yod (Eretz Israel). Catalogued under Palestine.

periods.label: Interim Period
periods.authority: Minhelet Ha'am (People's Administration / Provisional Council) and local committees
periods.note: 1-15 May 1948. Overprinted JNF/KKL labels on sale from 2 May, validated by a rubber 'Doar' handstamp; local ad hoc postage at Safed, Nahariya and elsewhere. Mostly listed as locals/semi-official rather than in the main Israel listing.

periods.label: State of Israel
periods.authority: Provisional Government post, then Israel Postal Authority / Israel Post (Doar Israel)
periods.note: Doar Ivri from 16 May 1948; from 1949 stamps inscribed ISRAEL. Ongoing.

predecessors: Austrian / French / German / Italian / Russian offices in the Turkish Empire
successors: Palestinian Authority (from 1994, for areas transferred under Oslo - a parallel issuer, not a successor to Israel)
inscriptions: DOAR IVRI (Hebrew, 1948 first issue - the word Israel does NOT appear)
Arabic form ISRAIL on trilingual issues
DOAR (Hebrew for 'post', the rubber overprint on Interim Period KKL labels)
Predecessor material reads PALESTINE / Arabic Filastin / Hebrew Palestina with the Aleph-Yod abbreviation
gaps: No gap since May 1948 - Israel has issued continuously. The functional gap for a country-keyed search is everything before 1948, sitting under Turkey, the foreign offices, and Palestine. There was a brief interruption in April 1948 when the British discontinued postal services before the Provisional Government took over.
forgery_risk: High in specific areas. The Interim Period is the worst: overprinted KKL/JNF labels, Safed and Nahariya locals, and Interim-period covers and cancellations are extensively forged, and the field is notorious for fabricated covers assembled from genuine components. Doar Ivri stamps with full tabs are faked by reattaching or regumming tabs to tabless copies, and Doar Ivri first-day and early commercial covers are frequently manufactured. Expertisation is effectively mandatory for Interim material and high-value tabbed Doar Ivri.
wallpaper_risk: None. Israel is a disciplined, conservative issuer with a modest annual programme, no agency involvement, and no appearance on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Explicitly NOT a wallpaper country. Separately, unauthorised commemorative-looking labels and JNF/KKL fundraising labels circulate widely and are not postage - an identification trap, not a wallpaper problem.
count_source: Approximation of Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue major numbers for Israel through the mid-2020s; comparable order of magnitude in Bale's Catalog of Israel Postage Stamps (the specialist standard). +/- 20%.
uncertain: Total not verified against a specific catalogue edition and excludes Interim Period locals, which Bale lists but Scott largely does not. Myths to flag: (1) Israel's first stamps are often said to be inscribed 'Israel' - they are not, they read only Doar Ivri, and the state name first appears on later 1948-49 issues; (2) the Interim Period is sometimes described as an official Israeli issuing era, but the KKL/JNF overprinted labels were locally authorised expedients and catalogue treatment varies; (3) tab premiums are frequently misapplied to issues never printed with tabs.

count_source: Estimate from Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (Italy regular postage, semi-postals, airmails, express, parcel post) through the 2020s; the Italian Sassone catalogue counts substantially differently and is the domestic standard
summary: Italy as a catalogue country begins in 1862 with the Kingdom of Italy, but stamps first appeared on the peninsula in 1850 in Lombardy-Venetia (Austrian administration) and from 1 January 1851 in Sardinia, whose issues became the stamps of unified Italy. Sardinian perforated Victor Emmanuel II stamps were issued in 1862, and the first issue actually inscribed POSTE ITALIANE appeared 1 December 1863. Italy's record is complicated by the pre-unification Italian States, by a very large colonial and post-office-abroad network, by the 1943-45 split between the Kingdom in the south and the Italian Social Republic in the north, and by the separate San Marino and Vatican administrations inside Italian territory.

periods.label: Italian States (pre-unification)
periods.authority: Lombardy-Venetia (Austria) 1850; Sardinia 1851; Tuscany 1851; Papal States 1852; Modena 1852; Parma 1852; Naples 1858; Sicily 1859; Romagna 1859
periods.note: All catalogued as separate entities. Sardinia's issues are the direct ancestor of Italy's.

periods.label: Kingdom of Italy
periods.authority: Poste Italiane under the House of Savoy
periods.note: POSTE ITALIANE inscription from 1863. Includes the Fascist period 1922-43 with heavy commemorative output.

periods.note: Overprints G.N.R. and REPUBBLICA SOCIALE ITALIANA and fascio designs on Kingdom stamps, plus own issues. Runs in parallel with Allied-occupied southern Italy.

periods.authority: AMG (Allied Military Government) and the Italian Lieutenancy in the south
periods.note: AMG issues for Sicily and Naples; Luogotenenza issues 1944-46.

periods.authority: Poste Italiane / Poste Italiane S.p.A. (from 1998)
periods.note: REPUBBLICA ITALIANA / ITALIA. Continuous.

periods.authority: Italian colonial and military administrations
periods.note: Eritrea, Somalia, Libya, Italian East Africa, Aegean Islands (Egeo), Italian offices in the Ottoman Empire and China, plus WWII occupations (Albania, Ionian Islands, Montenegro, Ljubljana/Lubiana, Dalmatia). Trieste Zone A (AMG-FTT) 1947-54. All catalogued separately.

successors: Trieste Zone A / AMG-FTT and Zone B (1947-54)
San Marino and Vatican City (separate, coexisting administrations)
inscriptions: REPUBBLICA SOCIALE ITALIANA and G.N.R. (RSI overprints 1943-45)
colonial: ERITREA, SOMALIA ITALIANA, LIBIA, AFRICA ORIENTALE ITALIANA (A.O.I.), TRIPOLITANIA, CIRENAICA, EGEO, ISOLE ITALIANE DELL'EGEO
office-abroad overprints: ESTERO, CONSTANTINOPOLI, TRIPOLI DI BARBERIA, PECHINO, TIENTSIN
AMG-VG and AMG-FTT (Allied Military Government, Venezia Giulia and Free Territory of Trieste)
gaps: No year without Italian stamps since 1862. The 1943-45 period is not a gap but a split: the RSI issued in the north while AMG and Luogotenenza issues served the south, so both must be catalogued in parallel rather than in sequence. Individual Italian States stop issuing at unification (1859-62) rather than leaving gaps.
forgery_risk: Substantial and well documented. The Italian States are the classic danger zone — Sicily (the 1859 Bomba heads, especially the 1/2 grano), Naples, Tuscany, Modena, Parma and the Papal States are all heavily forged, with Sperati and Fournier both active; Sardinia's early issues likewise. The RSI 1943-45 overprints (G.N.R. and fascio) are extensively forged, as are the wartime occupation overprints for Albania, the Ionian Islands, Montenegro and Ljubljana, and outright bogus overprints exist for territories Italy never overprinted. Italian colonial overprints are widely forged. Forged cancellations, especially on Italian States and on scarce used Kingdom high values, are pervasive. Certificates are standard practice for States, RSI and occupation material.
wallpaper_risk: None in the agency sense. Italy has no IGPC or Stamperija involvement and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars.
