Its programme is large — the Fascist era in particular produced heavy commemorative and propaganda output, and modern annual programmes are sizeable — but these are genuine national issues, not agency product.
uncertain: BATCH LIST CONFLICT: this country appears in the task message's list but not in the batch file, which names Finland, Armenia, Switzerland and Malaysia. See the note on Switzerland. FIRST-ISSUE-YEAR JUDGEMENT: I have given 1850 as the year stamps first appeared in the territory now called Italy (Lombardy-Venetia, under Austrian administration). If the catalogue convention is 'first stamps of the entity Italy', the correct answer is 1862 (Sardinian perforated issues used as Italian) or 1863 (first POSTE ITALIANE inscription). The parent should decide which convention applies; I have not silently picked one. MAJOR CATALOGUE TRAP: all pre-1862 material sits under nine separate Italian States headings, and the entire colonial, offices-abroad, occupation and Trieste record sits under dozens of separate headings — a country-keyed search on 'Italy' misses all of it. San Marino (from 1877) and Vatican City (from 1929) are separate countries entirely despite being inside Italy. The RSI is catalogued separately in some catalogues and as an Italy sub-section in others, which is a common source of duplicate or missing records. My total-stamp estimate is not verified against a current edition and Scott and Sassone differ materially in what they grant major numbers to.

count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (Japan); the JSCA / Sakura catalogue is the Japanese national standard and numbers differently
summary: Japan's first stamps were the four imperforate Dragon stamps issued on 20 April 1871, denominated in mon with facing dragons and the value in black. Japan has issued continuously since, through the Meiji, Taisho, Showa and Heisei/Reiwa eras, with no gaps even during the Second World War. The Dragons are among the most heavily forged stamps in world philately — forgeries are said to outnumber genuine examples by roughly ten to one. A major cataloguing trap: Japanese occupation issues for territories across Asia, and the post-war US administration issues for the Ryukyus, are catalogued under those territories, not under Japan.

periods.label: Meiji era
periods.authority: Imperial Japanese Post (Ministry of Posts)
periods.note: First issue 20 April 1871: Dragons, 48m brown, 100m blue, 200m orange, 500m blue-green. Currency changes from mon to sen/yen in 1872.

periods.label: Taisho era
periods.authority: Imperial Japanese Post

periods.label: Showa era — Empire
periods.authority: Imperial Japanese Post / Ministry of Communications
periods.note: Wartime issues include poor-quality paper and gum. Separate Japanese occupation issues for Malaya, Netherlands Indies, Burma, Philippines, North Borneo, Hong Kong and elsewhere are catalogued under those territories.

periods.label: Allied occupation
periods.authority: Japanese postal administration under SCAP supervision
periods.note: Japan continued issuing its own stamps under occupation; there is no separate Allied Military Government stamp issue for the Japanese home islands. Ryukyu Islands were separated and issued their own stamps 1948-1972 under US administration, catalogued as Ryukyu Islands.

periods.label: Post-war Japan
periods.authority: Ministry of Posts and Telecommunications; Japan Post from 2003, Japan Post Holdings from 2007
periods.note: Sovereignty restored by the San Francisco Treaty, 1952. Ryukyus returned to Japan 1972.

successors: Ryukyu Islands (1948-1972, catalogued separately, reverted to Japan 1972)
inscriptions: Japanese characters only on early issues — no Latin script (the 1871 Dragons carry no roman lettering at all)
NIPPON (roman script, on all Japanese stamps since the 1960s)
Dai Nippon Teikoku Yubin — Empire of Great Japan Post (pre-1947, in kanji)
Nippon Yubin — Japan Post (post-war, in kanji)
NIPPON with value in yen (standard modern form)
gaps: None. Japan issued stamps continuously from 1871 to the present, including throughout the Second World War and the Allied occupation. The occupation did not interrupt Japanese issuing — SCAP supervised rather than replaced the postal administration, which is a common misconception. The Ryukyu Islands are the exception: they were administratively detached from Japan 1945-1972 and issued separately.
forgery_risk: Extremely high, concentrated on the 1871 Dragons, which are among the most counterfeited stamps in the world — forgeries are commonly estimated to outnumber genuine examples by around 10:1, so the default assumption for an unexpertised Dragon should be that it is not genuine. The situation is complicated by official government reproductions: the Government Printing Bureau produced an engraved souvenir sheet in 1971 for the centenary, reproducing all four Dragon designs in their original colours, and cut-out singles from these sheets circulate as fakes. The 1872 Cherry Blossom issues are similarly forged. Forged and altered cancellations are pervasive across all classic Japanese material. Expertisation is essential for anything pre-1876.
wallpaper_risk: Japan does NOT have an agency-driven or excessive-issue reputation, has never used IGPC or Stamperija, and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. All output is genuinely postal. Japan does issue a large volume of modern commemoratives, prefectural (furusato) issues and greeting stamps, and the prefectural series in particular substantially inflates the modern count — but these are legitimate domestic issues with real postal use, not commercially manufactured wallpaper.
uncertain: The total count is an estimate and has not been verified against a physical catalogue; the Sakura/JSCA catalogue and Scott differ considerably, and the treatment of prefectural furusato issues and the very large modern greeting/personalised output swings the figure well beyond a 20% band. Attribution of specific early Dragon printings and settings is a specialist matter that general sources handle unreliably. Japanese occupation issues of 1941-1945 across Southeast Asia are catalogued under the occupied territories and are entirely invisible to a Japan-keyed search, concealing a substantial and philatelically important body of material.

summary: Kenya's first stamps were issued 23 May 1890 by the Imperial British East Africa Company — GB stamps surcharged in annas and inscribed BRITISH EAST AFRICA COMPANY, followed on 14 October 1890 by the company's own sun-and-crown design. Kenya then spent 73 years without a stamp bearing its own name alone, its post passing through five successive joint administrations. The first stamps of independent Kenya appeared 12 December 1963, but joint East African issues continued in parallel until 1976.

periods.label: Imperial British East Africa Company
periods.authority: Imperial British East Africa Company (chartered company)
periods.note: Post offices at Mombasa and Lamu from May 1890. Company neared bankruptcy.

periods.label: British East Africa Protectorate
periods.authority: British Crown administration
periods.note: Took over 1 July 1895. Used overprinted stamps of Great Britain, India and Zanzibar alongside its own issues.

periods.label: East Africa and Uganda Protectorates
periods.authority: Joint EA & Uganda protectorate postal administration
periods.note: Postal union with Uganda from 1901; joint stamps in use from 1904.

periods.label: Kenya and Uganda
periods.authority: Joint Kenya & Uganda postal administration

periods.label: Kenya, Uganda and Tanganyika (KUT)
periods.authority: East African Posts and Telecommunications / joint administration
periods.note: Tanganyika joined the union. KUT is the standard collecting shorthand.

periods.label: Independent Kenya
periods.authority: Kenya Posts and Telecommunications Corporation, later Postal Corporation of Kenya
periods.note: First own-name issues 12 Dec 1963.

periods.label: East African Community joint issues (parallel)
periods.authority: East African Posts and Telecommunications Administration
periods.note: KENYA UGANDA TANZANIA issues ran alongside Kenya's own stamps until the EAC postal union broke up.

predecessors: Great Britain, India and Zanzibar (overprinted/used in period)
successors: Kenya, Uganda and Tanzania (joint issues, ran in parallel to 1976)
inscriptions: KENYA UGANDA TANGANYIKA / KENYA UGANDA TANGANYIKA POSTAGE & REVENUE
JAMHURI YA KENYA / KENYA POSTAGE (Swahili forms appear on some issues)
gaps: No period without stamps available. Kenya as a named issuer is absent 1890-1963 — during those decades its post used company, protectorate or joint-territory inscriptions, never 'Kenya' alone. Currency changes (annas/rupees to East African shillings, 1922) mark the eras more sharply than any interruption.
forgery_risk: The 1890-1895 British East Africa company period is the main risk area: the provisional surcharges and handstamped provisionals of 1890-95 (including the Mombasa provisionals) exist with faked overprints and altered values, and revenue-cancelled stamps are routinely passed as postally used. Expertisation is expected on the high-value early material. Modern Kenya has no significant forgery problem.
wallpaper_risk: Kenya does NOT have an agency-driven or wallpaper reputation. It is not an IGPC or Stamperija volume client and I found no UPU illegal-issue circular naming Kenya. Its output is modest and postally motivated; modern availability is poor rather than excessive.
count_source: Not established. Stanley Gibbons Commonwealth: East Africa with Egypt & Sudan is the standard reference (it lists Kenya, Uganda and Tanzania together); Scott lists Kenya separately from British East Africa and KUT. No verified major-number total obtained.
uncertain: No catalogue total verified, so left at 0 rather than guessed. TRAP: this is one of the worst country-keyed-search traps in Africa — a search on 'Kenya' returns only 1963-onward material plus the 1922-35 Kenya and Uganda issues; the 1890-1922 era hides under British East Africa and East Africa and Uganda Protectorates, and the entire 1935-63 era hides under Kenya Uganda and Tanganyika (KUT). Also note the 1964-76 KENYA UGANDA TANZANIA joint issues are catalogued as a separate entity in most catalogues even though they were valid in Kenya. Whether Kenya's own 12 Dec 1963 stamps or the joint EAC issues should be treated as the primary independence-era listing varies by catalogue.

summary: Korea's first stamps were issued 18 November 1884 by the Joseon kingdom — 5 and 10 mun values, inscribed in Chinese characters with 'COREAN POST' in Roman letters (spelled with a C, not a K). They saw almost no use: the post office burned during the Gapsin Coup that December, and no further stamps appeared until 1895. The Korean Empire issued the 'Plum Blossom' series from 1900. Japanese annexation in 1910 ended Korean issues entirely until liberation; South Korea's own stamps begin 1 February 1946 with USAMGIK overprints on Japanese stamps. Any 'South Korea' catalogue listing that starts in 1946 hides the whole 1884-1910 Korean Empire era, which is listed separately as 'Korea'.

periods.label: Joseon kingdom
periods.authority: Korean Postal Administration (Joseon)
periods.note: 5 and 10 mun issued 18 Nov 1884; 25, 50, 100 mun printed but not issued. Post office burned in the Gapsin Coup, December 1884.

periods.label: No postal service
periods.authority: none
periods.note: Postal service suspended. Japanese and Chinese post offices operated in Korea during this window.

periods.label: Joseon / Korean Empire
periods.authority: Korean postal administration; Korean Empire (Daehan Jeguk) from 1897
periods.note: Service reintroduced 1895. UPU membership 1900. 'Plum Blossom' series of 13 values, 1900 — the first stamps actually printed in Korea.

periods.label: Japanese postal control / annexation
periods.authority: Empire of Japan
periods.note: Korean postal service absorbed by Japan from 1905; formal annexation 1910. Japanese stamps used throughout — no Korean issues. Catalogued under Japan.

periods.label: US Army Military Government in Korea
periods.authority: USAMGIK
periods.note: Japanese stamps overprinted from 1 Feb 1946; first commemoratives 1 May 1946; 'Joseon' definitives 10 Sept 1946.

periods.label: Republic of Korea
periods.authority: Korea Post (Republic of Korea)
periods.note: Government established 15 Aug 1948; inscriptions become 'Republic of Korea'. Issuing continued without interruption through the Korean War.

inscriptions: COREAN POST (1884, Roman lettering on higher values)
Chinese characters (hanja) on all early issues
大韓 / 大韓帝國 (Daehan, Korean Empire)
Script change: hanja-dominant to hangul-dominant after 1945; Roman 'KOREA' present on most modern issues
gaps: 1885-1894: no issues at all — the postal service collapsed after the 1884 Gapsin Coup destroyed the post office, and was not reinstated until 1895. 1906-1945: no Korean issues; Japanese stamps used under Japanese postal control and then annexation. Issuing was NOT interrupted by the Korean War (1950-53), though wartime output was thin and printing quality poor.
forgery_risk: High for the classic period. The 1884 5 and 10 mun are extensively forged, and genuinely used examples are rare enough that faked cancellations are a bigger problem than faked stamps — nearly 3 million were printed but almost none saw postal use, so any 'used' 1884 stamp deserves suspicion. The 1895 and 1897 surcharges and overprints on earlier values are commonly counterfeited. Reprints and 'remainders' of Korean Empire material circulate freely. Early 1950s Korean War-period issues exist with faked perforations and fabricated wartime covers.
wallpaper_risk: No. Korea Post is a conventional, moderately prolific national issuer with no agency involvement and no presence on UPU illegal-issue circulars. (Note the contrast with North Korea, which has a well-documented agency-driven and CTO reputation — do not carry that reputation across to the South.)
count_source: StampWorld online catalogue, major numbers for South Korea 1946-present. The separate 'Korea' listing (1884-1904) adds roughly 40-50 major numbers. Approximate.
uncertain: Catalogue totals not verified against a printed Scott or Stanley Gibbons volume; treat as approximate. The exact scope and number of USAMGIK overprint varieties (1946) was not established. Myth flag: the 1884 issue is often described as 'never used' — it was used, briefly, in November-December 1884, but genuine covers are extremely rare and most 'used' examples on the market carry favour or fabricated cancels. Second trap: catalogues split this territory into 'Korea' (1884-1905) and 'Korea, South' (1946-), with Japanese stamps filling 1905-1945 under Japan — three separate country keys for one continuous territory. North Korean material (1946-) is a fourth, entirely separate listing and must not be merged.

summary: Lithuania's first stamps - the crude First Vilnius issue of 10 and 15 skatikų, printed overnight at Martynas Kukta's Vilnius printshop - went on sale 27 December 1918, inscribed 'Lietuvos paštas'. The independent republic issued roughly 768 stamps to 1940, then stamp issuing ceased for half a century under Soviet and German rule, resuming with independence in 1990. Several adjacent entities are catalogued separately and are easily missed: Central Lithuania (1920-22), Memel/Klaipėda, the Ober Ost German occupation, and the 1941 German-invasion local 'June' issues.

periods.label: Russian Empire
periods.authority: Imperial Russian Post; stamps of Russia used
periods.note: Catalogued under Russia

periods.label: German occupation (Ober Ost / Postgebiet Ob. Ost)
periods.authority: German Imperial administration of the eastern occupied territories
periods.note: Catalogued under Germany, not Lithuania

periods.label: Republic of Lithuania (first independence)
periods.authority: Lietuvos paštas, Republic of Lithuania
periods.note: First Vilnius issue 27 Dec 1918; capital moved to Kaunas after Polish seizure of Vilnius 1920

periods.label: Central Lithuania (Litwa Środkowa)
periods.authority: Pro-Polish Republic of Central Lithuania at Vilnius; absorbed by Poland 1922
periods.note: Separate catalogue country

periods.label: Memel / Klaipėda
periods.authority: Allied (French) administration 1920-23, then Lithuanian administration after the January 1923 revolt
periods.note: Separate catalogue country ('Memel' / 'Klaipeda'); Lithuanian-administered overprints 1923-25

periods.label: Soviet annexation
periods.authority: Lithuanian SSR / USSR; stamps of the USSR used
periods.note: Brief 1940 Soviet overprints on Lithuanian stamps

periods.label: German occupation - local 'June' issues and Ostland
periods.authority: Wehrmacht local commanders (Telšiai, Rokiškis, Zarasai, Alsėdžiai etc.), then Reichskommissariat Ostland
periods.note: Ostland overprints catalogued under Germany

periods.label: Lithuanian SSR
periods.authority: USSR; stamps of the USSR used - no Lithuanian issues

periods.label: Restored Republic of Lithuania
periods.authority: Lietuvos paštas / AB Lietuvos paštas
periods.note: First restored issue October 1990

inscriptions: Lietuvos paštas (Lithuanian Post) - first Vilnius issue 1918
skatikų / skatiku, auksinas, later litas and centas as currency words
Post-1990: LIETUVA, with euro values from 2015
Central Lithuania: LITWA SRODKOWA (Polish, separate entity)
Memel: MEMELGEBIET, MEMEL, and Lithuanian KLAIPEDA / KLAIPĖDA overprints
1941 local issues: LAISVA LIETUVA (Free Lithuania), NEPRIKLAUSOMA LIETUVA (Independent Lithuania)
gaps: 1941-1990 essentially: after the 1940 Soviet annexation and the scattered 1941 German-invasion local issues, no stamps were issued in Lithuania's name until October 1990. Soviet (1940-41, 1944-90) and German Ostland (1941-44) stamps served instead. Cause: annexation and occupation.
forgery_risk: Very high in several areas. The 1918-19 first Vilnius and Kaunas issues are extensively forged, as are their perforation and paper varieties. The 1941 German-occupation local 'June' issues (Telšiai, Rokiškis, Zarasai, Alsėdžiai, Panevėžys and others) are among the most heavily faked material in European philately - many 'varieties' are pure fantasy and some issues are entirely bogus. Memel/Klaipėda overprints and Central Lithuania overprints are also common forgery targets. Expertisation is essential for all of these.
wallpaper_risk: No. Lithuania has a restrained, domestically-programmed output and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. It has no IGPC/Stamperija-style agency history.
count_source: Michel Europa / specialised Lithuanian catalogues (Lituanica); approximate, combining c.768 major numbers 1918-1940 with post-1990 issues
uncertain: The post-1990 major-number total is an estimate; I could not confirm a current final Michel or Scott number. The '768 stamps 1918-1940' figure comes from a secondary source and may count differently from Michel or Scott. Note the trap that Central Lithuania, Memel/Klaipėda, Ober Ost and Ostland are catalogued as separate countries (or under Poland/Germany), so a country-keyed search on 'Lithuania' will miss those eras entirely. Whether particular 1941 local issues are genuinely postally authorised remains disputed in the literature.

summary: The territory's first adhesives were Indian stamps overprinted with a crown and a cents value from 1 September 1867, when the Straits Settlements became a Crown Colony; De La Rue 'STRAITS SETTLEMENTS POSTAGE' definitives followed from December 1867. Before that, stamps of India were used from 1854. The individual Malay states began issuing from Johore 1876, then Perak 1878, Selangor 1881, Pahang 1889, Negri Sembilan 1891, with Federated Malay States issues 1900-1935. Stamps inscribed MALAYSIA date only from 1963; state definitives continue to this day alongside federal issues, which is why a country-keyed search under 'Malaysia' silently omits roughly a century of material.

periods.label: Pre-stamp / Indian stamps used
periods.authority: Post Office of India (Bengal circle to 1861, Burma circle thereafter)
periods.note: Earliest postal markings from Prince of Wales Island (Penang) c.1806. No distinctive stamps; identified by cancellation only.

periods.label: Straits Settlements
periods.authority: Straits Settlements Crown Colony post office
periods.note: Covers Singapore, Penang, Malacca and later Labuan. Catalogued as 'Straits Settlements', not Malaysia.

periods.label: Individual Malay States
periods.authority: Johore, Perak, Selangor, Pahang, Negri Sembilan, Kedah, Kelantan, Perlis, Trengganu, Sungei Ujong, Sarawak, North Borneo, Labuan
periods.note: Johore 1876 first. Several states still have their own low-value definitives under Pos Malaysia today.

periods.label: Federated Malay States
periods.authority: Federated Malay States postal administration
periods.note: Perak, Selangor, Negri Sembilan, Pahang under a joint inscription.

periods.label: Japanese occupation
periods.authority: Imperial Japanese Military Administration (Malai/Malaya)
periods.note: Overprints on Straits Settlements, state and FMS stamps, plus Japanese stamps; also locally printed issues.

periods.label: Thai occupation of the four northern states
periods.authority: Kingdom of Thailand (Si Rat Malai)
periods.note: Kedah, Kelantan, Perlis, Trengganu ceded by Japan to Thailand Oct 1943. Catalogued under Thailand occupation issues (Scott 2N-series), not under Malaysia.

periods.label: British Military Administration
periods.authority: British Military Administration, Malaya
periods.note: Straits Settlements stamps overprinted BMA MALAYA from 19 Oct 1945; c.240 million overprinted.

periods.label: Malayan Union / Federation of Malaya
periods.authority: Malayan Postal Union; Federation of Malaya from 1957
periods.note: 1948 territorial definitives for Penang, Malacca, Singapore and the states; Federation of Malaya national issues from 5 May 1957 (independence 31 Aug 1957).

periods.label: Malaysia
periods.authority: Federation of Malaysia; Pos Malaysia (formerly Jabatan Perkhidmatan Pos)
periods.note: Formed 16 Sept 1963 with Singapore, Sarawak, North Borneo (Sabah). Singapore left 1965. Federal high values inscribed MALAYSIA; state low values carry the state name.

inscriptions: state names: JOHORE / JOHOR, PERAK, SELANGOR, PAHANG, NEGRI SEMBILAN, KEDAH, KELANTAN, PERLIS, TRENGGANU, MALACCA / MELAKA, PENANG / PULAU PINANG, SABAH, SARAWAK
Jawi (Arabic-script Malay) legends on many state issues
gaps: No true nationwide gap. Regular colonial issuing was interrupted 1942-45 by the Japanese occupation, when overprinted and locally printed occupation stamps replaced normal issues; the four northern states were under Thai administration 1943-45. Individual states have long dormant stretches (e.g. Sungei Ujong ends 1895 when absorbed into Negri Sembilan).
forgery_risk: High for overprints. Japanese occupation overprints are extensively forged — many are cheap enough to fake profitably and expertisation is routinely advised. B.M.A. MALAYA overprints have forged and faked varieties (inverted, double, missing letters). Early Malay state overprints and surcharges (Perak, Selangor, Sungei Ujong, Johore) are commonly forged; Straits Settlements 1867 crown-and-value overprints on India likewise. Thai occupation Kelantan/Trengganu overprints are among the most forged items in the area.
wallpaper_risk: No. Malaysia is not an agency-driven issuer and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Output is moderate and produced by Pos Malaysia. Some collectors grumble about miniature-sheet volume since the 1990s, but this is ordinary modern practice, not wallpaper.
count_source: StampWorld online catalogue, major numbers for 'Malaysia' 1963-present (federal issues only; state issues and all pre-1963 predecessor entities are catalogued separately and add several thousand more). Figure is approximate.
uncertain: Exact catalogue totals were not verified against a physical Scott or Stanley Gibbons volume; the count is an order-of-magnitude figure. Also unverified: whether any Sarawak/North Borneo material overlaps the Malaysia listing in Gibbons Part 1 versus a separate section. Myth flag: collectors often speak of 'Malaya' as a single stamp-issuing country — it is not. 'Malaya' is a collecting area covering roughly 20 separate catalogue entities, and Gibbons/Scott list them individually. A search keyed on 'Malaysia' returns nothing before 1963. Second trap: the 1963 Malaysia-formation commemoratives exist inscribed for Singapore too, and Singapore's 1963-65 issues are catalogued under Singapore, not Malaysia.

count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (Mexico); the MEPSI specialised literature and the Follansbee catalogue are the specialist standards for the classic period
summary: Mexico issued its first stamps on 1 August 1856, portraying Miguel Hidalgo y Costilla. Mexico is philatelically unique for its district overprint system: from 1856 until officially the end of 1883, stamps were overprinted with the name of the district post office to which they were consigned, as an anti-theft measure, often with consignment numbers and years. This makes Mexican classics a study in overprints rather than designs, and makes them heavily forged. Mexico has issued continuously since 1856 apart from disruption during the Revolution, when multiple factional authorities issued concurrently.

periods.label: Republic — first issues
periods.authority: Correos de México (Republican government)
periods.note: First issue 1 August 1856: five values (1/2r, 1r, 2r, 4r, 8r) with Hidalgo portrait and district overprints.

periods.label: Second Mexican Empire
periods.authority: Imperial government of Maximilian I
periods.note: Eagle and Maximilian issues. District overprint system continues.

periods.label: Restored Republic
periods.authority: Correos de México
periods.note: District overprints officially discontinued at the end of 1883. Porfiriato era issues from 1876.

periods.label: Mexican Revolution — concurrent authorities
periods.authority: Multiple competing factions: Constitutionalist (Carranza), Conventionist, Sonora and other regional/provisional governments, plus the federal administration
periods.note: The 'Mexican Revolution issues'. Numerous overprints, provisionals and local issues from competing authorities. Postal service was severely disrupted.

periods.label: Post-revolutionary and modern Mexico
periods.authority: Correos de México / Servicio Postal Mexicano (SEPOMEX from 1986)

predecessors: Spain (Spanish colonial and early independent Mexico used stampless postal markings — no adhesive predecessor)
inscriptions: CORREOS MEJICO (early form, with J — a key dating point)
District names applied as overprints (MEXICO, VERACRUZ, GUADALAJARA, PUEBLA, ZACATECAS and dozens more) — these are overprints, not the country name
gaps: No complete national gap, but the Revolution period 1913-1916 saw the unified postal service break down, with different factions controlling different regions and issuing separately. Normal national issuing was disrupted rather than suspended. The spelling change from MEJICO to MEXICO is a useful dating marker across the 19th century.
forgery_risk: Very high, and structurally worse than most countries because of what Mexico itself did. In the early 20th century the Mexican government sold the original printing plates for the 1856, 1861 and 1867 issues — along with genuine handstamps, cancellation devices and paper — to stamp dealers in the United States, who produced unauthorised reprints for sale to collectors. This means 'forgeries' of Mexican classics can be struck from genuine plates with genuine handstamps on genuine paper, which defeats most ordinary authentication tests. Separately, the rare district overprints command high premiums and are extensively faked, as are consignment numbers and years. The 1913-1916 Revolution issues are among the most forged material in world philately — for some issues, forgeries on the market substantially outnumber genuine examples. Nothing in Mexican classic or revolutionary philately should be bought unexpertised.
wallpaper_risk: Mexico does NOT have an agency-driven or excessive-issue reputation, does not use IGPC or Stamperija, and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Modern output is moderate and genuinely postal. The historical abundance problem is the unauthorised reprint scandal described above, not commercial wallpaper.
uncertain: The total count is an estimate and has not been verified against a physical catalogue; the figure is exceptionally sensitive to counting rules because district overprints can be treated as major varieties (Follansbee and the specialist literature list thousands of overprint combinations that Scott collapses into far fewer major numbers), so the true 'catalogued item' count could be an order of magnitude higher than the major-number count given. The status of many 1913-1916 factional issues as legitimately postal versus speculative is genuinely disputed in the literature and I could not resolve it. Exact dates for the discontinuation of district overprints vary slightly between sources around 1883-1884.

summary: Montenegro issued its first stamps in 1874 on the opening of the first public post office at Cetinje: seven values (2, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15, 25 novcic) bearing Prince Nikola's portrait, a design retained for over 25 years. Issues ran to 1916, then ceased for ninety years apart from occupation and exile material. Modern Montenegro resumed issuing in December 2005, six months before the 3 June 2006 declaration of independence.

periods.label: Principality, later Kingdom of Montenegro
periods.authority: Montenegrin post, Cetinje, under Prince (from 1910 King) Nikola I
periods.note: First issue 1874, seven denominations, Prince Nikola portrait. Issues continue through 1913.

periods.label: Austro-Hungarian occupation
periods.authority: k.u.k. Militarverwaltung Montenegro
periods.note: Austrian military overprints. Catalogued under Austrian occupation issues.

periods.label: Gaeta exile issues
periods.authority: Montenegrin government-in-exile, Gaeta, Italy
periods.note: King Nikola definitives overprinted СЛОБОДНА ЦРНА ГОРА (Free Montenegro), plus postage dues and advice-of-receipt. NONE were postally used - these are effectively cinderellas despite catalogue listing.

periods.label: Kingdom of Yugoslavia
periods.authority: Kingdom of SHS, then Kingdom of Yugoslavia
periods.note: No Montenegrin issues.

periods.label: Italian occupation / Protectorate of Montenegro
periods.authority: Italian occupation administration
periods.note: Yugoslav stamps with Italian overprints, then Italian stamps overprinted CRNA GORA / ЦРНА ГОРА in Cyrillic.

periods.label: German occupation
periods.authority: German military administration
periods.note: Separate small catalogue listing.

periods.label: SFR Yugoslavia, then FR Yugoslavia, then Serbia and Montenegro
periods.authority: Yugoslav PTT, later Serbia and Montenegro
periods.note: No Montenegro-specific issues.

periods.label: Republic of Montenegro
periods.authority: Posta Crne Gore
periods.note: First modern series 15 December 2005 - map, National Assembly in Podgorica, arms and flag. Independence declared 3 June 2006.

inscriptions: ЦРНА ГОРА (Crna Gora, Cyrillic - the standard historic inscription)
CRNA GORA (Latin, used on Italian occupation overprints and on modern issues)
КРАЉЕВИНА ЦРНА ГОРА (Kingdom of Montenegro, from 1910)
СЛОБОДНА ЦРНА ГОРА (Free Montenegro - Gaeta exile overprint)
MONTENEGRO (on Austrian and German occupation material)
Script note: historic issues are Cyrillic; modern issues use Latin CRNA GORA and MONTENEGRO, often both
gaps: Two long gaps. 1916-1941 with only the non-postal Gaeta exile material and Austrian occupation overprints. 1945-2005, sixty years with no Montenegrin issues at all, the territory being covered by Yugoslav and then Serbia and Montenegro stamps. Both gaps are political, not postal collapse.
forgery_risk: Very high, and this is the defining caution for the country. The 1874-1898 Prince Nikola issues were forged wholesale by the Spiro Brothers of Hamburg; their products show inferior paper and inconsistent portrait shading. Numerous other forgers worked the series. Forged and faked overprints are common on the 1941-43 Italian occupation CRNA GORA issues, which are cheap to fake on common Italian stock. The Gaeta 1921-22 exile issues are themselves of doubtful philatelic standing — never postally used — and are additionally forged. Expert certification is effectively mandatory for anything scarce before 1945.
wallpaper_risk: No. Montenegro does NOT have an agency-driven or excessive-issue reputation. Posta Crne Gore issues a small conventional programme and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Note separately that a body of 19th-century Montenegrin material exists in reprint form, which is a different problem from modern agency wallpaper.
count_source: Not reliably established. Colnect shows a figure near 58 for one Montenegro listing, which is clearly a partial or modern-only count and should not be used. Michel Europa or Scott would be the correct authority for a combined 1874-1916 plus 2005-present total.
uncertain: BATCH FILE MISMATCH: batch_15.txt does not contain Montenegro; it appears in batch_22.txt with 99 records held. Please confirm which list is authoritative. Unverified: exact total catalogue count; whether the last pre-WWI issue is 1913 or 1916 — sources give both, likely reflecting 1913 as the last new design and 1916 as the last year of issue or of validity; the exact end date of the German occupation listing. Myth flag: the 1921-22 Gaeta issues are routinely offered as genuine postal stamps of Montenegro; they were never postally used and were sold to refugees and collectors at Gaeta, so they should be treated as government-in-exile labels rather than postal issues. Trap flag: 1918-2005 Montenegrin material is catalogued under Yugoslavia, Serbia and Montenegro, Austrian Occupation, Italian Occupation of Montenegro, and German Occupation of Montenegro — five separate country keys, none of them 'Montenegro'.

count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue, listed under Burma/Myanmar (approximate major numbers through the early 2020s); Stanley Gibbons Part 21 comparable
summary: Burma had no stamps of its own until 1 April 1937, when India stamps overprinted BURMA appeared on separation from British India; the first stamps actually used in the territory were the 1854 issues of India, identifiable only by postmark, so 1854 is the first-use date and 1937 the first-issue date. The Japanese occupation of 1942-45 produced a complex group (Burma Independence Army, Japanese administration, State of Burma), followed by 1945 MILY ADMN overprints and independence on 4 January 1948. English inscriptions tracked the country's changing name — Union of Burma, then Socialist Republic of the Union of Burma, then Union of Myanmar from 1990 and Republic of the Union of Myanmar after 2010.

periods.label: British Indian usage
periods.authority: Government of India Post Office
periods.note: India stamps used unoverprinted; catalogued under India, identifiable only by Burmese postmarks. A whole era hidden from a Burma-keyed search.

periods.label: British Burma
periods.authority: Post Office, Burma (British administration)
periods.note: BURMA overprints on India 1937; first stamps inscribed BURMA in the design, KGVI, 1938-40.

periods.label: Japanese occupation
periods.authority: Burma Independence Army; Japanese military administration; State of Burma (Ba Maw government, independent 1943)
periods.note: Multiple overlapping issuers, overprints and locally printed designs.

periods.label: British Military Administration
periods.authority: British Military Administration, Burma
periods.note: Pre-war Burma stamps overprinted MILY ADMN, 1945; civil administration issues follow.

periods.label: Interim Government / Union of Burma
periods.authority: Union of Burma Posts and Telegraphs
periods.note: Independence 4 Jan 1948; 1947 interim-government overprints in Burmese precede it.

periods.label: Socialist Republic of the Union of Burma
periods.authority: Burma Posts and Telecommunications
periods.note: Last stamp so inscribed appeared 6 September 1989.

periods.label: Union of Myanmar / Republic of the Union of Myanmar
periods.authority: Myanmar Posts and Telecommunications / Myanma Posts
periods.note: All stamps inscribed UNION OF MYANMAR from 12 May 1990; later issues reflect the 2010 name change.

inscriptions: SOCIALIST REPUBLIC OF THE UNION OF BURMA
REPUBLIC OF THE UNION OF MYANMAR
Burmese script မြန်မာ / ဗမာ
Japanese occupation issues carry Burmese and Japanese text, and the 'peacock' device
gaps: Effectively no British-administration issues 1942-45 during the Japanese occupation, when occupation authorities issued instead. Output has been extremely thin in several later periods, with some years producing only one or two stamps; whether any single calendar year saw literally no issue I could not confirm.
forgery_risk: High in two areas: the 1942-45 Japanese occupation and Burma Independence Army material, where overprints (including peacock handstamps) are among the most forged issues in Asian philately and where genuine bogus/fantasy items also circulated at the time; and the 1937 BURMA overprints and 1945 MILY ADMN overprints, both routinely faked, especially inverted and double varieties. Certification is advisable for anything scarce from 1937-48.
wallpaper_risk: No — the opposite. Myanmar is a notably sparse issuer with a state-run programme, no IGPC or Stamperija agency arrangement, and no appearance on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Its low output is a defining characteristic of the country for collectors.
uncertain: first_issue_year is recorded as 1854 per the brief's 'first stamps used there' rule; Myanmar's first stamps of its own are 1937, and catalogues start the country at 1937. The 1942-45 occupation period is bibliographically unstable — different catalogues assign the Burma Independence Army, Japanese administration and Ba Maw State of Burma issues to different headings and sequences, so counts and attributions vary. The total stamp count is the least reliable figure here; post-2010 output is poorly recorded in mainstream catalogues.

summary: The Netherlands issued its first stamps on 1 January 1852: three imperforate values (5c, 10c, 15c) showing King William III, watermarked posthorn. Critically for identification, the earliest Dutch stamps carry NO country name at all - they are inscribed only POST ZEGEL and the value, which regularly traps collectors sorting unidentified classics. The country has issued continuously ever since, including throughout the German occupation of 1940-45, when the Dutch PTT continued to operate under German control. NEDERLAND appears as the standard inscription from the later 19th century onward.

periods.label: Kingdom of the Netherlands - William III
periods.authority: Dutch Post Office (Rijkspostkantoor)
periods.note: First issue 1852 inscribed POST ZEGEL only, no country name. Later William III types add NEDERLAND.

periods.label: Wilhelmina
periods.authority: Dutch PTT
periods.note: Long reign covering the Bontkraag, Veth, Konijnenburg and wartime types.

periods.label: German occupation
periods.authority: Dutch PTT operating under the German Reichskommissariat
periods.note: Stamps continued to be issued and are catalogued under Netherlands, not as an occupation entity. Includes propaganda and Legioen/Winterhulp semi-postals. Liberation 5 May 1945; the pre-war and wartime stamps stayed in use until the new series of 1 April 1946.

periods.label: Juliana
periods.authority: Dutch PTT (Staatsbedrijf der PTT)

periods.label: Beatrix
periods.authority: PTT Post, later TPG Post
periods.note: Guilder to euro changeover 2002.

periods.label: Willem-Alexander
periods.authority: PostNL

predecessors: Pre-stamp Dutch state post (no adhesives before 1852)
inscriptions: POST ZEGEL (1852 first issue - NO country name, the key identification trap)
KONINKRIJK DER NEDERLANDEN (on some sheets and later issues)
NEDERLAND with value in CENT / GULDEN (G, GLD, f) to 2001, EURO from 2002

Script note: Latin throughout, no script change. The absence of a country name on the 1852-1867 issues is the single most useful identification fact; identify them by the posthorn watermark and the POST ZEGEL wording.
gaps: None. The Netherlands has issued in every year since 1852. It did NOT stop issuing during the 1940-45 German occupation - stamps continued to appear under Dutch PTT imprint with German oversight, and there is no year without issues. Some post-liberation supply gaps were covered by continuing use of wartime stock until April 1946.
forgery_risk: Moderate and well-defined. The 1852 first issue and the 1867 third issue are faked and, more commonly, repaired, reperforated or regummed - certificates are expected on the better values. Fake and improved cancellations on classics (to fill number-cancel collections) are widespread. The 1923 Jubilee high values (2.50g and 5g) are a known target. Occupation-era propaganda and semi-official material, and the several sets of unofficial 1944-45 Liberation and 'free Netherlands' labels, include outright fantasies with no postal status. Netherlands Indies and Curacao Japanese-occupation overprints attract far more forgery than metropolitan Dutch material.
wallpaper_risk: No. The Netherlands is not agency-driven and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. PostNL's modern programme is large and includes extensive personalised stamps (Persoonlijke Postzegels), business-customer sheets and collector products, which inflate any raw count considerably; NVPH lists many of these outside the main numbering, so a headline total depends heavily on where the line is drawn.
count_source: Approximate major numbers, NVPH (Nederlandsche Vereeniging van Postzegelhandelaren) catalogue, ordinary postage plus back-of-book
uncertain: The total is an estimate. NVPH, Michel and Scott diverge sharply on the modern era because of personalised stamps, self-adhesive booklet sheets and se-tenant sheetlets - NVPH's own supplementary numbering for these can add thousands of items if counted. NVPH numbering is the standard for this country and differs entirely from Scott, so any count is only meaningful against a named catalogue. CATALOGUE TRAP: a country-keyed search on 'Netherlands' will miss the whole overseas empire and several adjacent administrations, all catalogued separately - Netherlands Indies (from 1864), Netherlands New Guinea (1950-62), Curacao (1873-1948) and its successors Netherlands Antilles, Aruba, Curacao (2010- ) and Caribbean Netherlands/Bonaire-Saba-St Eustatius (2011- ), Suriname (1873-1975), and the Netherlands Offices in Turkey. Also outside 'Netherlands': the Dutch military and field post issues, and the 1945-49 Netherlands Indies material issued under Dutch authority during the Indonesian revolution.

summary: New Zealand's first stamps appeared 18 July 1855: the imperforate 'Full Face Queens' or Chalon heads (1d, 2d, 1s), engraved by Perkins Bacon & Co, London, from Alfred Edward Chalon's 1838 portrait of Queen Victoria. From 1856 the plates were used locally by John Richardson in Auckland, and the resulting London-vs-local printings, paper and watermark varieties, and locally applied provincial perforations (notably 'perf 13 at Dunedin', 1863) make the Chalon period one of the most heavily specialised classic areas in the Commonwealth. Issuing has been continuous under British colonial, then Dominion, then independent New Zealand authority with no gaps. The country's philatelic footprint extends well beyond its own name: New Zealand administered and overprinted stamps for several Pacific territories, and those eras are catalogued under the territory, not under New Zealand.

periods.label: Pre-stamp colonial mails
periods.authority: Colony of New South Wales, then Crown Colony of New Zealand postal service
periods.note: No adhesive stamps of its own; handstruck marks. New Zealand was administered from NSW until 1841.

periods.label: Crown Colony issues
periods.authority: Colonial Government of New Zealand (British Crown Colony)
periods.note: Chalon heads to 1873, then Side Face and pictorial issues; 1901 Universal Penny Postage.

periods.label: Dominion of New Zealand
periods.authority: New Zealand Post and Telegraph Department
periods.note: Some 1909-10 issues literally inscribed DOMINION OF NEW ZEALAND.

periods.label: Independent New Zealand
periods.authority: NZ Post Office, from 1987 New Zealand Post Ltd
periods.note: Statute of Westminster adopted 1947. NZ Post corporatised 1987; heavy modern commemorative and self-adhesive programme.

periods.label: Ross Dependency (separate catalogue entity)
periods.authority: New Zealand Post on behalf of the Ross Dependency
periods.note: Inscribed ROSS DEPENDENCY; valid in NZ but catalogued separately.

predecessors: New South Wales (administrative predecessor 1840-41, no NZ-specific stamps)
inscriptions: N.Z. (abbreviated, incl. life insurance and official issues)
N.Z. GOVT. LIFE INSURANCE DEPT. / NEW ZEALAND GOVERNMENT LIFE INSURANCE
Te Reo Maori wording alongside English on many modern commemoratives
gaps: None. New Zealand has issued stamps continuously every decade since 1855, through both World Wars.
forgery_risk: Substantial for the classic period. At least seven distinct forgery types of the 1855-73 Chalon heads were catalogued in the RPSNZ 'Postage Stamps of New Zealand' vol. 1 (1938); the field also swarms with genuine-stamp alterations rather than outright fakes - added or reperforated perforations, faked or chemically altered papers and watermarks, repaired margins on imperforates, and cleaned or fiscally-used copies passed as postal. Reprints and proofs from the Chalon plates circulate and are regularly mistaken for issued stamps. Forged and re-applied OFFICIAL overprints are a known problem, as are faked cancellations on the 1906 Christchurch Exhibition set and on high-value pictorials. Expertisation is standard practice for early Chalons.
wallpaper_risk: New Zealand is NOT an agency-driven issuer and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. All issues are genuine postal issues of a UPU member. NZ Post does run a large modern commemorative programme with frequent miniature sheets, personalised stamps and collector formats, and some collectors regard post-1990 output as over-issued - a volume complaint, not a legitimacy one.
count_source: StampWorld listing count for New Zealand (all major numbers, postage plus back-of-book, to 2025); Scott lists roughly 2,900 regular postage major numbers. Figure is approximate.
uncertain: Exact total is approximate - StampWorld, Scott, SG and the specialised NZ (CP/ACS) catalogues differ substantially, and the specialised catalogues break the Chalon period into hundreds of listed varieties that general catalogues collapse into a few numbers, so any single count is catalogue-dependent. CATALOGUE TRAP: whole eras of New Zealand postal administration are catalogued under other country headings and are invisible to a New-Zealand-keyed search - the 1914 New Zealand occupation of German Samoa sits under Samoa; Cook Islands, Aitutaki (1903-32), Penrhyn (1902-32), Niue and Tokelau are separate catalogue countries printed and administered by New Zealand; Ross Dependency is a separate entity though valid within NZ. MYTH FLAG: the 1914 'G.R.I.' overprints are frequently bundled with New Zealand's own issues - the G.R.I. overprints proper were applied at Rabaul for German New Guinea by the Australian expedition, while the New Zealand force in Samoa used separately overprinted German Samoa stock; different operations, catalogued separately. Also often misstated: the 1855 first printing is said to be entirely London-printed, but plates reached Auckland in 1855 and local Richardson printings begin 1856, so an 1855 date alone does not establish a London printing.

summary: Modern Nigeria's philately begins not with 'Nigeria' but with Lagos, whose first Queen Victoria stamps appeared 10 June 1874 — the date Nigeria itself celebrated as its stamp sesquicentenary in 2024. Four separate British entities (Lagos, Oil Rivers/Niger Coast Protectorate, Northern Nigeria, Southern Nigeria) issued before the unified colony's first stamps of 1 June 1914. Independence issues began 1 October 1960, republic definitives 1 November 1965. The breakaway Republic of Biafra issued its own stamps 1967-1970 and is catalogued separately.

periods.label: Colony of Lagos
periods.authority: British Colony of Lagos post office
periods.note: First stamps 10 June 1874 (1d, 2d); 3d/4d/6d/1s followed August 1874. Lagos absorbed into Southern Nigeria 16 Feb 1906.

periods.label: Oil Rivers / Niger Coast Protectorate
periods.authority: British Oil Rivers Protectorate, renamed Niger Coast Protectorate 12 May 1893
periods.note: Began July 1892 with GB stamps overprinted BRITISH / PROTECTORATE / OIL / RIVERS; own designs from 1894. Merged with Royal Niger Company territories 1 Jan 1900.

periods.label: Northern Nigeria Protectorate
periods.authority: British Protectorate of Northern Nigeria

periods.label: Southern Nigeria Protectorate
periods.authority: British Protectorate of Southern Nigeria (incl. Lagos from 1906)

periods.label: Colony and Protectorate of Nigeria
periods.authority: British colonial post office
periods.note: First unified Nigeria stamps 1 June 1914, KGV Empire keytype carried over from Northern Nigeria.

periods.label: Independent Nigeria (Dominion)
periods.authority: Nigerian Posts and Telegraphs
periods.note: First independence stamps 1 Oct 1960; definitives 1 Jan 1961.

periods.label: Federal Republic of Nigeria
periods.authority: Nigerian Postal Service (NIPOST) and predecessors
periods.note: Republic definitives 1 Nov 1965; still issuing, though at a low and irregular rate in recent decades.

predecessors: Great Britain (stamps used before local issues)
successors: Cameroons UKTT (Nigerian stamps overprinted CAMEROONS U.K.T.T. 1960-61)
gaps: No true nationwide gap in issuing. Coverage is fragmented rather than absent: 1874-1913 material is split across four separate catalogue countries, and the 1967-70 civil war years saw Biafran territory served by Biafra issues rather than Nigeria's. Modern output has been sparse and erratic (multi-year stretches with few or no new issues) but not formally interrupted.
forgery_risk: Early Lagos and Niger Coast Protectorate material is the risk area — Oil Rivers surcharges and the NIGER COAST PROTECTORATE provisional surcharges of 1893-94 exist with faked overprints and altered surcharges, and expertisation is normal for high values. Biafra 1968-70 issues attract fantasy and privately produced labels, and Biafran overprints on Nigerian stamps are frequently faked.
wallpaper_risk: Nigeria does NOT have an agency-driven wallpaper reputation. It is not a Stamperija or IGPC volume client and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars in the way many other African issuers do. If anything Nigeria's modern problem is the opposite — under-issuing and poor availability. Note separately that Biafra-labelled 'issues' outside 1967-70 are bogus.
count_source: Not established. Stanley Gibbons Commonwealth: West Africa is the standard reference for Nigeria and its four predecessor entities; Scott lists Nigeria separately from Lagos/Niger Coast/Northern/Southern Nigeria. No verified major-number total obtained.
uncertain: No catalogue total verified, so approx_total_stamps left at 0 rather than guessed. TRAP: a country-keyed search on 'Nigeria' silently loses 1874-1913 entirely — that era is catalogued under Lagos, Niger Coast Protectorate, Northern Nigeria and Southern Nigeria, four distinct catalogue countries. MYTH to flag: the frequently repeated claim that Nigeria's first stamps date from 1914 is only true of the unified colony; the first stamps for territory that is now Nigeria are the 1874 Lagos issues, which is the basis of the 2024 '150 years' commemorations. Exact start date of Northern Nigeria's own issues (1900 vs 1902 depending on catalogue treatment of overprints) not independently confirmed.

count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (approximate major numbers, regular postage + commemoratives, through the early 2020s); Stanley Gibbons Part 1 gives a broadly similar figure
summary: Pakistan's own stamps begin 1 October 1947 with the 'Nasik' PAKISTAN overprints on King George VI stamps of India, followed by pictorial definitives inscribed in English and Urdu. Before Partition the territory used India, and the earliest stamps ever used in what is now Pakistan are the 1852 Scinde Dawks of Sind (Karachi), catalogued under India — an era invisible to a Pakistan-keyed search. The feudatory state of Bahawalpur issued separately 1945-1949 and is catalogued under its own heading. East Pakistan's postal territory became Bangladesh in 1971.

periods.label: Pre-stamp and British Indian usage
periods.authority: East India Company / Government of India Post Office
periods.note: Scinde District Dawk 1852 (catalogued under India); India stamps used throughout Sind, Punjab, NWFP, Baluchistan and East Bengal. Identifiable only by postmark.

periods.label: Dominion of Pakistan — overprints
periods.authority: Pakistan Post Office (Dominion)
periods.note: PAKISTAN overprints on India, Nasik machine-printed plus large numbers of local handstamped, typewritten and manuscript overprints from Peshawar, Karachi, Lahore, Bahawalpur and elsewhere.

periods.label: Dominion / Republic definitives
periods.authority: Pakistan Post Office Department
periods.note: First own designs 9 July 1948 (Independence anniversary). Includes East Pakistan usage.

periods.label: Islamic Republic after separation of East Pakistan
periods.authority: Pakistan Post Office Department / Pakistan Post
periods.note: Continuous issuing; no renumbering.

periods.label: Bahawalpur (separate catalogue heading)
periods.authority: Amir of Bahawalpur
periods.note: Officials from 1945, ordinary issues 1947-1949, then absorbed into Pakistan.

periods.label: Las Bela (separate catalogue heading)
periods.authority: Jam of Las Bela
periods.note: Local state issues, valid only internally; catalogued under Indian Feudatory States, not Pakistan.

inscriptions: Las Bela issues: LAS BELA (English and Urdu)
gaps: No years without issues since 1947. The apparent 'gap' before 1947 is simply the British Indian period, catalogued under India.
forgery_risk: High for the 1947-49 provisional period. The Nasik overprints and especially the local handstamped/typewritten PAKISTAN overprints are extensively forged, as are inverted and double-overprint varieties; expertisation is normally required. Bahawalpur's 1947 'star and crescent' overprints on India are contentious and widely faked. The 1948 Bahawalpur pictorials exist in large speculative remainder quantities.
wallpaper_risk: No. Pakistan has a conservative, low-volume programme run by Pakistan Post itself and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. No agency (IGPC/Stamperija) involvement.
uncertain: Exact total is approximate; catalogues differ by more than 20% depending on whether officials, service stamps and the 1947-49 local overprints are given major numbers. The often-repeated claim that all 1947 overprints were made at Nasik is wrong — Nasik supplied only the first machine-overprinted set, and a large proportion of genuine overprints were produced locally in Pakistan.

count_source: Estimate from Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (Peru regular postage, semi-postals, airmails, officials) through the 2020s; not verified against a current edition
summary: Peru's first adhesives were the 1857 Pacific Steam Navigation Company stamps (1 real blue, 2 reales red), a British shipping company's stamps authorised by the Peruvian government for postal use from 1 December 1857 during a trial period; Peru's own lithographed three-value issue followed in March 1858. The record is broken up by the War of the Pacific (1879-83), when Chile occupied much of the country including Lima in 1881: the Peruvian government withdrew to Arequipa and issued its own stamps there 1881-85, while Chilean occupation overprints were applied elsewhere. Peru is also notable for the 1873 Callao-Lima railway commemorative, often cited as one of the world's first commemorative stamps.

periods.label: Pacific Steam Navigation Company issues
periods.authority: Pacific Steam Navigation Company (British), authorised by the Peruvian government
periods.note: 1 real and 2 reales used for Peruvian domestic mail from 1 December 1857 as a trial. Catalogued under Peru.

periods.label: Republic of Peru (early)
periods.authority: Republic of Peru postal administration
periods.note: Own lithographed issues from March 1858. Includes the 1873 railway commemorative.

periods.label: War of the Pacific — Chilean occupation
periods.authority: Chilean military occupation authorities; separately, the Peruvian government in exile at Arequipa
periods.note: Chilean occupation overprints on Peruvian stamps in occupied areas; Arequipa 'PROVISIONAL 1881-1882' issues by the Peruvian government for the area it still controlled (Arequipa, Cuzco, Moquegua). Various local and provisional issues (Ancachs, Chala, Cuzco, Paita, Pisco, Puno, Yca) date from this period.

periods.label: Restored Republic
periods.authority: Republic of Peru; modern SERPOST (Servicios Postales del Peru)
periods.note: Continuous issuing since the war's end.

inscriptions: overprints of the Chilean occupation (Chilean arms handstamp on Peruvian stamps)
local provisional names: ANCACHS, CHALA, CUZCO, PAITA, PISCO, PUNO, YCA, AREQUIPA
gaps: No complete national gap, but the War of the Pacific years 1879-85 are fragmented rather than blank — regular national issuing was disrupted, replaced by Chilean occupation overprints, the Arequipa government issues and numerous scattered local provisionals, with no single authority covering the whole territory. Later 19th-century political instability also produced irregular issuing.
forgery_risk: High in the classic period. The 1857 PSNC stamps are forged and are also frequently offered with faked Peruvian cancellations (unused PSNC stamps are common; genuine Peruvian postal use is what carries the premium). The Chilean occupation overprints and the Arequipa provisionals are extensively forged, and the 1881-85 local provisionals (Ancachs, Chala, Cuzco, Paita, Pisco, Puno, Yca) include both forgeries and outright bogus items — this is one of the classic 'fantasy overprint' fields in South American philately. Early lithographed issues are widely counterfeited and reprints exist. Forged cancellations are common throughout. Certificates strongly advised for anything from 1857-1885.
wallpaper_risk: None currently. Peru does not use IGPC or Stamperija and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars; its modern programme is small, and SERPOST's output is if anything sparse and irregularly distributed. The 1881-85 provisional era carries a bogus-issue problem, but that is a forgery matter rather than an agency wallpaper reputation.
uncertain: BATCH LIST CONFLICT: this country appears in the task message's list but not in the batch file, which names Finland, Armenia, Switzerland and Malaysia. See the note on Switzerland. FIRST-ISSUE-YEAR JUDGEMENT: 1857 is correct if the PSNC stamps count as Peru's first (major catalogues list them under Peru); if the convention is 'first stamps issued by the Peruvian state', the answer is 1858. I have not silently chosen — flagging for the parent. CATALOGUE TRAP: the Arequipa 1881-85 issues, the Chilean occupation overprints and the 1881-85 local provisionals are handled inconsistently — some catalogues list them under Peru, some as separate headings, and some relegate them to appendices or omit them; a country-keyed search on 'Peru' may miss the entire War of the Pacific era. Chilean occupation overprints may alternatively be indexed under Chile. MYTH: the 1873 Callao-Lima railway stamp is often called 'the world's first commemorative' — the claim is contested (candidates include the 1871 Peru issue itself, the 1876 US Centennial, and New South Wales 1888) and the safe statement is 'one of the earliest', not 'the first'. My total-stamp count is an unverified estimate. I could not establish exact date ranges for every local provisional.

summary: The Philippines issued its first adhesives on 1 February 1854 under Spanish colonial rule, making it the first stamp-issuing territory in Southeast Asia. Those first issues carry no country name — they are identified by the portrait of Isabel II — and it was only in 1872 that stamps first read CORREOS FILIPINAS. The islands then passed through a short-lived revolutionary republic, US military and civil administration, Commonwealth, Japanese occupation, and independence in 1946, each with distinct inscriptions. This makes the Philippines an unusually layered issuer for a single archipelago.

periods.label: Spanish colonial
periods.authority: Spanish colonial administration, Correos
periods.note: 1854-1872 designs shared with or generic to Spanish colonies and unnamed; from 1872 inscribed FILIPINAS

periods.label: Philippine Revolution / First Republic
periods.authority: Revolutionary government of Emilio Aguinaldo (Malolos)
periods.note: Small, scarce issue inscribed in Spanish; heavily faked

periods.label: US military administration
periods.authority: US Military Government
periods.note: US stamps overprinted PHILIPPINES from 30 June 1899

periods.label: US insular / civil administration
periods.authority: Bureau of Posts under US civil government
periods.note: 'PHILIPPINE ISLANDS - UNITED STATES OF AMERICA' series

periods.label: Commonwealth
periods.authority: Commonwealth of the Philippines Bureau of Posts (US sovereignty)
periods.note: COMMONWEALTH overprints and inscriptions

periods.label: Japanese occupation
periods.authority: Japanese Military Administration, then the Japanese-sponsored Second Republic (Laurel) from Oct 1943
periods.note: Scott N1-N39, NB1-NB8, NJ1, NO1-NO7; Commonwealth stamps overprinted, then original designs

periods.label: Commonwealth restored
periods.authority: Commonwealth Bureau of Posts, VICTORY overprints

periods.label: Republic of the Philippines
periods.authority: Bureau of Posts, then Philippine Postal Corporation (PHLPost)
periods.note: Independence 4 July 1946

inscriptions: No country name at all on the 1854-1871 Isabel II issues — identify by portrait
PHILIPPINE ISLANDS - UNITED STATES OF AMERICA
REPUBLIKA NG PILIPINAS / PILIPINAS (Filipino, dominant from the 1960s)
gaps: No true year-long gap, though the 1898-99 revolutionary period produced only a handful of issues and civil postal service was badly disrupted in 1899-1901 and again in 1944-45 during the reconquest.
forgery_risk: Substantial. The 1898-99 revolutionary issues are extensively forged and faked cancels are the norm rather than the exception. Spanish-period surcharges and the many provisional handstamps are faked. The 1899-1906 US overprints on US stamps attract fakes and faked inverted/double overprints — a genuine-stamp-plus-forged-overprint is the common form. Japanese occupation overprints, VICTORY overprints, and guerrilla/local provisional issues are all faked, and some 'guerrilla' locals sold to collectors are outright fantasies.
wallpaper_risk: Mild but real. The Philippines is a legitimate state issuer and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars, but PHLPost has run a heavy programme of topical and personalised issues with high face values in recent decades, and some collectors regard the modern output as bloated. This is not agency-driven wallpaper in the IGPC/Stamperija sense.
count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (major numbers including back-of-book), approximate; Stanley Gibbons and the specialised Philippine catalogues differ substantially on the Spanish and occupation periods
uncertain: Catalogue trap worth flagging: the 1854-1872 issues carry no country name and some early Spanish-colonial material used in the Philippines is catalogued under Spain or Spanish Colonies (and Cuba/Puerto Rico share designs), so a country-keyed search on 'Philippines' can miss or misattribute the earliest era. Likewise the 1899-1906 US-overprinted stamps are listed in Scott among US Administration/Possessions as well as under Philippines. The Japanese occupation issues sit under Philippines with N-prefix numbers in Scott but under a separate 'Japanese Occupation of the Philippines' heading in Stampworld and others. I could not verify a precise total-issue count from a current catalogue edition; the figure given is an estimate.

summary: Poland's first stamp appeared in January 1860 for the Congress Kingdom under Russian control — a near-copy of Russia's first stamp and the only Polish stamp of the 19th century. No further Polish stamps followed until 17 November 1918, when Warsaw local pictorials were overprinted POCZTA POLSKA six days after the armistice. Poland then issued continuously until 1939, ceased entirely under German and Soviet occupation, and resumed in 1944-45. The 1918-22 border-formation period and the 1939-45 occupation generate a mass of separately-catalogued entities that a Poland-keyed search will miss.

periods.label: Congress Kingdom of Poland under Russia
periods.authority: Russian postal administration, Kingdom of Poland
periods.note: Single stamp (Poland No. 1); Russian stamps thereafter

periods.label: Partition period, no Polish issues
periods.authority: Russia, Germany, Austria-Hungary
periods.note: Occupying powers' stamps used; WWI German 'Gen.-Gouv. Warschau' and Austrian overprints

periods.label: Second Polish Republic
periods.authority: Poczta Polska, Ministry of Post and Telegraphs
periods.note: Begins with POCZTA POLSKA overprints on Warsaw locals and on Austrian stocks (Cracow issue, Jan 1919)

periods.label: Occupation, no Polish state issues
periods.authority: German General Government (Deutsche Post Osten / Generalgouvernement); Soviet administration in the east
periods.note: Government-in-exile issues produced in London from 1941; Polish Corps issues in Italy

periods.label: People's Republic of Poland
periods.authority: Poczta Polska, PRL

periods.label: Third Polish Republic
periods.authority: Poczta Polska S.A.

inscriptions: GROSZ / ZLOTY / MARKA / FENIG (currency, useful for dating)
GENERALGOUVERNEMENT and DEUTSCHE POST OSTEN (occupation, not Polish issues)
gaps: Two large gaps. 1860/65-1918: no Polish issues during the partitions, occupying powers' stamps used. September 1939 - 1944: no issues by a Polish state authority in Poland; German Generalgouvernement stamps served, while the government-in-exile issued from London 1941-45.
forgery_risk: Very high in specific areas. The 1918-19 POCZTA POLSKA overprints, especially the Cracow issue, have plentiful and excellent counterfeits. The 1918-19 local issues (Przedborz, Zarki, Luboml and others) include both good forgeries and outright bogus creations. Plebiscite and border issues (Central Lithuania, Upper Silesia, Eastern Silesia) and the WWII camp and government-in-exile material are also faked. Poland No. 1 (1860) is forged and, more often, faked from repaired or re-cancelled examples.
wallpaper_risk: No. Poland is a conventional national issuer with no agency arrangement, no IGPC/Stamperija involvement, and no UPU illegal-issue listing. Communist-era output was high but was genuine postal issue.
count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (Poland regular, semi-postal, airmail, official and postage due sections; estimate)
uncertain: The total is an estimate. Major traps: Poland No. 1 (1860) is catalogued under Poland, but all other Russian-Poland postal material falls under Russia; the 1915-18 German 'Gen.-Gouv. Warschau' and Austrian K.u.K. issues are catalogued under German/Austrian occupation headings; Central Lithuania, Upper Silesia, Eastern Silesia, Port Gdansk, Danzig and the Polish Levant offices are separate catalogue countries; and the 1939-45 Generalgouvernement is listed under German occupation, not Poland. Whether the 1860 stamp remained valid to 1865 or later is stated variously in sources and was not confirmed.

count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (Portugal); Afinsa (the Portuguese national catalogue) is the specialist standard
summary: Portugal's first stamps were issued on 1 July 1853: four imperforate values with an embossed profile of Queen Maria II, inscribed only CORREIO — no country name at all, which is the key identification point for the classics. Portugal has issued continuously since 1853 with no gaps, spanning the monarchy, the 1910 republic, the Estado Novo and the modern republic. Its 1853 issue was officially reprinted repeatedly (1864, 1885, 1905, 1953), and separating reprints from originals is the central difficulty of Portuguese classic philately.

periods.label: Kingdom of Portugal
periods.authority: Portuguese Crown postal administration (Correio)
periods.note: First issue 1 July 1853, Maria II embossed. Successive monarchs: Pedro V, Luis I, Carlos I, Manuel II.

periods.label: First Republic
periods.authority: República Portuguesa
periods.note: Begins with REPUBLICA overprints on monarchy stamps, then Ceres definitives from 1912.

periods.label: Military dictatorship and Estado Novo
periods.authority: República Portuguesa under the Estado Novo (Salazar, then Caetano)
periods.note: Continues to be catalogued under Portugal without a break.

periods.label: Third Republic
periods.authority: CTT Correios de Portugal
periods.note: Carnation Revolution, April 1974. CTT privatised 2013-14 but continues as the designated postal operator.

inscriptions: CORREIO (1853 first issue — no country name)
CONTINENTE (on some issues distinguishing mainland from Azores/Madeira)
gaps: None. Portugal has issued continuously since 1853, through both world wars (in which it was a belligerent in WWI), the 1910 revolution, the 1926 coup and the 1974 revolution. No period of postal collapse.
forgery_risk: High for the classics. The 1853 issue and the subsequent embossed King issues are extensively forged — Spiro and Oneglia are the named forgers to watch, and their products typically carry fraudulent postmarks or appear imperforate on modern paper lacking the fluorescence of genuine stock. Separately and more insidiously, the 1853 issue was officially reprinted in 1864, 1885, 1905 and 1953 (with further reproductions in 1928); these reprints are genuine government products but are not the original stamps, and are routinely sold as such. They are distinguished by paper quality, gum, watermark and fine engraving details, notably the rendering of the hair. Portuguese colonial overprints are another significant fake area.
wallpaper_risk: Portugal does NOT have an agency-driven or excessive-issue reputation and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Modern output is moderate and legitimately postal. Historically, however, Portugal and especially its colonies produced large omnibus and commemorative sets in the late 19th and early 20th centuries that were criticised at the time as speculative — the 1898 Vasco da Gama omnibus across the colonies is the classic example — but this is a historical criticism, not a modern agency problem.
uncertain: The total count is an estimate and has not been verified against a physical volume; Afinsa and Scott number Portugal differently. Portugal's colonial and island material is catalogued under separate headings — Azores, Madeira, Angola, Mozambique, Portuguese India, Macao, Cape Verde, Portuguese Guinea, São Tomé e Príncipe, Timor — and a Portugal-keyed search will miss all of it, which is a very large body of material. The precise distinguishing criteria between the several 1853 reprintings are contested in the specialist literature.

summary: Romania's catalogue begins with the 1858 Moldavian Bull's Heads - four values (27, 54, 81, 108 parale) issued by the Principality of Moldavia from July to October 1858, inscribed PORTO SCRISOREI in Cyrillic. Moldavia and Wallachia united in 1859, and the united Principalities, Kingdom, People's Republic, Socialist Republic and post-1989 Republic all issue continuously under the general heading 'Romania'. The inscription history is unusually informative: ROMINA orthography 1954-63, ROMANA before and after.

periods.label: Principality of Moldavia
periods.authority: Moldavian postal administration, Iasi
periods.note: Bull's Heads. 24,064 printed, 11,756 sold; the reserve was destroyed by fire in 1874, leaving roughly 724 survivors. Catalogued as Romania Nos. 1-4 by Scott but issued by Moldavia, not Romania - a real trap.

periods.label: United Principalities of Moldavia and Wallachia
periods.authority: United Principalities postal administration
periods.note: Second and third issues also show the aurochs head, the later one paired with the Wallachian eagle.

periods.label: Principality / Kingdom of Romania
periods.authority: Posta Romana
periods.note: Kingdom proclaimed 1881. Includes the Romanian post offices in the Levant (Constantinople etc.), catalogued separately.

periods.label: German occupation of Romania
periods.authority: Militaer-Verwaltung in Rumaenien (M.V.i.R.)
periods.note: German stamps overprinted M.V.i.R.; catalogued under German Occupation Issues, not under Romania.

periods.label: Romanian occupation issues abroad
periods.authority: Romanian military administration
periods.note: Romanian occupation of Hungary - overprints used at Debrecen and in Transylvania; catalogued under Hungary (Romanian Occupation).

periods.label: People's Republic
periods.authority: Posta Romana / Posta Romina, Republica Populara Romana
periods.note: Inscribed REPUBLICA POPULARA ROMANA from 1948; changed to ROMINA orthography from mid-1954; back to POSTA ROMANA from November 1963.

periods.label: Socialist Republic
periods.authority: Posta Romana, Republica Socialista Romania
periods.note: R.S. ROMANIA inscriptions.

periods.label: Republic of Romania
periods.authority: Posta Romana / Romfilatelia (philatelic agency)
periods.note: Romfilatelia handles the philatelic programme.

predecessors: Austrian Post in the Turkish Empire (offices in the Principalities)
inscriptions: POSTA ROMANA / POSTA ROMANA (Kingdom onward, sometimes POSTA ROMANA with diacritics)
REPUBLICA POPULARA ROMINA / R.P. ROMINA / POSTA ROMINA (mid-1954 to Nov 1963)

Script note: the 1858 issues use Romanian in Cyrillic script; the transitional Cyrillic-Latin alphabet appears on the 1860s issues; Latin script thereafter. The 1954-63 ROMINA spelling is a communist-era orthographic reform and is a reliable dating cue.
gaps: 1859-1861: no stamps between the withdrawal of the Bull's Heads and the second issue, and 1858 itself was only a few months of validity. No modern gaps - issues continued through both world wars, although 1917-18 the government-in-exile at Iasi and the German occupation administration issued in parallel.
forgery_risk: High at the classic end. The 1858 Bull's Heads are among the most forged stamps in the world - forgeries, reprints, faked cancels and repaired copies vastly outnumber genuine examples, and no Bull's Head should be accepted without expertisation. The 1862-65 aurochs issues and the 1868-72 Prince Carol issues also have forgery and reprint problems. M.V.i.R. and 1919 Romanian-occupation overprints on Hungarian stamps are widely forged; Debrecen overprints in particular.
wallpaper_risk: Partly. Romania is not an IGPC/Stamperija territory and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars, but the state administration has a long-standing reputation for an inflated programme aimed at collectors - heavy use of imperforate variants, miniature sheets, restricted printings and high face values, especially in the 1960s-70s and again under Romfilatelia since the 1990s. Treat it as a high-volume official issuer rather than an agency country.
count_source: Approximate: Scott major numbers for Romania, all periods, including semi-postals and airmails. Not verified against a current edition; Michel Rumaenien and the Romanian LP catalogue number differently and the LP numbers are the ones Romanian dealers quote.
uncertain: Total count is an estimate only. Note that Romania was NOT in the batch file - the file lists China, United Arab Emirates, Ukraine and Azerbaijan, with no record count for Romania, so no existing-records figure could be checked. Cataloguing traps: the 1858 Bull's Heads were issued by Moldavia though catalogued as Romania 1-4; Romanian post offices in the Levant, the German M.V.i.R. occupation of Romania, and the Romanian occupation of Hungary (Debrecen, Transylvania) are all catalogued under other headings and vanish from a country-keyed search on 'Romania'. Also, Moldova (the modern republic, from 1991) is a wholly separate stamp-issuing entity and should not be conflated with the 1858 Principality of Moldavia.

summary: Imperial Russian general-issue stamps were placed on sale 10 December 1857 and became mandatory for internal mail on 1 January 1858 (1 March 1858 in the Caucasus, Transcaucasia and Siberia); the first stamp was an imperforate 10-kopeck arms type designed by Franz Kepler at the Expedition for the Procurement of State Papers, St Petersburg. Note the Tiflis city post stamp of 1857 predates the general issue but is a local, not a national issue. The issuing authority changed repeatedly: Imperial Post, RSFSR, USSR (1923-1991), and the Russian Federation from 1992. Russia is one of the most heavily sub-divided areas in philately - Zemstvo locals, Russian Offices Abroad, Civil War regimes and the successor republics are all catalogued separately from 'Russia' proper.

periods.label: Russian Empire
periods.authority: Imperial Russian Post (Expedition for the Procurement of State Papers)
periods.note: Includes Russian Offices in the Turkish Empire/Levant, China, and Crete, plus separate listings for Russian Poland and Finland.

periods.label: Provisional Government / early Soviet Russia
periods.authority: Russian Provisional Government, then RSFSR
periods.note: RSFSR stamps carry minimal wording: ПОЧТА and РСФСР. Includes 1922 famine-relief and inflation surcharges.

periods.label: Civil War and separatist regimes
periods.authority: Various White, national and interventionist authorities
periods.note: South Russia, Denikin, Wrangel, Far Eastern Republic, Siberia, Ukraine tridents, Armenia, Georgia, Azerbaijan, Batum, North-West Army - catalogued as separate entities, NOT under Russia.

periods.label: Soviet Union
periods.authority: Ministry of Communications of the USSR
periods.note: Inscription ПОЧТА СССР. Enormous output, much of it sold cancelled-to-order.

periods.label: Russian Federation
periods.authority: Marka / JSC Marka, Russian Post
periods.note: Inscription РОССИЯ, often with РОССИЯ / ROSSIJA and CYRILLIC-only forms.

predecessors: Pre-stamp Imperial Russian postal service (stamped envelopes 1845-1848, St Petersburg city post)
inscriptions: КОП / КОПѢЙКА (pre-1918 orthography with the yat)
CCCP (Latin transliteration used on some issues)

Script note: Cyrillic throughout; pre-1918 issues use pre-reform orthography (ѣ, і, ъ), a useful dating clue. Offices Abroad issues carry surcharges in piastres, paras, centimes or Chinese currency and are inscribed in Cyrillic plus the local currency.
gaps: No true national gap since 1857.
