Practical disruption 1917-1922: the Imperial stocks were simply re-used and surcharged rather than replaced, and in parts of the former Empire the postal system effectively collapsed during the Civil War. The 1992 changeover left Soviet stamps in use alongside early Russian Federation issues.
forgery_risk: Very high in specific areas. Zemstvo locals were extensively faked (Fournier and others) and many 'zemstvos' offered are bogus. Civil War overprints - Ukraine tridents, Armenia, Georgia, South Russia, Far Eastern Republic, Batum, Siberia - are forged more often than genuine, and expertisation is essential. The 1922 famine-relief and airmail surcharges, the 1923 'Consular' issues, and the Levant/China office overprints are also heavily forged. Soviet rarities (1935 Levanevsky overprint, 1922-23 gold-currency issues) have well-known counterfeits.
wallpaper_risk: Russia and the USSR are not agency-driven in the IGPC/Stamperija sense, and Russia does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars as an issuer. However Soviet output was very large and a great deal of it is cancelled-to-order remainder material, which depresses used values and inflates apparent scarcity ratings. Separately, a large body of BOGUS labels has been produced in the name of Russian and post-Soviet entities - Tatarstan, Bashkortostan, Chechnya/Ichkeria, Kalmykia, Udmurtia, Abkhazia, South Ossetia, Transnistria - none of which are legitimate Russian issues.
count_source: Approximate major numbers, Michel Russland/Sowjetunion (Europa Band 15) plus Michel Russische Föderation; roughly 130 Empire, ~100 RSFSR, ~6,200 USSR to 1991, ~3,000+ Russian Federation to date
uncertain: The total count is an estimate: catalogues disagree substantially because Michel, Scott, Stanley Gibbons and the Russian Standart-Kollektsiya catalogue differ on whether to give major numbers to perforation, watermark and printing varieties, and Soviet-era se-tenant and souvenir-sheet handling varies. Two common claims to flag: (1) it is often said the first Russian stamp appeared in 1858 - that is the date of compulsory use; the stamps went on sale 10 December 1857 and catalogues date them 1857. (2) The Tiflis 6-kopeck of 1857 is frequently described as 'the first Russian stamp'; it is a city-post local for Tiflis and Kojori, not a general Imperial issue. CATALOGUE TRAP: Finland (from 1856) and Russian Poland (1860) were Imperial territories but are catalogued under their own names; Russian Offices in the Levant, China and Crete are catalogued as separate entities; and all the 1918-1923 Civil War and national regimes sit outside 'Russia' in every major catalogue. A country-keyed search on 'Russia' will silently miss all of those eras.

summary: Seychelles was administered as a dependency of Mauritius from 1810 and used Mauritius stamps from 1848, identifiable only by the 'B64' obliterator; the first post office opened at Victoria, Mahe, on 11 December 1861. The first stamps actually inscribed SEYCHELLES appeared 5 April 1890, Queen Victoria key types, with surcharges in 1893 and 1901. The islands became a separate Crown Colony in 1903 and independent in 1976. A parallel outer-islands issuer existed 1980-1992 under three successive Creole names, catalogued separately from Seychelles proper.

periods.label: Mauritius stamps used in Seychelles
periods.authority: Colonial post of Mauritius (British)
periods.note: No Seychelles-inscribed stamps. Identified by the numeral obliterator B64. Catalogued under Mauritius / 'Mauritius used abroad', NOT under Seychelles.

periods.label: British colony, dependency of Mauritius
periods.authority: Seychelles postal administration under Mauritius
periods.note: QV key types inscribed SEYCHELLES; surcharges 1893, 1901.

periods.label: Separate British Crown Colony
periods.authority: Seychelles Post Office (Crown Colony)
periods.note: KEVII through QEII key types and pictorials.

periods.label: Republic of Seychelles
periods.authority: Seychelles Postal Administration / Seychelles Post
periods.note: Independence 29 June 1976; one-party socialist state 1977-1993, then multiparty republic. Issuing continues.

periods.label: Seychelles Outer Islands (Zil Eloigne / Elwagne / Elwannyen Sesel)
periods.authority: Seychelles Postal Administration, separate outer-islands issue
periods.note: For Aldabra, Coetivy, Farquhar and the Amirantes, served by the travelling post office on M.V. Cinq-Juin. Catalogued as a distinct entity in Scott and SG Commonwealth (Indian Ocean).

successors: Seychelles Outer Islands (Zil Elwannyen Sesel) - parallel issuer, not a successor
British Indian Ocean Territory (Aldabra, Farquhar and Desroches were detached to BIOT 1965-1976 and used BIOT stamps)
inscriptions: SESEL (Seychellois Creole form on some modern issues)
gaps: No true gap in issuing since 1890; no wartime interruption. The only era invisible to a country-keyed search is 1848-1890, when only Mauritius stamps were used and the material is catalogued under Mauritius.
forgery_risk: Low to moderate. Early QV key types and the 1893/1901 surcharges attract forged and altered surcharges and faked cancellations. The highest-risk item is the B64 obliterator faked onto Mauritius stamps, since that mark alone converts an ordinary Mauritius stamp into a valuable Seychelles-usage item.
wallpaper_risk: Moderate but not extreme. Seychelles has never appeared on UPU illegal-issue circulars and is NOT an IGPC/Stamperija wallpaper country. However the 1980-1992 Zil Eloigne/Elwagne/Elwannyen Sesel outer-islands programme was explicitly a revenue-raising thematic operation aimed at foreign collectors (flora, fauna, ships) covering a genuine but tiny postal need, and is regarded by some as semi-agency material. Annual issue counts stayed modest, so it does not reach true wallpaper status.
count_source: Approximation from Stanley Gibbons Commonwealth & British Empire Stamps / SG Indian Ocean listing, cross-checked against Scott. Order of magnitude only, +/- 20%.
uncertain: Exact major-number total not verified against a physical catalogue edition; figure covers Seychelles proper and excludes the roughly 230 Zil Elwannyen Sesel numbers, which catalogues carry separately. The exact date on which Mauritius stamps ceased to be valid in Seychelles (whether they ran alongside the 1890 issue) was not established.

summary: Singapore had no stamps of its own until 1 September 1948; before that its mail carried stamps of India (from 1854), then Straits Settlements (from 1867), Japanese occupation issues (1942-45), and B.M.A. MALAYA overprints (from 19 Oct 1945). The 1948 KGVI definitives inscribed SINGAPORE / MALAYA are the first true Singapore issues. Inscriptions then track constitutional status: STATE OF SINGAPORE 1959-63, a Malaysia period 1963-65, then SINGAPORE from independence. Nearly the whole pre-1948 record is catalogued under Straits Settlements, which hides it from a country-keyed search.

periods.label: Indian stamps used
periods.authority: Post Office of India
periods.note: Identifiable only by Singapore cancellations (B-172 obliterator).

periods.label: Straits Settlements
periods.authority: Straits Settlements Crown Colony
periods.note: Singapore was the colony's capital; all its stamps of this era are catalogued under Straits Settlements.

periods.label: Japanese occupation (Syonan)
periods.authority: Imperial Japanese Military Administration
periods.note: Overprints on Straits Settlements stamps and Japanese issues.

periods.label: British Military Administration
periods.authority: BMA Malaya
periods.note: Straits Settlements stamps overprinted 'B.M.A. MALAYA', issued 19 Oct 1945, used until 1948.

periods.label: Crown Colony of Singapore
periods.authority: Singapore Post Office, within the Malayan Postal Union
periods.note: First stamps 1 Sept 1948; inscribed SINGAPORE with MALAYA.

periods.label: State of Singapore (self-government)
periods.authority: State of Singapore
periods.note: Inscription changes to STATE OF SINGAPORE.

periods.label: Within Malaysia
periods.authority: State of Singapore within the Federation of Malaysia
periods.note: Singapore continued its own stamps; catalogued under Singapore, not Malaysia.

periods.label: Republic of Singapore
periods.authority: Singapore Post (SingPost) / formerly Postal Services Department
periods.note: Brief 'REPUBLIC OF SINGAPORE' inscription in 1966, then plain SINGAPORE.

inscriptions: SINGAPURA (Malay form, appears on some issues)
Occasional four-language issues: English, Chinese, Malay (Jawi and Rumi), Tamil
gaps: No gap once issuing began in 1948. Before 1948 Singapore has no issues of its own at all — a 94-year blank in any Singapore-keyed catalogue run, filled by India, Straits Settlements, Japanese occupation and BMA material catalogued elsewhere.
forgery_risk: Modest for Singapore's own issues. The real risk sits in the predecessor material used in Singapore: Japanese occupation overprints and B.M.A. MALAYA overprints are heavily forged, as are Straits Settlements early overprints. Among Singapore-proper issues, the 1948 Royal Silver Wedding and higher KGVI/QEII definitive values attract faked cancels and regumming rather than outright forgery.
wallpaper_risk: No. Singapore is a disciplined, low-volume issuer with a strong domestic collector base. It has never been an agency-driven producer and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars.
count_source: StampWorld online catalogue, major numbers for Singapore 1948-present. Approximate; Scott and Stanley Gibbons totals will differ, partly because they treat definitive redrawings and self-adhesive variants differently.
uncertain: Catalogue totals not verified against a printed Scott or Gibbons volume. Whether Singapore issued any stamp actually inscribed 'MALAYSIA' during the 1963-65 union was not confirmed — the sources consulted say Singapore continued its own inscriptions and the Malaysia-formation commemorative was a joint design, but this should be checked. Trap to flag: 'Singapore #1' in most catalogues is the 1948 KGVI 1c, which routinely misleads people into thinking the island had no earlier postal history at all.

summary: Slovene territory used Austrian stamps until 1918. Its first distinct stamps were the 'Verigar' (Chainbreaker) issues of the State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs, first printing 3 January 1919 from Ljubljana, designed by Ivan Vavpotic. These are catalogued under Yugoslavia in most catalogues, NOT under Slovenia — a major trap. Modern Slovenia began issuing on 26 June 1991; Yugoslav stamps stayed valid until April 1992.

periods.label: Austrian period
periods.authority: Austrian post (k.k. Post), Austria-Hungary
periods.note: Generic Austrian stamps; identifiable only by cancellation. No Slovene-specific issues.

periods.label: State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs (Verigar/Chainbreakers)
periods.authority: SHS postal administration, Ljubljana
periods.note: First printing 3 Jan 1919 (Ljubljana lithography, 8 values, 3-40 heller); second printing 8 Apr 1919 (letterpress, 14 values to 10 kronen), printed Ljubljana and Vienna. Catalogued under Yugoslavia.

periods.label: Kingdom of SHS / Yugoslavia
periods.authority: Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes, then Kingdom of Yugoslavia
periods.note: Unified Yugoslav issues replace Slovene-specific ones.

periods.label: Italian occupation - Province of Ljubljana
periods.authority: Italian civil administration, Provincia di Lubiana
periods.note: Mostly overprints on Yugoslav stock. Catalogued under Italy/Ljubljana, not Slovenia.

periods.label: German occupation - Lower Styria and Provinz Laibach
periods.authority: German military/civil administration
periods.note: Overprints on Italian and Yugoslav stock.

periods.label: SFR Yugoslavia
periods.authority: Yugoslav PTT
periods.note: No Slovene-specific issues.

periods.label: Free Territory of Trieste Zone B (partial overlap)
periods.authority: Yugoslav Military Government / STT VUJA
periods.note: Affects only a small coastal strip; catalogued under Trieste, not Slovenia.

periods.label: Republic of Slovenia
periods.authority: Posta Slovenije
periods.note: First issues 26 June 1991.

predecessors: State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs
inscriptions: Verigar issues also appear with Cyrillic ДРЖАВА С.Х.С.
gaps: No Slovene-specific issues 1921-1941 (subsumed into Kingdom of SHS/Yugoslavia) and 1945-1991 (SFR Yugoslavia). Occupation issues 1941-45 are catalogued under Italy/Germany. These are administrative gaps, not postal collapse.
forgery_risk: The 1919 Verigar/Chainbreaker series is heavily forged and, more commonly, faked by perforation and paper manipulation. The series was printed on some 36 paper types with wildly variable perforation, which makes fabricated 'rare varieties' easy and expert certificates essential for high values. Modern Slovenia (post-1991) has no significant forgery problem.
wallpaper_risk: No. Slovenia does NOT have an agency-driven or excessive-issue reputation. Posta Slovenije issues a modest, restrained programme and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars.
count_source: Not established. Colnect and Stampworld return pagination figures rather than a stated major-number total, and I could not verify a Michel or Scott count. Michel Europa Band 4 would be the correct authority.
uncertain: BATCH FILE MISMATCH: /private/tmp/.../scratchpad/profiles/batch_15.txt contains Palestine (327), Yemen (308), Congo Republic (298), Malta (274) — NOT the four countries named in the prompt. Slovenia appears in batch_21.txt with 110 records held. Please confirm which list is authoritative. Also unverified: exact total catalogue count; the precise date Yugoslav stamps ceased validity (sources say April 1992 without a day). Myth flag: the 1919 Chainbreakers are frequently sold as 'the first stamps of Slovenia' — they are State of SHS issues used in the Slovene region and are catalogued under Yugoslavia, so a country-keyed search on 'Slovenia' will silently miss the entire 1919-21 era.

summary: The Union of South Africa's first stamp is the November 1910 Opening of the Union Parliament commemorative (SG 1); its first definitives inscribed for the country appeared only in 1913. Between 31 May 1910 and 31 August 1913 the stamps of the four constituent colonies circulated interchangeably — the collected 'Interprovincial Period'. From 1926 many issues appear as bilingual English/Afrikaans pairs, and the country became a Republic in 1961. All pre-1910 material is catalogued under the individual colonies, which hides six decades from a South Africa country search.

periods.label: Constituent British colonies
periods.authority: Cape of Good Hope, Natal, Transvaal, Orange Free State (each separately)
periods.note: Catalogued as separate countries; Cape triangulars from 1853

periods.label: Union of South Africa, interprovincial
periods.authority: Union of South Africa Post Office
periods.note: Colonial stamps used union-wide; single Union commemorative 1910

periods.label: Union of South Africa
periods.authority: Union of South Africa Post Office
periods.note: Bilingual pairs from 1926

periods.label: Republic of South Africa
periods.authority: South African Post Office / SAPO
periods.note: Afrikaans-English, later English-only and multilingual

gaps: None. Continuous issuing since 1910. The only quasi-gap is 1910-13, when the Union issued just the single 1910 commemorative and relied on colonial stocks.
forgery_risk: Pre-Union material is the risk: Cape of Good Hope triangulars (especially the 'woodblock' 1d/4d errors of colour), Transvaal and Orange Free State overprints, Mafeking siege issues, and Stellaland/New Republic are all extensively forged. Union-era material is relatively safe apart from faked overprints and the 1941-46 war-effort varieties.
wallpaper_risk: South Africa itself: no — it is a conventional national issuer with no agency arrangement and no UPU illegal-issue listing. Caveat: the four nominally independent homelands (Transkei, Bophuthatswana, Venda, Ciskei, 1976-1994) issued heavily for the collector market and are sometimes described as wallpaper; they are catalogued separately and ceased in 1994.
count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (Union plus Republic, all sections; estimate)
uncertain: The total is an estimate. Two traps: (a) all 1853-1910 material is catalogued under the individual colonies, not South Africa; (b) South West Africa / Namibia and the four homelands are separate catalogue countries despite being South African-administered. A common error is treating the 1913 Springbok definitives as South Africa's first stamps — the 1910 Union commemorative precedes them.

count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (Spain); Edifil (the Spanish national catalogue) is the specialist standard and numbers differently
summary: Spain issued its first stamps on 1 January 1850, lithographed with a portrait of Isabel II. A key identification trap: the earliest issues do NOT bear the country name — they carry a year date and either FRANCO or CERTIFICADO — and ESPAÑA only appears reliably from the later 1860s. Spain has issued continuously since 1850 with no gaps, including through the Civil War, when Nationalist and Republican authorities issued concurrently. It is one of the most heavily forged classic countries in the world.

periods.label: Kingdom of Isabel II
periods.authority: Spanish Crown postal administration (Correos)
periods.note: First issue 1 January 1850. Early issues lack the country name.

periods.label: Provisional Government and Regency
periods.authority: Provisional revolutionary government after the Glorious Revolution
periods.note: ESPAÑA inscriptions, allegorical figures replace the deposed queen.

periods.label: Reign of Amadeo I
periods.authority: Kingdom of Spain under Amadeo of Savoy

periods.label: First Republic
periods.authority: República Española
periods.note: Concurrent Carlist issues in the north during the Third Carlist War.

periods.label: Bourbon Restoration
periods.authority: Kingdom of Spain, Alfonso XII then Alfonso XIII
periods.note: Long Alfonso XIII definitive era.

periods.label: Second Republic
periods.authority: República Española
periods.note: Overprints on monarchy stamps, then republican designs.

periods.label: Civil War — concurrent authorities
periods.authority: Republican government (Madrid/Valencia/Barcelona) and Nationalist government (Burgos), plus numerous local, provincial and municipal issues
periods.note: The most complex and most faked period. Vast quantities of local charity and provincial issues, many of doubtful status.

periods.label: Francoist State
periods.authority: Estado Español
periods.note: Inscribed ESTADO ESPAÑOL on many issues.

periods.label: Kingdom of Spain (constitutional monarchy)
periods.authority: Correos y Telégrafos / Correos
periods.note: Juan Carlos I, then Felipe VI from 2014.

inscriptions: FRANCO (on 1850-1860s issues, in place of a country name)
gaps: None — Spain has issued every year since 1850. There is no national gap even during the Civil War, when both sides issued simultaneously rather than either side ceasing. The apparent 'gaps' a collector encounters are usually the result of material being catalogued under a separate heading (Carlist issues, Civil War local issues, or the colonies).
forgery_risk: Very high, and among the most serious in world philately. Postal forgeries — stamps forged at the time to defraud the post office — began within 90 days of the first issue, with an Alicante case in April 1850; more than 200 distinct postal forgeries are recorded from 1850-1880, and these are themselves collectable. Philatelic forgeries are equally abundant: Sperati produced dangerous reproductions of Spanish classics, and Seguí is the other name specialists watch for. The 1850 6-cuartos and the 1851 2-reales are particularly targeted. The Civil War period is a minefield of bogus local overprints, fantasy issues and privately produced labels with no postal validity — a great deal of 1936-39 'Spain' material was never valid for postage at all.
wallpaper_risk: Spain does NOT have an agency-driven or excessive-issue reputation and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Modern output is moderate and legitimately postal. The historical excess is not agency wallpaper but the Civil War local and charity issues, which were locally produced rather than commercially manufactured for collectors.
uncertain: The total count is an estimate; Edifil and Scott number Spain quite differently, and the figure swings substantially depending on whether Civil War local issues and the extensive charity/tax obligatory labels are included. The precise inscription sequence on the 1850-1854 issues is best verified against a specialist Edifil listing. Note also that Spanish colonial material (Cuba, Puerto Rico, Philippines, Spanish Morocco, Fernando Po, Rio Muni, Spanish Sahara, Ifni) is catalogued under those names and not under Spain, hiding whole eras from a Spain-keyed search.

count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue, which numbers Ceylon and Sri Lanka in one continuous run (approximate major numbers through the early 2020s); Stanley Gibbons Part 1 similarly runs Ceylon into Sri Lanka
summary: British Ceylon issued its first stamps on 1 April 1857, a 6d imperforate Queen Victoria; the imperforate and early perforated Victorians include the celebrated 4d 'Dull Rose' of 1859. Issues were inscribed CEYLON from 1857 through independence (1948) and the Dominion period, and the name changed to SRI LANKA with the republican constitution of 22 May 1972. A collector searching only 'Sri Lanka' will miss 115 years of material catalogued as Ceylon in older catalogues and dealer stock.

periods.label: Crown Colony of Ceylon
periods.authority: British colonial Post Office, Ceylon
periods.note: Queen Victoria to King George VI; also postal fiscals and revenue-postage usage.

periods.label: Dominion of Ceylon
periods.authority: Ceylon Post Office
periods.note: Independence 4 Feb 1948; stamps still inscribed CEYLON, increasingly with Sinhala and Tamil text alongside English.

periods.label: Republic of Sri Lanka
periods.authority: Department of Posts, Sri Lanka
periods.note: Name change on stamps from 22 May 1972.

periods.label: Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka
periods.authority: Department of Posts, Sri Lanka
periods.note: Continuous issuing through the civil war (1983-2009) and after.

predecessors: Ceylon (same entity under its former name)
inscriptions: Sinhala ලංකා on some pre-1972 issues alongside CEYLON
Trilingual Sinhala/Tamil/English text is characteristic of modern issues
gaps: No complete years without issues. Output was thin during both World Wars and during periods of the 1983-2009 civil war, but never ceased.
forgery_risk: The 1857-67 imperforate and early perforated Victorians are extensively forged and, more dangerously, widely reperforated, repaired and regummed; the 4d 'Dull Rose' and the high-value imperforates are prime targets. Cancelled-to-order and cleaned postal-fiscal material is a persistent problem. Modern Sri Lanka has no significant bogus-overprint tradition.
wallpaper_risk: No. The Department of Posts controls its own programme and issue counts are moderate. Sri Lanka does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars and has no IGPC/Stamperija agency arrangement.
uncertain: The exact total is uncertain because catalogues split Ceylon and Sri Lanka differently and treat the large postal-fiscal group inconsistently. Also unverified in this research: the precise date from which a Sinhala 'ලංකා' inscription became standard on Ceylon issues — commonly given as the late 1950s, but I could not confirm a specific year from a reliable source, so treat any 1958 date as unverified.

count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (regular postage, semi-postals incl. Pro Juventute/Pro Patria, airmails, officials) through the 2020s; Zumstein/SBK gives a differently structured, generally higher count
summary: Switzerland is the third stamp-issuing territory in the world: the canton of Zurich issued the 'Zurich 4' and 'Zurich 6' on 1 March 1843, followed by Geneva (30 September 1843, the 'Double Geneva') and Basel (1845, the embossed 'Basel Dove', inscribed STADT POST BASEL). Federal issues valid throughout the country began 7 April 1850 under the new federal post. The Latin form HELVETIA (from Confoederatio Helvetica) is used precisely to avoid privileging German, French, Italian or Romansh, and is the single most reliable identifier. Switzerland has issued continuously since 1850 with no wartime gap, and additionally hosts a very large body of international-organisation issues (SDN/League of Nations, BIT/ILO, UPU, WHO, UN Geneva) that are catalogued separately.

periods.label: Cantonal issues
periods.authority: Cantons of Zurich, Geneva and Basel (independent cantonal posts)
periods.note: Zurich 1843, Geneva 1843, Basel 1845. Basel issued only one stamp. Valid only within/from the issuing canton.

periods.label: Transitional federal ('Rayon') issues
periods.authority: Swiss federal post (Eidgenossische Post) under the 1848 constitution
periods.note: Ortspost, Poste Locale and Rayon I/II/III issues; cross and horn design, no country name at all on the earliest.

periods.label: Strubeli / seated and standing Helvetia
periods.authority: Swiss federal post
periods.note: First appearance of HELVETIA as the country inscription.

periods.label: Modern Confederation
periods.authority: Swiss PTT (to 1997), then Die Post / La Poste / La Posta / Swiss Post
periods.note: Continuous. Pro Juventute annual charity series from 1913, Pro Patria from 1938.

periods.label: International organisation issues in Switzerland
periods.authority: League of Nations, ILO, UPU, WHO, ITU, WIPO, WMO, UN Office at Geneva
periods.note: Catalogued separately from Switzerland proper in Scott and Zumstein; UN Geneva (from 1969) is catalogued under United Nations, not Switzerland.

inscriptions: SCHWEIZ (used alongside Helvetia on some 20th-century commemoratives)
POSTE DE GENEVE / PORT CANTONAL / PORT LOCAL (Geneva 1843)
SOCIETE DES NATIONS / S.d.N. and B.I.T. (overprints on Swiss stamps for international bodies)
gaps: None. Switzerland has issued stamps every year since 1850 and was neutral in both world wars. Individual cantonal issuers stopped (Zurich and Geneva 1850, Basel earlier in practice) but the territory was never without stamps.
forgery_risk: Very high at the classic end. The three cantonal issues (Zurich 4/6, Double Geneva, Basel Dove) are among the most heavily forged stamps in world philately, including Fournier and Sperati productions; certificates are effectively mandatory. Rayon and Ortspost/Poste Locale issues are widely forged and also widely repaired/reperforated. Cancellations on cantonals and Rayons are forged more often than the stamps. Fournier also produced large quantities of Swiss 'facsimiles'. Modern era is comparatively clean, but forged cancels on used high-value Pro Juventute and airmail sets, and faked overprints on the League of Nations/ILO officials, are known.
wallpaper_risk: None. Switzerland has no agency-driven or excessive-issue reputation, does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars, and its annual programme is modest and philatelically conservative. Pro Juventute/Pro Patria surcharges are genuine charity issues with real postal validity, not agency product.
uncertain: BATCH LIST CONFLICT: the file at scratchpad/profiles/batch_04.txt lists Finland (2532 records), Armenia (2358), Switzerland (1930), Malaysia (1701); the task message named Switzerland, Italy, Peru, Denmark. Only Switzerland is common to both. I have profiled all seven so neither reading is lost — the parent should confirm which list is authoritative before ingesting. CATALOGUE TRAP: Swiss early material hides in three places — the 1843-45 cantonals are catalogued under Switzerland in Scott but under separate headings (Zurich, Geneva, Basel) in Zumstein/SBK; the League of Nations, ILO, UPU, WHO etc. issues are catalogued as separate entities though they are Swiss-printed and Swiss-valid; and UN Geneva (1969-) is catalogued under United Nations. A country-keyed search on 'Switzerland' will silently miss all of these. MYTH: the Basel Dove is often called the world's first multicoloured stamp — it is three-colour printed with embossing and that claim is defensible, but the frequently repeated claim that it is the first embossed stamp is wrong. The exact total-issue count varies by more than 20% between Scott and Zumstein because Zumstein splits types Scott lumps; treat 1900 as an order of magnitude, not a precise figure. Records held (1930) is very close to my Scott-based estimate, which is consistent but should not be read as confirmation.

summary: Modern Tanzania is a 1964 union of Tanganyika and Zanzibar, and its philately is really four separate catalogue countries stacked on one territory. The first stamps issued for the mainland were German East Africa in 1893 (German keytypes surcharged in pesa), while Zanzibar had its own stamps from 1895 and foreign post offices earlier still. Mainland material is catalogued under German East Africa, Tanganyika, and then Kenya Uganda and Tanganyika (KUT) for the whole 1935-1963 stretch, which is the single biggest trap for a country-keyed search. Tanzania proper issues from 1964/65 and has a serious agency and illegal-issue problem from the mid-1990s onward.

periods.label: German East Africa
periods.authority: German colonial postal administration (Deutsche Reichspost / Deutsch-Ostafrika)
periods.note: 1893 German keytypes surcharged in pesa; from 1896 inscribed DEUTSCH-OSTAFRIKA. Catalogued as German East Africa, not Tanzania.

periods.label: British occupation of German East Africa
periods.authority: British and Belgian occupying military administrations
periods.note: 'N.F.' (Nyasaland-Rhodesian Field Force) overprints on Nyasaland 1916; 'G.E.A.' overprints on East Africa and Uganda Protectorates stamps from 1917. Belgian occupation of the north-west is catalogued separately under Ruanda-Urundi / Belgian Congo occupation issues.

periods.label: Tanganyika Territory (British mandate)
periods.authority: Tanganyika Territory postal administration under British League of Nations mandate
periods.note: G.E.A. overprints with Crown/Script CA watermark 1921-22 are treated as Tanganyika's first issues; 1922 giraffe-head definitives inscribed TANGANYIKA.

periods.label: Kenya, Uganda and Tanganyika joint administration
periods.authority: East African Posts and Telecommunications Administration
periods.note: Stamps inscribed KENYA UGANDA TANGANYIKA (later KENYA UGANDA TANGANYIKA ZANZIBAR). Catalogued under Kenya Uganda and Tanganyika (KUT) - 28 years of Tanganyika material invisible to a Tanzania-keyed search.

periods.label: Independent Tanganyika
periods.authority: Government of Tanganyika
periods.note: Independence commemoratives inscribed TANGANYIKA alongside continuing KUT definitives.

periods.label: Zanzibar (parallel entity)
periods.authority: Sultanate of Zanzibar under British protection; from 1964 revolutionary government
periods.note: First stamps 1895, British India overprinted ZANZIBAR. Indian and French post offices operated there earlier (from the 1870s-1890s) using unoverprinted Indian and French stamps. Zanzibar issued separately after the 1964 revolution until absorbed.

periods.label: United Republic of Tanzania
periods.authority: Tanzania Posts Corporation (earlier East African Posts and Telecommunications to 1977)
periods.note: 1964-1976 issues largely appear as joint Kenya/Uganda/Tanzania East African Community issues as well as national Tanzania issues; fully independent national issuing after the EAC collapse in 1977.

inscriptions: DEUTSCHES REICH (1893 surcharged keytypes, before the colony name was added)
TANZANIA KENYA UGANDA (East African Community joint issues)
gaps: No true nationwide gap, but there is no stamp inscribed for the mainland alone between 1935 and 1961 - the territory was served by joint KUT issues. Zanzibar had no stamps of its own before 1895 despite an active mail service via Indian and French offices.
forgery_risk: German East Africa surcharges and the 1916 N.F. and 1917 G.E.A. overprints are all classic overprint-forgery targets and should be expert-certified. Zanzibar's 1895-96 British India overprints exist with numerous forged overprints and forged cancels. Zanzibar 1964 revolutionary overprints also exist in bogus and privately made forms. Beware also the large body of fantasy 'Zanzibar' material produced by agencies in the 1990s that has no postal status.
wallpaper_risk: Yes - substantial. Tanzania has one of Africa's worse reputations for agency-driven output, with heavy CTO topical sets from the 1980s onward and a large body of outright illegal issues produced in its name from the mid-1990s. The UPU has issued International Bureau circulars denouncing illegal stamps in the name of Tanzania (including IB Circular 219/2011), and illegal 'Zanzibar' issues have likewise been denounced. Dedicated illegal-issue listings for Tanzania are maintained by philatelic reference sites.
count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (Tanzania listing only; excludes German East Africa, Tanganyika, KUT and Zanzibar, which are separate country listings). Figure is a rough order of magnitude, not a verified count.
uncertain: TASK DISCREPANCY: the batch file at scratchpad/profiles/batch_10.txt lists Philippines, Belgium, Kyrgyzstan and South Africa, not the four countries named in my instructions. I profiled the countries named in the instruction. Please confirm which list is authoritative. On Tanzania specifically: I could not verify a precise catalogued total - published counts vary widely because catalogues differ on whether to admit the disputed 1990s agency issues, and Scott has delisted material at various points. The exact number of UPU circulars naming Tanzania is not established here beyond IB Circular 219/2011. Myth flag: it is often said Tanzania's stamps 'begin in 1961 or 1964' - collectors should be aware that essentially all mainland material from 1935 to 1963 sits under Kenya Uganda and Tanganyika, and pre-1916 material under German East Africa.

summary: Siam's first stamps, the Solot series of six values printed in recess by Waterlow and Sons, were issued on 4 August 1883, the day the national post office opened. They carry Thai script only, with no country name or value in Roman letters, because Siam was not yet a UPU member; joining the UPU in 1885 forced their replacement in 1887. Issuing has been continuous ever since under a single sovereign authority — Thailand was never colonised — with the country name changing from SIAM to THAILAND (1939), briefly back to SIAM (1945-49), then permanently THAILAND. Thailand also produced occupation issues for four Malay states in 1943-45.

periods.label: British Consular Post Office, Bangkok
periods.authority: British Post Office / Straits Settlements
periods.note: Straits Settlements stamps used at the Bangkok consular office, cancelled 'B' in oval. Pre-dates Siam's own stamps; catalogued under Straits Settlements or as 'British Post Offices in Siam'.

periods.label: Kingdom of Siam (Rama V onward)
periods.authority: Siamese Post and Telegraph Department
periods.note: Solot series 1883; UPU membership 1885; Roman-lettered issues from 1887.

periods.label: Thailand (first name change)
periods.authority: Kingdom of Thailand
periods.note: Country renamed 1939; stamps inscribed THAILAND.

periods.label: Thai occupation of Malaya (Si Rat Malai)
periods.authority: Kingdom of Thailand, administering Kedah, Kelantan, Perlis and Trengganu
periods.note: Overprints for Kelantan (Nov 1943) and a War Memorial set inscribed THAILAND but in Malayan cents (Dec 1943). Catalogued as Thailand occupation issues (Scott 2N-series).

periods.label: Siam (name reverted)
periods.authority: Kingdom of Siam
periods.note: Name reverted after WWII; stamps again inscribed SIAM.

periods.label: Thailand (present)
periods.authority: Thailand Post (formerly Communications Authority of Thailand)
periods.note: Name permanently THAILAND from 1949.

predecessors: British Post Offices in Siam (Straits Settlements stamps used at Bangkok, 1882-85)
inscriptions: Thai script only on the 1883 Solot series — no Roman lettering, no country name
ประเทศไทย (Prathet Thai)
ไปรษณีย์ไทย (Thailand Post)
ไปรษณียากร (postage)
Currency units: solot, att, siao, sik, fueang, salueng, tical, satang, baht
Note the script change direction: Thai-only 1883-87, then Thai plus Roman from 1887 to comply with UPU rules
gaps: No years without issues since 1883. The 1883-87 Solot period is effectively a domestic-only era (no international-standard inscriptions). No wartime interruption — Thailand kept issuing throughout WWII as a nominally sovereign Japanese ally, unlike its neighbours.
forgery_risk: Significant in the classic period. The 1885 '1 Tical' surcharges on the 1 solot exist in three types and are widely forged. Counterfeit overprints and surcharges are common across the 1889-1899 provisional issues, which are numerous and were made under pressure of currency shortage — expertisation is standard practice for these. Faked cancellations on the 1883 Solot series (used copies price higher than mint in some values) are also a known problem. Siam Scout Fund issues have documented fakes and forgeries.
wallpaper_risk: No. Thailand Post is a conventional national issuer with steady, moderate output and no agency involvement. It does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. There is no IGPC or Stamperija connection.
count_source: StampWorld online catalogue, major numbers for Thailand 1883-2026. Approximate. Scott's total is lower because it treats some Thai definitive re-issues and colour changes as minor varieties.
uncertain: Catalogue totals not verified against a printed Scott or Stanley Gibbons volume; treat the count as approximate to within ±20%. The precise sequencing of the 1945-49 SIAM/THAILAND inscription reversion (which specific issues carry which name) was not verified issue by issue. Trap to flag: everything before 1939 is catalogued under 'Siam' in older albums and in some dealer stock, so a search keyed on 'Thailand' can miss 56 years of the most valuable material. Second trap: Straits Settlements stamps cancelled at the Bangkok British consular office 1882-85 are Thai postal history but are catalogued under Straits Settlements, not Thailand.

summary: The Ottoman Empire issued its first adhesive stamps on 1 January 1863 — the Tughra issue, showing Sultan Abdulaziz's monogram over a crescent inscribed Devlet-i Aliyye-i Osmaniyye in Arabic script. Turkey is catalogued as a single continuous country from 1863 through the Empire, the 1923 Republic and the present, so no era is orphaned. The decisive identification break is the alphabet reform: Arabic script gives way to Latin from the mid-1920s, with Latin-only inscriptions such as TURKIYE CUMHURIYETI from 1929. 1920-22 is genuinely difficult because two rival authorities, the Sultan's government at Constantinople and the Nationalists at Ankara, issued simultaneously.

periods.label: Ottoman Empire
periods.authority: Ottoman Post (Devlet-i Aliyye-i Osmaniyye)
periods.note: Tughra a design element throughout to 1922

periods.label: Dual authorities
periods.authority: Constantinople (Sultanate) and Ankara (Grand National Assembly / Nationalists)
periods.note: Concurrent, competing issues

periods.label: Republic of Turkey, Arabic script
periods.authority: Turkish Post, Republic of Turkey
periods.note: Transitional; Latin appears from the 1926 London issue

periods.label: Republic of Turkey, Latin script
periods.authority: PTT / Turkish Post (now PTT A.S.)
periods.note: Latin-only inscriptions

inscriptions: EMP. OTTOMAN / POSTES OTTOMANES (French, on some issues)
gaps: No year without issues since 1863. The 1920-22 period is not a gap but the opposite — overlapping issues from two competing governments.
forgery_risk: Substantial. The early Tughra and Duloz issues are forged, and Ottoman overprints of every kind (postal-service, war-charity, provisional surcharges) are a classic forgery field, as are the 1920-22 Nationalist overprints. Cilicia, Alexandretta and Hatay overprints attract many forged and bogus varieties.
wallpaper_risk: No. Turkey is a conventional national issuer with no agency arrangement, no IGPC/Stamperija involvement, and no UPU illegal-issue listing.
count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (Turkey regular, semi-postal, airmail, official and postage due sections; estimate)
uncertain: The total is an estimate. Traps: Ottoman post offices abroad, Cilicia, Alexandretta, Hatay, and the Levant offices of foreign powers operating in Ottoman territory are all separate catalogue entities and will not appear under a Turkey key. The exact first Latin-script issue is commonly given as 1926 (third London series) but the transition ran to 1929, and some references date it differently; treat the 1926 date as approximate rather than definitive.

summary: Ukraine's first stamps are the 1918 Ukrainian People's Republic issues: trident overprints applied to Imperial Russian stamps from August 1918, plus the 1918 'Shahy' pictorial definitives. A separate short-lived West Ukrainian National Republic issued overprints on Austrian and Bosnian stamps in 1918-19. Ukrainian issues then cease for seventy years under the USSR, resuming with trident overprints on Soviet stamps in late 1991-92 and the first stamps of the independent state on 1 March 1992. The trident overprints are one of the most heavily forged fields in European philately.

periods.label: Russian Empire / RSFSR postal use
periods.authority: Imperial Russian Post
periods.note: Pre-1918 covers from Ukrainian territory bear Russian stamps and are catalogued under Russia (and under Russian Zemstvo posts for the rural locals).

periods.label: Ukrainian People's Republic (UNR) / Hetmanate
periods.authority: Ukrainian Ministry of Posts, Kyiv
periods.note: Trident overprints ordered by 20 Aug 1918 across six postal districts - Kyiv, Poltava, Katerynoslav, Kharkiv, Odesa, Podillia - plus the Shahy definitives and the 1920 Vienna issue (never postally used, prepared in exile).

periods.label: West Ukrainian National Republic (ZUNR)
periods.authority: ZUNR postal administration, Lviv/Stanislaviv
periods.note: Overprints on Austrian and Bosnian stamps; catalogued as a separate country in most catalogues - a genuine search trap.

periods.label: Soviet period
periods.authority: USSR Ministry of Communications (Ukrainian SSR had no separate stamps)
periods.note: No Ukrainian-inscribed stamps at all; a seven-decade gap.

periods.label: Carpatho-Ukraine
periods.authority: Carpatho-Ukraine autonomous government, 1939; Soviet Transcarpathian Ukraine, 1944-45
periods.note: Catalogued separately (also under Czechoslovakia and Hungary for adjacent periods).

periods.label: German occupation
periods.authority: Deutsche Post Osten / Reichskommissariat Ukraine
periods.note: 'UKRAINE' overprints on German stamps, catalogued under German Occupation Issues, not under Ukraine.

periods.label: Independent Ukraine
periods.authority: Ukrposhta
periods.note: Provisional trident overprints on Soviet stamps at Kyiv, Lviv, Chernihiv and elsewhere in 1992; first commemorative 1 March 1992 (Cossack 500th anniversary / emigration to Canada pair).

inscriptions: Trident emblem alone - the 1918 overprints carry no country name, only the tryzub, over Russian-inscribed stamps
UKRAINE / UKRAINA (Latin, on some modern issues)
UKRAINE (German occupation overprint on Deutsches Reich stamps, 1941-44)
Script note: Cyrillic throughout; the 1918 orthography differs from modern (НАРОДНЯ vs НАРОДНА).
gaps: 1921/23-1991: no Ukrainian issues during the Soviet period, when USSR stamps served the territory. Also a shorter effective gap 1920-21 as the UNR collapsed (the 1920 Vienna issue was prepared abroad and never validly used).
forgery_risk: Severe, and the defining problem of the area. The 1918 trident overprints - roughly 75 basic overprint types with 50-plus variants applied across some 20 values each, well over 1,500 combinations - have been forged since the 1920s, and the scarcer district types are forged more often than not. Expertisation is essential; Scott lists about 19 major numbers and around 500 minor numbers, and the minors are where the fakes concentrate. West Ukrainian overprints and the 1920 Vienna issue are also widely faked. Fantasy 'Ukrainian' labels from the 1919-21 civil war period circulate.
wallpaper_risk: No. Ukrposhta runs a conventional, moderate programme and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Wartime issues since 2022 have sold in very high quantities but are genuine postal issues, not agency wallpaper.
count_source: Approximate: modern Ukraine major numbers in Scott (1992 to date) plus the 1918-20 majors; Michel and the Ukrainian-language Kramarenko catalogue number differently. Not verified against a current edition.
uncertain: Total count not verified. Whether the 1918 trident overprints should be treated as one issue or as six district issues is a cataloguing choice, not a fact - counts vary enormously depending on whether minor numbers are included. Cataloguing traps: the West Ukrainian National Republic, Carpatho-Ukraine, and the German occupation 'UKRAINE' overprints are all filed under separate headings, and everything before 1918 is under Russia, so a country-keyed search on 'Ukraine' misses those eras entirely.

summary: The first stamps used on what is now UAE territory were Indian stamps at the Dubai post office, opened 19 August 1909 under the Indian Sind circle and identified by the 'DUBAI PERSIAN GULF' cancel. British-administered Trucial States stamps followed in 1961, then separate emirate issues from 1963. The UAE as a single issuer begins 1 January 1973. The emirate period 1964-72 produced the notorious 'Dunes' - vast quantities of agency-produced thematic labels with little or no postal use - which dominate the area's reputation.

periods.label: Indian postal administration
periods.authority: Indian Post Office (Sind circle), Dubai
periods.note: Indian stamps with 'DUBAI PERSIAN GULF' cancellations. Catalogued under India Used Abroad.

periods.label: Pakistani then British administration
periods.authority: Pakistan Post (1947-48), then British Postal Agencies in Eastern Arabia
periods.note: British stamps surcharged in Indian/Gulf currency, used at Dubai.

periods.label: Trucial States
periods.authority: British Postal Agencies in Eastern Arabia
periods.note: A single definitive set inscribed TRUCIAL STATES, issued 7 Jan 1961, used only at Dubai.

periods.label: Individual emirate issues
periods.authority: Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Sharjah, Ajman, Umm al-Quwain, Ras al-Khaima, Fujeira - each with its own (mostly agency-run) postal authority
periods.note: Dubai from 1963, Abu Dhabi 1964, then the northern emirates. The states were not UPU members. Printing contracts were terminated 31 July 1972.

periods.label: United Arab Emirates
periods.authority: Emirates Post / UAE General Postal Authority
periods.note: Unified issues from 1 January 1973; UAE joined the UPU in 1973.

predecessors: British Postal Agencies in Eastern Arabia
inscriptions: AJMAN / UMM AL QIWAIN / RAS AL KHAIMA / FUJEIRA (with Arabic equivalents)
Script note: Arabic and Latin bilingual throughout. Currency runs Indian rupee/naye paise, then Gulf rupee, then riyal/dirham (UAE dirham/fils from 1973).
gaps: No stamps of any local authority before 1961 - the territory used Indian and then British stamps, so 1909-1960 has postal history but no locally-inscribed issues. Brief transitional period late 1972 to 1 Jan 1973 between the end of emirate issues and the first UAE issues.
forgery_risk: Moderate for genuine material, but the dominant problem is authenticity of status rather than of printing. Post-1972 'stamps' of Ajman, Umm al-Quwain and others continued to be printed after the contracts were terminated and were sold in tourist packets; outright copies of earlier emirate issues and further bogus items were also produced. Genuine postally-used covers from the northern emirates are scarce and faked cancellations are common - a CTO or favour cancel is the norm and a real commercial cover is the exception.
wallpaper_risk: Yes - this is one of the textbook wallpaper areas. The 'Dunes' issues of Ajman, Umm al-Quwain, Sharjah, Ras al Khaima and Fujeira in the 1960s-early 1970s were produced by philatelic agencies purely for the collector market, in enormous quantity and with negligible postal need; they are colourful and essentially worthless, and most catalogues either omit them or list them in an appendix. The UAE's own post-1973 programme is by contrast conventional and legitimate, and the UAE government itself repudiated the post-1972 emirate emissions.
count_source: Approximate: Scott major numbers for United Arab Emirates 1973 to date, excluding the pre-1973 emirate issues (which Scott and Stanley Gibbons treat very differently - Gibbons declines to list most Dunes at all). Not verified against a current edition.
uncertain: Total count unverified, and any count for this area is unusually catalogue-dependent because the major catalogues disagree on whether the 1964-72 emirate material counts as stamps at all. The choice of 1909 as first_issue_year reflects the brief's rule of using the first stamps USED on the territory (Indian stamps at Dubai); if the criterion is the first locally-inscribed stamps, the answer is 1961 (Trucial States), and if it is the first stamps of the UAE as a state, 1973. Flag this explicitly in the catalogue rather than letting a single number stand. Cataloguing trap: nearly everything before 1973 sits under seven separate emirate headings plus 'Trucial States' and 'India Used Abroad', so a country-keyed search on 'United Arab Emirates' returns only the post-1973 era.

summary: Great Britain issued the world's first adhesive postage stamps on 1 May 1840 (valid 6 May): the Penny Black and Twopence Blue. As the originator, it holds the unique UPU privilege of omitting the country name - identification relies on the sovereign's head, which appears on every issue to date. There is no break in issuing since 1840. Catalogue coverage is complicated by the regional issues for Scotland, Wales, Northern Ireland, the Isle of Man, Guernsey and Jersey, and by the pre-1970 use of GB stamps in the Channel Islands and Man.

periods.label: Line-engraved and surface-printed Victorian issues
periods.authority: General Post Office, United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland
periods.note: Penny Black 1840; extensive plating, check letters and corner-letter systems make this the most intensively studied period in philately.

periods.label: Edwardian to George VI
periods.authority: General Post Office
periods.note: Ireland's separation in 1922 removes the south from GB coverage; Irish Free State overprints on GB stamps are catalogued under Ireland.

periods.label: Elizabeth II
periods.authority: General Post Office to 1969, then Post Office Corporation / Royal Mail
periods.note: Machin definitive head from 1967 - an enormous specialist field of its own. Decimal currency from 15 February 1971. Regional issues for Scotland, Wales, Northern Ireland from 1958.

periods.label: Charles III
periods.authority: Royal Mail
periods.note: New definitive head; barcoded definitives introduced and non-barcoded stamps withdrawn from validity in 2023.

inscriptions: No country name at all - the defining identification feature. The sovereign's effigy serves as the national mark.
Denominations only on modern Machins, plus 1ST / 2ND / LARGE / SIGNED FOR non-value indicators
Regional issues carry no name either, only national symbols; Wales issues have carried CYMRU
gaps: None. Great Britain has issued continuously since 1840 with no interruption for war or regime change.
forgery_risk: Considerable, but concentrated in specific areas rather than whole issues. The 1840-41 line-engraved issues attract reperforated, repaired and re-gummed material and faked plate/check-letter combinations more than outright forgeries. The 1847-54 embossed issues and the high-value surface-printed Victorians are faked. There is a large body of Victorian-era postal forgeries (notably the 1872 Stock Exchange 1s forgery). Modern concerns are dominated by counterfeit Machin definitives made to defraud Royal Mail rather than collectors, and by faked errors, missing colours and imperforate varieties. Cinderella and 'Penny Black reproduction' material is abundant and usually harmless if identified.
wallpaper_risk: No agency or illegal-issue problem - Royal Mail runs its own programme and has never appeared on UPU illegal circulars. However, Royal Mail is widely criticised within the hobby for a very high modern output of commemoratives, miniature sheets, retail booklets, smilers and licensed-character issues, which many collectors regard as commercially excessive even though all of it is postally valid.
count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue, Great Britain regular postage major numbers (approximate). British collectors use Stanley Gibbons, whose numbering is entirely different and whose specialised volumes run to many thousands more listings.
uncertain: TASK DISCREPANCY: the batch file lists Philippines, Belgium, Kyrgyzstan, South Africa rather than the countries in my instructions - please confirm which list governs. The stamp total is an approximation across catalogue editions and should not be treated as exact; Scott and SG differ substantially, and the Machin definitives alone can be counted several different ways. Catalogue trap: Channel Islands German occupation issues (Guernsey and Jersey 1941-45) are catalogued under those islands, not GB; British post offices abroad and 'used abroad' GB stamps with numeral cancels are catalogued under the relevant colonies and agencies; Ireland to 1922 is under GB, after which it is under Ireland.

summary: Postmasters' provisionals appeared first — New York and Baltimore on 14 and 15 July 1845, followed by New Haven, St. Louis and seven other offices through 1846; these are catalogued under the United States (Scott 1X-11X). The first federal issues, the 5c Franklin and 10c Washington authorised by the Act of 3 March 1847, were distributed on 1 July 1847. Issuing has been continuous since, under the Post Office Department to 1971 and the United States Postal Service thereafter. Confederate States issues (1861-65) are a separate catalogue country covering the same territory.

periods.label: Postmasters' provisionals
periods.authority: Individual local postmasters under the 1845 rate act
periods.note: Eleven offices; New York, Baltimore, New Haven, St. Louis, Providence, Alexandria, Annapolis, Boscawen, Brattleboro, Lockport, Millbury.

periods.label: Post Office Department, pre-USPS
periods.authority: United States Post Office Department
periods.note: First federal issue 1.7.1847; stamps demonetised and reissued 1851.

periods.label: Confederate States
periods.authority: Confederate States of America Post Office Department
periods.note: Separate catalogue country; includes CSA postmasters' provisionals.

periods.label: United States Postal Service
periods.authority: United States Postal Service (independent establishment from 1 July 1971)
periods.note: Forever stamps from 2007.

gaps: None. The United States has issued without interruption since 1847. The 1851 demonetisation of the 1847 issue is a validity change, not a gap. Within Confederate territory 1861-65 federal stamps were not sold, but the federal series itself continued.
forgery_risk: High in specific classic areas: the 1845-47 postmasters' provisionals (especially St. Louis 'Bears' and Alexandria) are heavily faked; the 1918 24c inverted Jenny (C3a) has numerous fakes and altered copies; regummed and reperforated classics are endemic; the 1869 pictorial inverts, the Columbians and Trans-Mississippi high values, and Kansas-Nebraska 1929 overprints are all commonly forged. Fake cancels to convert cheap mint to scarce used, and used-to-mint regumming, are the dominant risks rather than wholly bogus stamps. Local post / carrier stamps (Scott LO and 1L-x listings) contain many 19th-century fantasies from the Boyd and Hussey era.
wallpaper_risk: No. The USPS is a genuine postal administration with no philatelic agency arrangement and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. Output has risen sharply since the 1990s and self-adhesive collector-targeted issues are numerous, but this is high output, not wallpaper.
count_source: Scott Specialized Catalogue of United States Stamps and Covers — regular postage major numbers had passed 5,900 by the mid-2020s. Adding back-of-book (airmail C, special delivery E, postage due J, officials O, revenues R, parcel post Q, plus Confederate States) takes the total well past 7,000.
uncertain: The exact current Scott major-number total was not verified against a current edition and should be checked before printing; Scott, Michel USA and Stanley Gibbons differ substantially because Scott splits self-adhesive/coil/booklet formats into separate major numbers where Michel often does not, and Scott's back-of-book conventions are unique. Two traps for a country-keyed search: the Confederate States (1861-65) and the US Possessions (Canal Zone, Guam, Hawaii, Philippines, Puerto Rico, Cuba under US administration, Ryukyu Islands 1948-72) are all separate catalogue countries covering US-administered postal territory. Myth to flag: the 1918 inverted Jenny is often described as the only US invert — there are several earlier ones, including the 1869 15c, 24c and 30c pictorial inverts and the 1901 Pan-American inverts.

count_source: Scott Standard Postage Stamp Catalogue (Vietnam, incl. Democratic Republic and post-1976 Socialist Republic); Michel Sudostasien broadly agrees
summary: Vietnam's own stamps begin in 1945 with Viet Minh overprints on French Indochina stocks after the August Revolution, followed by Ho Chi Minh definitives in 1946. The territory then split into multiple concurrent issuing authorities — the Democratic Republic in the north, the State/Republic of Vietnam in the south from 1951, and the NLF/Provisional Revolutionary Government from 1963 — before reunification in July 1976 under a single Socialist Republic postal service. All pre-1945 material for the territory is catalogued under French Indochina, which hides the entire colonial era from a Vietnam-keyed search. Catalogues split the 1951-1975 southern material under a separate 'Vietnam, South' heading.

periods.label: French Indochina
periods.authority: Postes de l'Indochine (French colonial administration)
periods.note: Catalogued under Indo-China, not Vietnam. Earlier still, French Colonies general issues and French offices were used. Territory-specific precursors include Annam-Tonkin (1888).

periods.label: Viet Minh provisionals
periods.authority: Democratic Republic of Vietnam (Viet Minh government)
periods.note: Overprints on remaining French Indochina stock reading Viet-Nam Dan-Chu Cong-Hoa / VNDCCH.

periods.label: Democratic Republic of Vietnam (North)
periods.authority: DRV postal administration, Hanoi
periods.note: First Ho Chi Minh definitives 1946. Issues effectively suspended during the 1946-1951 resistance period.

periods.label: State of Vietnam / Republic of Vietnam (South)
periods.authority: Bao Dai State of Vietnam from 1951; Republic of Vietnam from 1955
periods.note: Catalogued separately as Vietnam, South. Ends with the fall of Saigon, April 1975.

periods.label: NLF / Provisional Revolutionary Government
periods.authority: National Front for the Liberation of South Vietnam (1963-1970); Provisional Revolutionary Government of the Republic of South Vietnam (1970-1976)
periods.note: Concurrent with RVN issues. Catalogued as Viet Cong / South Vietnam (National Front) in most catalogues.

periods.label: Socialist Republic of Vietnam
periods.authority: Vietnam Post (Buu chinh Viet Nam)
periods.note: July-August 1976 issues still bear the DRV name; later issues simply Viet Nam.

inscriptions: VIET NAM BUU-CHINH (State of Vietnam / Republic of Vietnam, South)
MT DP MIEN NAM VIET NAM (National Liberation Front)
CONG HOA MIEN NAM VIET NAM (Provisional Revolutionary Government)
gaps: No distinct Vietnamese issues before 1945 — French Indochina stamps served the territory. The DRV issued little or nothing between roughly 1946 and 1951 during the resistance war against the French, when postal operations were disrupted and the government was operating from the countryside. Southern (RVN) issues cease abruptly in April 1975.
forgery_risk: The 1945-1946 Viet Minh overprints on French Indochina stock are extensively forged — the overprint is simple typography on common host stamps, which makes fakery easy and cheap. Forged and privately applied overprints are common. Some early DRV issues exist in unofficial reprints, and the crude wartime printings are hard to authenticate. Expertisation is advisable for anything from the 1945-1946 provisional period.
wallpaper_risk: Vietnam does NOT have an IGPC or Stamperija agency reputation and does not appear on UPU illegal-issue circulars. However, the DRV and later Socialist Republic had a well-known practice of producing large quantities of CTO (cancelled-to-order) material, much of it ungummed or with imperforate variants, sold in bulk to the Western packet trade from the 1960s onward. This is a genuine-but-abundant problem rather than an illegal-issue problem: postally used examples are much scarcer than the CTO flood suggests.
uncertain: The total count is an order-of-magnitude estimate and has not been verified against a physical catalogue volume; the figure also depends heavily on whether South Vietnam, NLF and PRG issues are counted in or out. Exact first-day dates for individual 1945 provisional overprints are disputed in the literature, as several were locally applied. Whether certain 1945-46 local Viet Minh overprints were officially authorised at all is unresolved.
